title: Proof of entropic order in Generalized Ising Models
comment: Main document: 5 pages, 3 figures Suppemental Material: 7 pages, 2 figures
summary: Ordering at arbitrarily high temperature - entropic order - has been argued to take place in a class of generalized Ising models parameterised by a real interaction parameter $p$ when $p\ge 1$. We give a rigorous proof of this conjecture. We further show that on arbitrary graphs, these models solve graph packing problems - crucially, the Maximum Independent Set optimisation problem. Due to the NP-hardness of this packing problem on generic graphs, some lattice systems will exhibit glassy phases. We call this phenomenon $entropic$ $glass$.

AI.tldr: Rigorous proof of entropic order in generalized Ising models for p≥1, showing these models solve Maximum Independent Set problems on arbitrary graphs, leading to glassy phases called "entropic glass" due to NP-hardness.
AI.motivation: To provide rigorous mathematical proof of the conjecture that entropic order (ordering at arbitrarily high temperature) occurs in generalized Ising models with interaction parameter p≥1, and to demonstrate their connection to graph packing problems.
AI.method: Analysis of generalized Ising models parameterized by real interaction parameter p, rigorous mathematical proof techniques, and examination of these models on arbitrary graphs to show they solve Maximum Independent Set optimization problems.
AI.result: Successfully proved the conjecture of entropic order for p≥1, demonstrated that these models solve Maximum Independent Set problems on arbitrary graphs, and identified that due to NP-hardness of this optimization problem, some lattice systems exhibit glassy phases termed "entropic glass."
AI.conclusion: Generalized Ising models with p≥1 exhibit entropic order and provide solutions to Maximum Independent Set problems, leading to the discovery of entropic glass phases in lattice systems due to the computational complexity of the underlying optimization problem.

title: Universality and ambiguity in extremes of anomalous diffusion
summary: Many biophysical processes begin when the fastest searcher finds a target out of many random searchers, which is called an extreme or fastest first passage time (fFPT). In some models, (i) the fFPT vanishes logarithmically as the number of searchers grows, and (ii) the fFPT can be faster for subdiffusive search compared to normal diffusion. Though mathematically rigorous, the relevance of (i) and (ii) to actual physical systems is suspect since their derivations involve searchers which move with unbounded speed. Indeed, we previously proved that the fFPT for searchers with bounded speed converges exponentially to a strictly positive minimal search time as the number of searchers grows. In this paper, we study fFPTs for a broad class of anomalous and normal diffusion models with bounded or unbounded speed. These models include scaled Brownian motion, Riemann-Liouville fractional Brownian motion, and fractional Brownian motion. For all of these models, we show that the fFPT decays logarithmically in the number of searchers and that subdiffusion can be faster than normal diffusion (we further show that superdiffusion can be slower than normal diffusion). In this sense, features (i) and (ii) are rather universal. On the other hand, we show that the parameter regimes in which (i) and (ii) are valid depend on the particulars of the individual model, and thus ambiguities remain in the relevance of these features to specific physical systems.

AI.tldr: The paper examines extreme first passage times (fFPT) for multiple searchers, showing that logarithmic decay with searcher number and subdiffusion outperforming normal diffusion are universal features across various diffusion models, though their relevance to specific physical systems depends on model parameters.
AI.motivation: To resolve ambiguity about the physical relevance of two key features of fastest first passage times: (i) logarithmic decay with increasing searcher number, and (ii) subdiffusion being faster than normal diffusion. Previous work suggested these features might be mathematical artifacts arising from models with unbounded speed.
AI.method: Study fFPTs across a broad class of anomalous and normal diffusion models with bounded or unbounded speed, including scaled Brownian motion, Riemann-Liouville fractional Brownian motion, and fractional Brownian motion. Analyze parameter regimes where features (i) and (ii) hold.
AI.result: Features (i) and (ii) are universal across all studied models: fFPT decays logarithmically with number of searchers, and subdiffusion can be faster than normal diffusion (while superdiffusion can be slower). However, the parameter regimes where these features are valid depend on specific model details.
AI.conclusion: The logarithmic decay of fFPT with searcher number and the advantage of subdiffusion over normal diffusion are universal mathematical features, but their physical relevance for specific systems remains ambiguous and depends on model parameters and implementation details.

title: Spectral Softening and the Structural Breakdown of Thermodynamic Equilibrium
summary: Under sufficiently slow driving, thermodynamics predicts reversible evolution through a sequence of equilibrium states. We show that this expectation fails near spectral degeneracy in driven quadratic Hamiltonian systems. As the soft-mode frequency collapses, the intrinsic dynamical timescale diverges and quadratic confinement is lost, leading to a breakdown of timescale separation and the failure of adiabatic following even under arbitrarily slow driving. More precisely, adiabaticity is lost once the soft-mode frequency falls below a finite, drive-dependent threshold, implying that the breakdown extends over a finite regime rather than being confined to a singular limit. Crucially, this dynamical instability is accompanied by a divergence of the canonical partition function, rendering equilibrium ensembles ill-defined and eliminating the foundation of quasistatic thermodynamic processes. This breakdown does not arise from unbounded Hamiltonians or critical slowing down, but emerges structurally from spectral softening within a bounded quadratic system. Analysis of the Wigner phase-space representation, together with its classical counterpart, reveals the same singular structure, demonstrating that this limitation is not uniquely quantum but originates from the underlying Hamiltonian phase-space geometry. These results show that thermodynamic reversibility is fundamentally constrained, as a direct consequence of the breakdown of equilibrium, whenever spectral softening removes an intrinsic frequency scale.

AI.tldr: Thermodynamic reversibility breaks down near spectral degeneracy in driven quadratic Hamiltonian systems due to loss of timescale separation and equilibrium ensemble ill-definedness, not from unbounded Hamiltonians but structural spectral softening.
AI.motivation: To investigate why the standard thermodynamic expectation of reversible evolution through equilibrium states under slow driving fails in certain physical systems, particularly near spectral degeneracy in quadratic Hamiltonian systems.
AI.method: Analysis of driven quadratic Hamiltonian systems, examining spectral degeneracy effects, timescale separation, and equilibrium ensemble properties using Wigner phase-space representation and its classical counterpart to reveal singular structure.
AI.result: Adiabaticity fails when soft-mode frequency falls below a finite drive-dependent threshold, with breakdown extending over a finite regime. Canonical partition function diverges, making equilibrium ensembles ill-defined. This limitation originates from Hamiltonian phase-space geometry and affects both quantum and classical systems.
AI.conclusion: Thermodynamic reversibility is fundamentally constrained by the breakdown of equilibrium when spectral softening removes intrinsic frequency scales, revealing a structural limitation not confined to singular limits but extending over finite regimes.

title: Algorithmic overlaps as thermodynamic variables: from local to cluster Monte Carlo dynamics in critical phenomena
summary: We investigate the spatial overlap of successive spin configurations in Markov chain Monte Carlo simulations using the local Metropolis algorithm and the Swendsen-Wang and Wolff cluster algorithms. We examine the dynamics of these algorithms for two models in different universality classes: the Ising model and the Potts model with three components. The overlap of two successive Wolff clusters reflects critical behavior and can be used as an order parameter for the algorithm's dynamics. In the case of the Swendsen-Wang algorithm, similar behavior is demonstrated by the variation in the overlap of two consecutive lattice configurations, which behaves like order parameter. Nothing similar is observed for the Metropolis algorithm, and the dynamics in the critical region are determined by the spin flip frequency, which is equivalent to the acceptance rate. Thus, the critical behavior of Wolff cluster overlap and the variation of configuration overlap in the Swendsen-Wang algorithm are naturally related to the critical behavior of geometric objects - Fortuin-Kastelein clusters. Interestingly, in all cases, the geometric quantity - configuration overlap or its variation - reflects the thermodynamics of the phase transition.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates spatial overlap of successive spin configurations in MCMC simulations using Metropolis, Swendsen-Wang, and Wolff algorithms for Ising and 3-state Potts models, showing cluster algorithm overlaps reflect critical behavior while Metropolis dynamics depend on acceptance rates.
AI.motivation: To understand how spatial overlap patterns in successive spin configurations differ between local (Metropolis) and cluster (Swendsen-Wang, Wolff) Monte Carlo algorithms, and to determine whether these overlaps can serve as order parameters for algorithmic dynamics near critical points.
AI.method: Comparative analysis of Markov chain Monte Carlo simulations using three algorithms: local Metropolis, Swendsen-Wang cluster algorithm, and Wolff cluster algorithm. The study examines two models in different universality classes: Ising model and 3-state Potts model. Key metrics include overlap of successive Wolff clusters, variation in overlap of consecutive lattice configurations for Swendsen-Wang, and spin flip frequency/acceptance rate for Metropolis.
AI.result: For cluster algorithms, the overlap of successive Wolff clusters exhibits critical behavior and can serve as an order parameter for algorithmic dynamics. Similarly, the variation in configuration overlap for Swendsen-Wang algorithm behaves like an order parameter. For Metropolis algorithm, no such behavior is observed - dynamics in critical region are determined by spin flip frequency (acceptance rate). The critical behavior of Wolff cluster overlap and Swendsen-Wang configuration overlap variation are naturally related to critical behavior of Fortuin-Kasteleyn clusters.
AI.conclusion: Geometric quantities (configuration overlap or its variation) in cluster algorithms reflect the thermodynamics of phase transitions, while local algorithms like Metropolis show different dynamics governed by acceptance rates. The overlap patterns in cluster algorithms provide insight into critical behavior and can serve as order parameters for algorithmic dynamics near phase transitions.

title: Dual Quantum Geometric Tensors and Local Topological Invariant
summary: The conventional quantum geometric tensor (QGT) is Hermitian, with a real symmetric quantum metric and an imaginary antisymmetric Berry curvature. We show that the Zeeman QGT is generically non-Hermitian and admits a natural decomposition into normal and anomalous metric-curvature sectors. The normal sector reduces to the conventional Hermitian structure, whereas the anomalous sector contains an imaginary symmetric metric-like tensor and a real antisymmetric curvature-like tensor with no counterpart in the standard QGT. In a two-dimensional Dirac system, the anomalous Zeeman curvature develops a radial flux singularity that is Hodge-dual to the tangential winding field of the Dirac node. This recasts the same local $π_1$ topology into a curvature-flux language, analogous to the flux representation of global $π_2$ topology by the conventional Berry curvature. At the level of linear response, the four symmetry-resolved components of the gyrotropic conductivity are in one-to-one correspondence with the four components of the Zeeman QGT, while their distinct low-frequency scalings provide an additional diagnostic for isolating the underlying geometric sector. The reciprocal kinetic magnetoelectric response offers a complementary experimental route to probe the same structure. These results establish a unified framework connecting non-Hermitian Zeeman quantum geometry, local Dirac-node topology, and measurable transport signatures.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a non-Hermitian Zeeman quantum geometric tensor with normal and anomalous sectors, connects it to Dirac node topology, and shows transport signatures in gyrotropic conductivity.
AI.motivation: To extend the conventional Hermitian quantum geometric tensor framework to include Zeeman effects, revealing new geometric structures and connecting them to measurable transport phenomena.
AI.method: Develops a non-Hermitian Zeeman quantum geometric tensor decomposition into normal (Hermitian) and anomalous (non-Hermitian) sectors, analyzes its properties in 2D Dirac systems, and connects components to gyrotropic conductivity via linear response theory.
AI.result: The Zeeman QGT has four symmetry-resolved components corresponding to four gyrotropic conductivity components, with anomalous sector showing imaginary symmetric metric and real antisymmetric curvature. In Dirac systems, anomalous curvature develops radial flux singularity dual to Dirac node winding.
AI.conclusion: Establishes unified framework connecting non-Hermitian Zeeman quantum geometry, local Dirac-node topology (π₁), and measurable transport signatures through gyrotropic conductivity and reciprocal kinetic magnetoelectric response.

title: Beyond Whittle: exact finite-time multispectral statistics from a single Brownian trajectory in a harmonic trap
summary: Power spectral densities are often interpreted through ensemble averages and long-time asymptotics. In many experiments, however, only a single finite record is available, so spectral estimators remain broadly distributed and the usual independence assumptions across frequencies need not hold. Here we develop an exact finite-$T$ multispectral theory for an overdamped Brownian particle in a harmonic trap. For a collection of frequencies $\{ω_i\}$, we obtain an exact characterization of the joint law of the finite-time estimators $\{S(ω_i,T)\}$, together with a covariance-explicit Gaussian representation for the associated Fourier projections. This representation makes the observation-window-induced inter-frequency correlations explicit and shows how they vanish as $T\to\infty$, thereby recovering the asymptotic Whittle picture. We then use this structure to formulate a hierarchy of spectral likelihoods for inference from a single trajectory, ranging from the factorized Whittle approximation to blockwise covariance-aware approximations in frequency space. Monte Carlo simulations validate the finite-time theory and quantify the effect of neglected cross-frequency correlations on single-trajectory estimates of the trap parameters. Our results provide a controlled finite-time benchmark for spectral inference beyond the asymptotic regime.

AI.tldr: Exact finite-time spectral theory for overdamped Brownian particle in harmonic trap, revealing inter-frequency correlations in spectral estimators and enabling improved inference from single trajectories.
AI.motivation: Spectral analysis typically relies on ensemble averages and asymptotic theory, but experimental data often consists of single finite-time records where spectral estimators remain broadly distributed and frequency independence assumptions break down.
AI.method: Develop exact finite-T multispectral theory for overdamped Brownian particle in harmonic trap, obtaining exact characterization of joint law of finite-time estimators S(ω_i,T) and covariance-explicit Gaussian representation for Fourier projections.
AI.result: Theory reveals observation-window-induced inter-frequency correlations that vanish as T→∞ (recovering Whittle picture). Hierarchy of spectral likelihoods formulated for inference, validated by Monte Carlo simulations quantifying effect of neglected cross-frequency correlations on parameter estimates.
AI.conclusion: Provides controlled finite-time benchmark for spectral inference beyond asymptotic regime, enabling improved parameter estimation from single trajectories by accounting for finite-time correlations.

title: Classical and Quantum Dynamics in an Information Theoretic Space
summary: We study elementary classical and quantum dynamics in an information geometric space corresponding to a Bernoulli random variable, extending work by Goehle and Griffin [Chaos, Solitons & Fractals, 188, 115535, (2024)], who study the information theoretic analog of the spring-mass system. Information geometric constructions are useful in both statistical physics and in physical interpretations of Friston's free energy principle, a form of the Bayesian brain hypothesis. In this letter, we derive the spectrum for the Laplace-Beltrami operator in Bernoulli space and find Green's functions for the Helmholtz equation, which provides solutions to the wave, heat, and Poisson equations. We then show how to quantize momentum in Bernoulli space and obtain energies and wavefunctions for both a free particle and a variety of quantum (harmonic) oscillators in this space. In particular, we show that quadratic approximation of the Kullback-Leibler potential used by Goehle and Griffin results in a quantum oscillator in information space that is equivalent to a quantum pendulum in Euclidean space.

AI.tldr: The paper extends information geometry to quantum dynamics in Bernoulli space, deriving Laplace-Beltrami spectrum, Green's functions, and quantizing momentum to obtain quantum oscillator solutions equivalent to quantum pendulums.
AI.motivation: To extend information geometric constructions from classical to quantum dynamics in Bernoulli space, building on previous work on information-theoretic analogs of spring-mass systems, with applications to statistical physics and Friston's free energy principle.
AI.method: Derived spectrum for Laplace-Beltrami operator in Bernoulli space, found Green's functions for Helmholtz equation, quantized momentum in Bernoulli space, and obtained energies and wavefunctions for free particles and quantum harmonic oscillators.
AI.result: Showed that quadratic approximation of Kullback-Leibler potential yields quantum oscillator in information space equivalent to quantum pendulum in Euclidean space, providing complete quantum mechanical treatment of dynamics in Bernoulli information space.
AI.conclusion: Successfully extended information geometry to quantum regime, establishing connections between information-theoretic oscillators and physical quantum systems, with implications for statistical physics and Bayesian brain hypothesis applications.

title: Heat Conduction in Momentum-Conserving Fluids: From quasi-2D to 3D systems
summary: Using nonequilibrium and equilibrium molecular dynamics simulations, we investigate heat conduction in a momentum-conserving mesoscopic fluid modeled by multiparticle collision dynamics. Across quasi-two-dimensional (q-2D) to three-dimensional (3D) systems, we identify three distinct transport regimes: (i) a \emph{ballistic regime}, where thermal conductivity scales linearly with system size ($κ\sim L$) and the total heat current autocorrelation function $C(t)$ remains constant; (ii)~a \emph{kinetic regime}, characterized by size-independent $κ$ and exponentially decaying $C(t)$, demonstrating that normal heat conduction dominated by kinetic effects is far more ubiquitous than previously observed in 1D systems; and (iii)~a \emph{hydrodynamic regime}, where the q-2D system exhibits logarithmically divergent conductivity ($ κ\sim \ln L $ ) with $ C(t) \sim t^{-1} $ , while the 3D system displays finite $ κ$ and $ C(t) \sim t^{-3/2} $. Our results, observed in the hydrodynamic regime, quantitatively validate the scaling predictions for heat transport and reveal a clear dimensional crossover -- from 2D-like anomalous transport to 3D Fourier behavior. These results lay a foundation for understanding thermal transport in q-2D to 3D systems and have practical implications for the design of micro- and nanoscale thermal devices.

AI.tldr: Molecular dynamics simulations reveal three heat transport regimes in momentum-conserving fluids: ballistic (κ∼L), kinetic (size-independent κ), and hydrodynamic (2D: κ∼ln L, 3D: finite κ), showing dimensional crossover from anomalous to Fourier behavior.
AI.motivation: To understand heat conduction mechanisms across dimensional scales (quasi-2D to 3D) in momentum-conserving mesoscopic fluids, which is crucial for designing micro- and nanoscale thermal devices.
AI.method: Nonequilibrium and equilibrium molecular dynamics simulations using multiparticle collision dynamics to model momentum-conserving mesoscopic fluids across quasi-2D to 3D systems.
AI.result: Identified three distinct transport regimes: (1) ballistic regime with κ∼L and constant heat current autocorrelation; (2) kinetic regime with size-independent κ and exponentially decaying autocorrelation; (3) hydrodynamic regime where q-2D shows κ∼ln L with C(t)∼t⁻¹, while 3D shows finite κ with C(t)∼t⁻³/².
AI.conclusion: The study reveals a dimensional crossover from 2D-like anomalous transport to 3D Fourier behavior, providing fundamental understanding of thermal transport in q-2D to 3D systems with practical implications for micro/nanoscale thermal device design.

title: Generative Circuit Design for Quantum-Selected Configuration Interaction
summary: Quantum-selected configuration interaction (QSCI) has emerged as a feasible approach for approximating electronic ground states on noisy quantum devices toward large-system demonstrations. In QSCI, Slater determinants are sampled from a quantum-prepared state, and the Hamiltonian is then diagonalized in the sampled subspace. To create a high-quality subspace under hardware constraints, the design of the state-preparation circuit is crucial. Here, we present a Generative Quantum Eigensolver (GQE)-based framework that optimizes ansatz structures using a Transformer policy trained on the QSCI subspace energy. We validate the framework on N2 in active spaces of up to 32 qubits. We found that the optimized circuits reach chemical precision with substantially lower gate counts than time-evolved circuits. Quantitatively, this corresponds to an average reduction of 98% in the required two-qubit gate count relative to the single-step first-order Trotterized approximation and 83% relative to the qDRIFT approximation. Furthermore, the resulting wavefunctions are competitive with heat-bath configuration interaction (HCI) in terms of compactness. In stretched-bond, strongly correlated regimes, they achieve chemical precision with subspaces that are 50% smaller than those required by HCI.

AI.tldr: GQE framework uses Transformer policy to optimize quantum circuit structures for QSCI, achieving chemical precision with dramatically reduced gate counts and more compact wavefunctions than classical HCI in strongly correlated regimes.
AI.motivation: QSCI requires high-quality state preparation circuits under hardware constraints; existing approaches have high gate counts, limiting scalability on noisy quantum devices.
AI.method: Generative Quantum Eigensolver (GQE) framework with Transformer policy trained on QSCI subspace energy to optimize ansatz structures; validated on N2 molecule in active spaces up to 32 qubits.
AI.result: Optimized circuits achieve chemical precision with 98% fewer two-qubit gates than Trotterized approximation and 83% fewer than qDRIFT; wavefunctions competitive with HCI, requiring 50% smaller subspaces in strongly correlated regimes.
AI.conclusion: GQE enables efficient quantum circuit design for QSCI, significantly reducing resource requirements while maintaining accuracy, particularly beneficial for strongly correlated systems.

title: Location of the liquid-vapor critical point in aluminum
summary: The precise location of the liquid-vapor critical point (CP) in aluminum has remained elusive for decades, with reported critical temperatures spanning nearly 4000~K. Here we resolve this long-standing uncertainty by combining deep potential molecular dynamics with large-scale simulations trained on high-fidelity electronic-structure data. We benchmark multiple exchange-correlation functionals against experimental liquid densities and identify PBEsol as providing the most consistent description. Using complementary approaches -- spinodal analysis of the equation of state and direct coexistence simulations with Gaussian mixture phase identification -- we converge on a critical temperature of $6531$-$6576$~K, a critical density of $0.637$~g/cm$^{3}$, and a critical pressure of $1.6$~kbar. The precision of these values, with uncertainties of $\sim$50~K in temperature, represents a step change over previous estimates. Our framework establishes a transferable strategy for predicting critical phenomena in metals, with implications for laser ablation, shock compression, and planetary modeling under extreme conditions.

AI.tldr: Researchers resolve decades-long uncertainty about aluminum's liquid-vapor critical point using deep potential molecular dynamics trained on high-fidelity electronic-structure data, identifying PBEsol as optimal functional and converging on precise critical parameters.
AI.motivation: The precise location of aluminum's liquid-vapor critical point has remained elusive for decades, with reported critical temperatures spanning nearly 4000 K, creating significant uncertainty in understanding aluminum's phase behavior under extreme conditions.
AI.method: Combined deep potential molecular dynamics with large-scale simulations trained on high-fidelity electronic-structure data; benchmarked multiple exchange-correlation functionals against experimental liquid densities; used complementary approaches including spinodal analysis of the equation of state and direct coexistence simulations with Gaussian mixture phase identification.
AI.result: Identified PBEsol as providing the most consistent description; converged on critical temperature of 6531-6576 K, critical density of 0.637 g/cm³, and critical pressure of 1.6 kbar with uncertainties of ~50 K in temperature, representing a step change in precision over previous estimates.
AI.conclusion: The framework establishes a transferable strategy for predicting critical phenomena in metals, with implications for laser ablation, shock compression, and planetary modeling under extreme conditions, resolving long-standing uncertainty about aluminum's critical point.

title: Sector length distributions of recursively definable graph states through analytic combinatorics
summary: The sector length distribution or Shor-Laflamme distribution (SLD) of quantum states is governed by the $k$-body correlations amongst the different systems, and has been used to study entanglement and error correction. A succinct description of a quantum state's SLD can be obtained by representing it through the coefficients of an appropriate weight enumerator polynomial, yielding bounds on fidelity under depolarizing noise and on multipartite entanglement. However, such expressions quickly grow out of hand and are generally difficult to achieve analytically, reflecting the computational hardness of the SLD. We sidestep this problem and, instead of a single state's SLDs, encode a family of quantum state's SLD as a generating function. We then find closed-form expressions for a large class of graph states which we call `recursively definable' and which include many common graphs such as path graphs, cycle graphs, star graphs, grid graphs, and more. As direct corollary, we obtain analytical expressions for such graph states' concentratable entanglement, bounds on their depolarizing fidelity, and a multipartite entanglement criterion. Our work opens up the use of generating functions and more generally analytic combinatorics to solve problems in quantum information theory.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a generating function approach to encode Shor-Laflamme distributions for families of graph states, enabling closed-form expressions for recursively definable graphs and deriving analytical results for entanglement and error correction bounds.
AI.motivation: The Shor-Laflamme distribution (SLD) captures k-body correlations and is useful for studying entanglement and error correction, but analytical expressions for individual states are computationally hard and quickly become intractable.
AI.method: Instead of computing SLDs for single states, the authors encode families of quantum states' SLDs as generating functions. They apply this approach to a large class of "recursively definable" graph states, which include common graph types like paths, cycles, stars, and grids.
AI.result: Obtained closed-form expressions for SLDs of recursively definable graph states, yielding analytical expressions for concentratable entanglement, bounds on depolarizing fidelity, and a multipartite entanglement criterion.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that generating functions and analytic combinatorics can be effectively applied to solve problems in quantum information theory, particularly for analyzing entanglement and error correction properties of graph states.

title: Confined kinetics and heterogeneous diffusion driven by fractional Gaussian noise: A path integral approach
summary: Many complex systems are described by Langevin-type equations in which the noise exhibits long-range correlations and couples to the system in a state-dependent, multiplicative manner, leading to heterogeneous non-Markovian diffusion. Here, we investigate the problem of diffusion driven by fractional Gaussian noise with a general multiplicative coefficient from a path-integral perspective. Using a stationary-phase approximation, we derive a Gaussian propagator expressed in terms of the Lamperti transform of the process. In the additive limit, our results recover the path-integral representation of fractional Brownian motion based on its Riemann-Liouville formulation and establish its equivalence with the Langevin construction. We further analyze the effect of subordinating the process to a killing rate within the Feynman-Kac framework, and develop a general procedure to derive kinetic equations in terms of effective local Hamiltonians. We show that the interplay between multiplicative diffusion and confinement induces an effective drift term, leading to probability accumulation in regions of low noise amplitude.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a path-integral approach for diffusion driven by fractional Gaussian noise with multiplicative coupling, deriving Gaussian propagators and analyzing confinement effects through Feynman-Kac formalism.
AI.motivation: Many complex systems exhibit heterogeneous non-Markovian diffusion characterized by long-range correlated noise with state-dependent multiplicative coupling, requiring theoretical frameworks beyond standard Langevin descriptions.
AI.method: Path-integral approach using stationary-phase approximation to derive Gaussian propagators expressed via Lamperti transform; Feynman-Kac framework for analyzing subordination with killing rates; derivation of kinetic equations through effective local Hamiltonians.
AI.result: Recovery of fractional Brownian motion path-integral representation in additive limit; demonstration of equivalence between Langevin and Riemann-Liouville formulations; identification of effective drift term induced by interplay between multiplicative diffusion and confinement.
AI.conclusion: The path-integral framework successfully captures multiplicative fractional diffusion, revealing that confinement in multiplicative noise systems leads to probability accumulation in low-noise regions through effective drift mechanisms.

title: Leggett-Garg Inequality Violations Bound Quantum Fisher Information
summary: We prove that a violation of a Leggett-Garg inequality for bounded observables in stationary pure states and thermal states yields a rigorous lower bound on the quantum Fisher information. This turns a qualitative foundations test of realism in quantum systems into a quantitative witness of useful quantum sensitivity and, in the collective setting, into a lower bound on multipartite entanglement depth in many-body systems. We further demonstrate that Leggett-Garg violations are constrained by the same spectral moments, susceptibilities, and $f$-sum-rule bounds that organize many-body response. Our results show that temporal correlations of a single collective observable can serve as an experimentally accessible witness of many-body quantum coherence, without requiring full state reconstruction.

AI.tldr: Leggett-Garg inequality violations in stationary states provide quantitative lower bounds on quantum Fisher information, serving as witnesses for quantum sensitivity and multipartite entanglement depth in many-body systems.
AI.motivation: To bridge the gap between foundational tests of quantum realism (Leggett-Garg inequalities) and practical quantum metrology, showing that temporal correlation measurements can provide quantitative information about quantum resources without full state reconstruction.
AI.method: Theoretical proof establishing that violations of Leggett-Garg inequalities for bounded observables in stationary pure states and thermal states yield rigorous lower bounds on quantum Fisher information, connecting temporal correlations to metrological sensitivity.
AI.result: Demonstrated that Leggett-Garg violations are constrained by spectral moments, susceptibilities, and f-sum-rule bounds that organize many-body response, showing temporal correlations of single collective observables can witness many-body quantum coherence.
AI.conclusion: Leggett-Garg inequality violations transform from qualitative tests of quantum foundations into quantitative witnesses of quantum sensitivity and entanglement depth, providing experimentally accessible methods to probe many-body quantum coherence without state tomography.

title: Forecasting Return Time of Extreme Precipitation by Large Deviation Theory
summary: Forecasting extreme precipitation is essential yet challenging due to its rarity and complexity. We develop a large deviation framework to estimate the return times of extreme precipitation events. We first find that the Landau distribution, originally introduced in plasma physics, accurately captures extreme precipitation at approximately 93% of global locations, outperforming conventional extreme value distributions with 76% matched locations under the same accuracy criterion. Enriching rare event samples by the fitted Landau distribution, we obtain more accurate estimates of large deviation rate functions and return times, enabling forecasts beyond historically observed precipitation intensities. Mapping historical return times to future projections from the Coupled Model Intercomparison Project Phase 6 (CMIP6), we show that return time curves under different emission scenarios collapse onto a unified relation, revealing a sharply increased lifetime exposure to extreme precipitation for 21st-century birth cohorts under most future emission scenarios.

AI.tldr: A large deviation framework using Landau distribution improves extreme precipitation forecasting, revealing sharply increased lifetime exposure for 21st-century birth cohorts across CMIP6 scenarios.
AI.motivation: Forecasting extreme precipitation is challenging due to its rarity and complexity, requiring better statistical methods to estimate return times and assess future climate risks.
AI.method: Developed a large deviation framework using Landau distribution to model extreme precipitation, enriched rare event samples, mapped historical return times to CMIP6 future projections, and analyzed return time curves across emission scenarios.
AI.result: Landau distribution outperforms conventional extreme value distributions (93% vs 76% global location match), enables more accurate rate function and return time estimates, and reveals unified return time relations across emission scenarios with sharply increased lifetime exposure to extreme precipitation for 21st-century birth cohorts.
AI.conclusion: The Landau distribution provides superior extreme precipitation modeling, and future climate scenarios indicate substantially increased lifetime exposure to extreme precipitation events for current and future generations.

title: Rigorous quantum state tomography for distributed quantum computing
summary: Distributed quantum computing offers a promising approach to scaling quantum devices by networking multiple quantum processors. We present a quantum state tomography protocol tailored for distributed quantum computers that avoids assuming remote entanglement as a primitive resource. The protocol extends projected least-squares (PLS) tomography based on projective 2-designs to systems composed of multiple quantum processors, using only local operations within each processor and classical communication between nodes. Assuming entanglement within each individual quantum processor is trusted, the protocol can be executed using mutually unbiased bases. We derive rigorous, non-asymptotic trace-norm error bounds for the PLS estimator, with explicit exponential dependence on the number of nodes. In addition, we establish certified error bounds for estimating entanglement negativity from the PLS estimator. Numerical simulations for systems of up to seven qubits distributed across several devices validate the theoretical error bounds.

AI.tldr: Distributed quantum state tomography protocol using local operations and classical communication without assuming remote entanglement, with rigorous error bounds and entanglement negativity certification.
AI.motivation: Distributed quantum computing requires scalable tomography methods that don't rely on remote entanglement as a primitive resource, enabling practical characterization of quantum states across networked quantum processors.
AI.method: Extends projected least-squares (PLS) tomography based on projective 2-designs to distributed systems, using only local operations within each processor and classical communication between nodes, with mutually unbiased bases when intra-processor entanglement is trusted.
AI.result: Derived rigorous non-asymptotic trace-norm error bounds with explicit exponential dependence on node count, established certified error bounds for estimating entanglement negativity, and validated with numerical simulations up to seven qubits across multiple devices.
AI.conclusion: The protocol enables practical quantum state tomography for distributed quantum computers without requiring remote entanglement, providing certified error bounds and entanglement characterization capabilities.

title: Dynamical Regimes of Discrete Diffusion Models
summary: Diffusion models generate high-dimensional data such as images by learning a process that gradually removes noise from corrupted data. Recent studies have shown that the backward dynamics of diffusion models exhibit two characteristic transitions: the speciation transition, at which generated samples begin to capture the global structure of the training data, and the collapse transition, at which the generation dynamics starts committing to individual training samples. While these transitions have been theoretically analyzed for continuous data, the same theoretical criteria have not been applied for discrete diffusion models, which are diffusion models for discrete data. In this work, we propose a simple effective model for discrete diffusion models trained on two-class Ising variable data with a general mixture ratio and analyze its backward dynamics using methods from statistical mechanics. We show that, as in the previous study on continuous data, the speciation transition can be determined through a second-order phase transition analysis using high-temperature expansion, while the collapse transition corresponds to a condensation transition described by the Random Energy Model. An analytical expression for the speciation time is obtained, and we show that its scaling becomes consistent with that of the continuous case when the noise increases with time as in practical diffusion models. These theoretical predictions are confirmed by numerical simulations and experiments with trained discrete diffusion models on real datasets. These results suggest that the original theoretical framework for continuous data remain valid for discrete data, and may provide a useful starting point for the statistical-mechanics analysis of discrete generative diffusion in more realistic settings.

AI.tldr: Discrete diffusion models exhibit speciation and collapse transitions similar to continuous models, with speciation corresponding to a second-order phase transition and collapse to a condensation transition in Random Energy Model.
AI.motivation: While theoretical criteria for speciation and collapse transitions have been established for continuous diffusion models, they haven't been applied to discrete diffusion models. The authors aim to extend this theoretical framework to discrete data.
AI.method: Proposed a simple effective model for discrete diffusion models trained on two-class Ising variable data with general mixture ratio. Analyzed backward dynamics using statistical mechanics methods, including high-temperature expansion for speciation transition and Random Energy Model for collapse transition.
AI.result: Showed that speciation transition corresponds to a second-order phase transition (determined via high-temperature expansion) and collapse transition corresponds to a condensation transition (described by Random Energy Model). Obtained analytical expression for speciation time, showing scaling consistency with continuous case when noise increases with time. Confirmed predictions through numerical simulations and experiments with trained discrete diffusion models on real datasets.
AI.conclusion: The original theoretical framework for continuous diffusion models remains valid for discrete data. This provides a foundation for statistical-mechanics analysis of discrete generative diffusion in more realistic settings.

title: Resist-free shadow deposition using silicon trenches for Josephson junctions in superconducting qubits
comment: 7 pages, 5 figures, supplementary material (9 pages, 9 figures, 2 tables)
summary: Superconducting qubit fabrication innovations continue to be explored to achieve higher performance. Despite improvements to base layer fabrication and processing, resist-based Josephson junction (JJ) schemes have largely remained unchanged. The polymer mask during deposition causes chemical contamination and limits in situ and ex situ surface preparation, junction materials, and scalability. Here, we demonstrate a resist-free approach to junction fabrication based on etched silicon trenches that is CMOS compatible and easily integrated into existing innovations in qubit base layer fabrication and chemical processing. We fabricate Al-AlOx-Al JJs and qubits using this method, measuring median energy relaxation times up to 184 microseconds. We find minimal contamination at the substrate-metal interface and fluctuations of energy relaxation on a 35 hour timescale that are narrow and normally distributed. The method widens the process window for substrate preparation and new materials platforms.

AI.tldr: Resist-free Josephson junction fabrication using etched silicon trenches achieves high-performance superconducting qubits with reduced contamination and improved process flexibility.
AI.motivation: Traditional resist-based Josephson junction fabrication methods cause chemical contamination, limit surface preparation options, restrict junction materials, and hinder scalability, creating a need for cleaner, more flexible fabrication approaches.
AI.method: Developed a resist-free Josephson junction fabrication method using etched silicon trenches that is CMOS-compatible and integrates with existing qubit base layer fabrication and chemical processing techniques.
AI.result: Fabricated Al-AlOx-Al Josephson junctions and qubits with median energy relaxation times up to 184 μs, minimal substrate-metal interface contamination, and narrow, normally distributed energy relaxation fluctuations over 35-hour timescales.
AI.conclusion: The resist-free trench-based fabrication method eliminates polymer mask contamination, expands the process window for substrate preparation, enables new materials platforms, and represents a significant advancement for superconducting qubit fabrication.

title: Scar subspaces stabilized by algebraic closure: Beyond equally-spaced spectra and exact solvability
summary: We construct a class of quantum many-body systems hosting an $\mathfrak{su}(3)$-invariant scar subspace, extending the conventional paradigm of quantum many-body scars beyond equally spaced spectra and single-directional tower structures. Our construction is based on local constraints that realize an algebraic closure within the scar subspace. As a result, the spectrum in the subspace is no longer equally spaced, but instead forms a multidirectional lattice structure parametrized by multiple independent quantum numbers. This leads to qualitatively new dynamical signatures: instead of single-frequency revivals, the system exhibits multifrequency oscillations governed by integer linear combinations of distinct energy scales. Importantly, the stability of the scar subspace does not rely on exact solvability of individual eigenstates. We show that algebraic closure preserves the invariant subspace even under perturbations that render the eigenstates analytically intractable, thereby realizing quantum many-body scars on an unsolvable reference state. Our results identify algebraic closure as a unifying mechanism underlying scar subspaces beyond the conventional $\mathfrak{su}(2)$ paradigm, and open a route toward richer nonthermal dynamics in nonintegrable quantum systems.

AI.tldr: The paper constructs quantum many-body systems with 𝔰𝔲(3)-invariant scar subspaces that exhibit multidirectional lattice spectra and multifrequency oscillations, extending beyond conventional 𝔰𝔲(2) paradigms through algebraic closure mechanisms.
AI.motivation: To extend the paradigm of quantum many-body scars beyond equally spaced spectra and single-directional tower structures, and to develop a more general framework for scar subspaces that can exist on unsolvable reference states.
AI.method: Construct quantum many-body systems based on local constraints that realize algebraic closure within the scar subspace, enabling 𝔰𝔲(3) invariance and multidirectional lattice spectra parametrized by multiple independent quantum numbers.
AI.result: The constructed systems exhibit qualitatively new dynamical signatures with multifrequency oscillations governed by integer linear combinations of distinct energy scales, and the scar subspace remains stable under perturbations even when individual eigenstates become analytically intractable.
AI.conclusion: Algebraic closure serves as a unifying mechanism for scar subspaces beyond conventional 𝔰𝔲(2) paradigms, enabling richer nonthermal dynamics in nonintegrable quantum systems and scar formation on unsolvable reference states.

title: Optimising Quantum Error Correction Using Morphing Circuits
summary:
Quantum error correction (QEC) codes are traditionally defined and searched for without specifying the manner in which its syndrome extraction circuits are executed using elementary gates and measurements. We show how morphing circuits introduced in Refs. [1-3] provide a way of optimising syndrome extraction circuits and codes directly in terms of connectivity, choice of two-qubit gate (ISWAP versus CNOT) and number of physical qubits. We discuss morphing circuits in code optimisation among Abelian two-block group algebra (2BGA) codes, handling boundaries for 2D codes, codes with single-shot properties, and improving performance in stability experiments against measurement and reset errors. We show that alternating syndrome extraction circuits - executed with alternating time-reversed rounds - can be viewed as a two-round morphing circuit whose fault-tolerant properties are computationally much easier to examine than non-alternating syndrome extraction circuits. Our methods find new codes and syndrome extraction circuits of practical interest, including Abelian 2BGA morphing circuits with better code parameters and connectivity than existing circuits.
  [1] Matt McEwen, Dave Bacon, and Craig Gidney. Relaxing hardware requirements for surface code circuits using time-dynamics. Quantum, 7:1172, 2023. [2] Craig Gidney and Cody Jones. New circuits and an open source decoder for the color code, 2023. [3] Mackenzie H. Shaw and Barbara M. Terhal. Lowering connectivity requirements for bivariate bicycle codes using morphing circuits.

AI.tldr: Morphing circuits optimize quantum error correction codes by adapting syndrome extraction circuits to hardware constraints like connectivity, gate choice, and qubit count, enabling better code parameters and fault-tolerant properties.
AI.motivation: Traditional QEC code design doesn't account for practical implementation details like syndrome extraction circuit execution with elementary gates, connectivity constraints, gate choices (ISWAP vs CNOT), and physical qubit count limitations.
AI.method: Use morphing circuits to optimize syndrome extraction circuits and codes directly for hardware constraints. Apply to Abelian two-block group algebra (2BGA) codes, handle boundaries for 2D codes, design codes with single-shot properties, and improve stability against measurement/reset errors. Analyze alternating syndrome extraction circuits as two-round morphing circuits for easier fault-tolerant property examination.
AI.result: Morphing circuits find new codes and syndrome extraction circuits with better parameters and connectivity than existing circuits. Alternating circuits are computationally easier to analyze for fault tolerance. Methods enable practical code optimization for real hardware constraints.
AI.conclusion: Morphing circuits provide a systematic approach to optimize quantum error correction codes for practical hardware implementation, addressing connectivity, gate choice, and qubit count constraints while maintaining or improving fault-tolerant properties.

title: Tensor-Network Population Annealing
summary: We propose a hybrid sampling method, tensor-network population annealing (TNPA), which combines tensor-network (TN) initialization with population annealing (PA). We apply this method to the two-dimensional Edwards-Anderson Ising spin glass. The approach is motivated by the limitations of existing methods: TN-based samplers can become numerically unstable in frustrated spin systems at low temperatures, whereas conventional PA requires a long annealing schedule when started from the high-temperature limit. In TNPA, TN contractions are used only within a reliable temperature range to generate initial configurations that are close to the equilibrium distribution. The subsequent low-temperature equilibration is then carried out by PA. To stabilize the initialization process, we introduce a diagnostic based on the effective sample size that adaptively selects the initialization temperature. The proposed framework provides a practical and physically motivated route to low-temperature sampling by combining the complementary strengths of TN and PA.

AI.tldr: TNPA combines tensor-network initialization with population annealing for efficient low-temperature sampling of frustrated spin glasses, overcoming limitations of both methods individually.
AI.motivation: Existing methods have limitations: TN-based samplers become numerically unstable in frustrated spin systems at low temperatures, while conventional PA requires long annealing schedules when starting from high-temperature limit.
AI.method: Hybrid method using TN contractions only within reliable temperature range to generate initial configurations close to equilibrium, followed by PA for low-temperature equilibration. Includes diagnostic based on effective sample size to adaptively select initialization temperature.
AI.result: The method provides a practical and physically motivated route to low-temperature sampling by combining complementary strengths of TN and PA.
AI.conclusion: TNPA framework successfully addresses limitations of individual methods for sampling frustrated spin systems at low temperatures.

title: Discovery of the Solution to the "Einstein-Podolsky-Rosen Paradox"
summary: In 1935, Albert Einstein, Boris Podolsky and Nathan Rosen (EPR) published a thought experiment that is entirely correct, has been demonstrated in real experiments, and is now the most famous in quantum physics. Their pioneering work described, for the first time, quantum correlations and can be regarded as a very early glimpse into today's 'deep' quantum technologies, by which I mean those that enhance functionality by making use of quantum correlations. However, their work also contains a paradox that Erwin Schroedinger had already recognised as such in 1935 and which has since been cemented by the so-called Bell experiments. Here, I am now able to pinpoint the origin of the paradox within the chain of reasoning, which ultimately resolves the paradox.

AI.tldr: The paper resolves the long-standing EPR paradox by pinpointing its origin in the original reasoning chain, showing that while EPR correctly described quantum correlations, their work contained a paradox that can now be resolved.
AI.motivation: To resolve the paradox in the famous 1935 EPR thought experiment, which correctly described quantum correlations but contained a logical inconsistency that has persisted despite experimental validation of quantum correlations through Bell experiments.
AI.method: The author analyzes the original EPR reasoning chain to identify the precise location where the paradox originates, using logical analysis of the thought experiment's structure rather than conducting new experiments.
AI.result: The author successfully identifies the specific point in the EPR reasoning where the paradox originates, ultimately resolving the long-standing paradox while acknowledging the correctness of EPR's description of quantum correlations.
AI.conclusion: The EPR paradox can be resolved by pinpointing its origin in the original reasoning, reconciling the correctness of quantum correlations with the elimination of the logical inconsistency that has puzzled physicists since 1935.

title: Sluggish quantum mechanics of noninteracting fermions with spatially varying effective mass
summary: We analyze a class of one-dimensional quantum systems characterized by a position-dependent kinetic term arising as the continuum limit of an inhomogeneous tight-binding model with spatially varying hopping amplitudes. In this limit, the Schrodinger equation takes the so-called BenDaniel-Duke form with an effective mass, scaling as $m_{eff}(x) = m_{eff}|x|^α$ with $α> 0$, leading to a framework we term sluggish quantum mechanics, where particle motion is progressively suppressed at larger distances. Both without any external potential and with $V_{ext}(x)=\frac{1}{2}m_{eff}ω^2 |x|^{α+2}$, we obtain the eigenfunctions and the quantum propagators exactly. We then investigate the problem of $N$ noninteracting spinless fermions in the trap, determining the many-body ground-state wavefunction and the joint probability density function of the positions of the $N$ fermions. We show that the many-body quantum probability density in the ground state forms a determinantal point process whose correlation kernel can be computed for any $N$, giving access to the average density as well as higher order correlation functions for any finite $N$. Moreover, we analyze the scaling form of this kernel in the large $N$ limit in the bulk, near the edge, and close to the origin. Our results show that the scaled average density profile for large $N$ has a finite support symmetric with respect to the origin, but has a non-monotonic shape with a vanishing minimum at the origin for any $α>0$. One of the key findings of our work is that the scaled kernel near the origin $x=0$ for $α>0$ is neither the Bessel nor the Airy kernel (that are standard for trapped fermions), but is new, and is given by a sum of two Bessel kernels with different indices. Our results thus provide a framework relevant to engineered optical lattices with position-dependent tunneling.

AI.tldr: The paper studies sluggish quantum mechanics - 1D quantum systems with position-dependent kinetic terms where effective mass scales as |x|^α, analyzing exact solutions for single particles and N noninteracting fermions, revealing novel correlation kernels near the origin.
AI.motivation: To understand quantum systems with position-dependent kinetic terms arising from inhomogeneous tight-binding models, which are relevant for engineered optical lattices with position-dependent tunneling amplitudes.
AI.method: Analyzes continuum limit of inhomogeneous tight-binding models leading to BenDaniel-Duke Schrödinger equation with effective mass m_eff(x) = m_eff|x|^α. Obtains exact eigenfunctions and quantum propagators for free particles and harmonic traps. Studies N noninteracting fermions using determinantal point process theory.
AI.result: Exact solutions for single-particle eigenfunctions and propagators. Many-body ground state forms determinantal point process with computable correlation kernel for any N. Scaled average density has non-monotonic shape with minimum at origin. Near origin, kernel is neither Bessel nor Airy but sum of two Bessel kernels with different indices.
AI.conclusion: Sluggish quantum mechanics provides framework for engineered optical lattices with position-dependent tunneling, revealing novel correlation structures distinct from standard trapped fermion systems, with potential applications in quantum simulation and many-body physics.

title: Planted-solution SAT and Ising benchmarks from integer factorization
summary: We present a family of planted-solution benchmark instances for satisfiability (SAT) solvers and Ising optimization derived from integer factorization. Given two primes $p$ and $q$, the construction encodes the arithmetic constraints of $N = p \times q$ as a conjunctive normal form (CNF) formula whose satisfying assignments correspond to valid factorizations of~$N$. The known pair $(p,q)$ serves as a built-in ground truth, enabling unambiguous verification of solver output. We show that for two $d$-bit primes the total number of carry contractions is on the order of $d^4$. Empirical benchmarks with SAT solvers show that median runtime grows exponentially in the bit-length of the factors over the range tested. The construction provides a scalable, structured, and verifiable benchmark family controlled by a single parameter, accompanied by open-source generation software.

AI.tldr: A family of SAT/Ising benchmark instances derived from integer factorization, where factoring N=p×q produces CNF formulas with built-in ground truth for verification.
AI.motivation: To create structured, scalable, and verifiable benchmark instances for SAT solvers and Ising optimization with unambiguous ground truth verification.
AI.method: Encode arithmetic constraints of integer multiplication N=p×q as CNF formulas, where satisfying assignments correspond to valid factorizations of N.
AI.result: For d-bit primes, carry contractions scale as O(d^4); empirical benchmarks show median SAT solver runtime grows exponentially with factor bit-length.
AI.conclusion: The construction provides a parameter-controlled, scalable benchmark family with open-source generation tools, offering structured verification for SAT/Ising solver evaluation.

title: Absence of thermalization after a local quench and strong violation of the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis
summary: Absence of thermalization after a global quantum quench is a well-established numerical observation in integrable many-body systems, and can be empirically related to a violation of the eigenstate thermalization hypothesis (ETH) in such models. Still, in many of those examples a weaker version of the conventional ETH (wETH) has been numerically reported or even rigorously proven. In this paper we show analytically and illustrate numerically that the absence of thermalization is already possible after a local quench. A closely related finding is a strong violation of the ETH, meaning that not even the wETH is fulfilled anymore. In our analytical explorations we focus on XX-spin-chain models with open boundary conditions, where the local quench is generated by initiating the system in thermal equilibrium and then suddenly switching on (or slightly changing) a single-spin impurity either at the end or in the center of the chain. Numerically we observe qualitatively similar phenomena also for more general XXZ-models in the case of an end-impurity, but not in the case of a central impurity.

AI.tldr: Local quantum quenches in XX spin chains can prevent thermalization even when global quenches would thermalize, showing strong ETH violation beyond weak ETH.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that absence of thermalization can occur after local quantum quenches (not just global ones), and to show strong violation of ETH beyond the weak ETH observed in integrable systems.
AI.method: Analytical study of XX-spin-chain models with open boundaries, examining local quenches generated by suddenly switching on or changing a single-spin impurity at chain ends or center. Numerical verification for more general XXZ-models.
AI.result: Local quenches can prevent thermalization, showing strong ETH violation (not even weak ETH holds). Similar phenomena observed numerically for XXZ-models with end-impurities but not central impurities.
AI.conclusion: Thermalization absence is possible after local quenches, demonstrating stronger ETH violation than previously observed in integrable systems, with impurity location affecting the phenomenon.

title: Polarization Tracking and Active Compensation Using Classical Headers in Quantum Wrapper Networking
summary: Quantum wrapper networking (QWN) is an emerging quantum networking protocol that wraps qubits in classical header bits to enable switching/routing, monitoring, and control without detecting the quantum signal. In this work, we encode header bits with two nonorthogonal polarization references to track and actively compensate for the changing birefringence of a 48 km deployed fiber link. Our method is analytical and deterministic, using motorized waveplates and a variable phase retarder to accurately and stably compensate the channel. We verify successful compensation by measuring the polarization stability of single photon qubits and the visibility of entangled photon pairs under both slow birefringence drift due to environmental fluctuations and large sudden changes designed to emulate those that occur during packet switching and rerouting over different fiber paths. For large, sudden changes, our compensator recovers the Stokes vector of single photons to within 10 degrees of the target state on the Poincaré sphere and restores two-photon interference visibilities to better than 79% on a deployed fiber link. Additionally, experiments monitoring long-term compensation over 44 hours show that visibilities remain above 84.5% with compensation active and degrade to below the quantum threshold of 70.7% within 4 hours of the compensator being turned off. These results add a polarization-control layer to QWN and illustrate that information-carrying headers can enable deterministic physical-layer compensation in the quantum channel over long-distance deployed fiber links.

AI.tldr: Quantum wrapper networking uses classical headers to enable polarization compensation in deployed fiber links, achieving stable qubit transmission and high entanglement visibility through deterministic birefringence correction.
AI.motivation: Quantum wrapper networking (QWN) requires polarization stability for quantum signals in deployed fiber links, but environmental fluctuations and network rerouting cause birefringence changes that degrade quantum information. There's a need for active compensation methods that can track and correct these changes without detecting the quantum signal itself.
AI.method: The method encodes classical header bits using two non-orthogonal polarization references to track birefringence changes in a 48 km deployed fiber link. An analytical, deterministic compensation system uses motorized waveplates and a variable phase retarder to correct polarization changes. The approach leverages the QWN protocol where classical headers enable monitoring and control without quantum signal detection.
AI.result: For large sudden changes (simulating packet switching/rerouting), the compensator recovers single photon Stokes vectors to within 10° of target states and restores two-photon interference visibilities to >79%. Long-term monitoring over 44 hours shows visibilities remain above 84.5% with compensation active, but degrade below the quantum threshold of 70.7% within 4 hours when compensation is turned off.
AI.conclusion: The work successfully demonstrates a polarization-control layer for QWN, showing that classical header information can enable deterministic physical-layer compensation in quantum channels over long-distance deployed fiber links. This approach addresses both slow environmental drift and sudden network-induced changes, maintaining quantum signal quality above operational thresholds.

title: Exact Criterion for Ground-State Overlap Dominance after Quantum Quenches
summary: Recently, conjectured and verified in TIFM model that for a sudden quench within the same physical phase region, the overlap of the initial ground state with the final eigenstates is maximal for the final ground state. We solve this problem exactly for a broad class of translationally invariant free-fermion systems. For Hamiltonians that factorize into independent $2\times2$ sectors, the final ground state is uniquely maximal if and only if the initial and final sector Bloch vectors have positive dot product. This exact criterion proves the conjecture for large classes, but also shows that it is false in general: in Kitaev chains there are same-phase quenches for which the final ground state is not the maximal-overlap state. The same mechanism has a direct dynamical consequence, implying that same-phase quenches can generate DQPTs without crossing an physical phase boundary.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes ground state overlaps after quantum quenches in free-fermion systems, proving a conjecture for some classes but showing it fails for Kitaev chains, with implications for dynamical quantum phase transitions.
AI.motivation: To rigorously test a conjecture that for sudden quenches within the same physical phase region, the overlap of the initial ground state with final eigenstates is maximal for the final ground state, using exact solutions in free-fermion systems.
AI.method: Exact analytical solution for translationally invariant free-fermion systems with Hamiltonians that factorize into independent 2×2 sectors. Analysis of Bloch vector dot products between initial and final states to determine overlap conditions.
AI.result: Proved the conjecture for large classes where initial and final sector Bloch vectors have positive dot product, but found counterexamples in Kitaev chains where same-phase quenches can yield non-maximal ground state overlaps.
AI.conclusion: The conjecture is not universally valid; same-phase quenches in Kitaev chains can violate maximal ground state overlap, and this mechanism enables dynamical quantum phase transitions without crossing physical phase boundaries.

title: A Polylogarithmic-Depth Quantum Multiplier
summary: We present a quantum algorithm for multiplying two $n$-bit integers with overall circuit depth and $T$-depth both bounded by $O(\log^{2} n)$, while using $O(n^{2})$ gates and ancillary qubits. Our construction generates partial products via indicator-controlled copying and adds them using a binary adder tree, enabling parallel accumulation with logarithmic depth overhead per level. To the best of our knowledge, our design has the lowest $T$-depth among all multiplication algorithms using the Clifford + $T$ model. By optimizing both circuit depth and $T$-depth, our construction advances the practical feasibility of large-scale fault-tolerant quantum algorithms.

AI.tldr: Quantum integer multiplication algorithm with O(log² n) circuit/T-depth and O(n²) gates/qubits using parallel accumulation via binary adder tree.
AI.motivation: To develop a quantum multiplication algorithm with optimal T-depth for fault-tolerant quantum computing, addressing the need for efficient arithmetic operations in large-scale quantum algorithms.
AI.method: Uses indicator-controlled copying to generate partial products, then accumulates them in parallel using a binary adder tree structure, achieving logarithmic depth overhead per level.
AI.result: Achieves O(log² n) circuit depth and T-depth with O(n²) gates and ancillary qubits, representing the lowest T-depth among multiplication algorithms in the Clifford+T model.
AI.conclusion: The algorithm advances practical fault-tolerant quantum computing by optimizing both circuit depth and T-depth, making large-scale quantum algorithms more feasible.

title: Optimization of cooling power of a thermoelectric refrigerator: A unified approach
summary: We analyze the steady-state formalism for optimizing the cooling power of a thermoelectric refrigerator (TER), unifying the endoreversible and exoreversible approximations within one framework. Although the cooling power is non-optimizable within the endoreversible model based on Newtonian heat-transfer law, we show that the issue can be circumvented in the near-reversible regime where the external thermal conductances are large, but finite. We extend this analysis to optimize the cooling power in the presence of both internal and external irreversibilities and derive a closed-form expression for the coefficient of performance (COP) that depends on the thermoelectric figure of merit and the ratio of internal to external thermal conductances. The model reproduces the endoreversible and the exoreversible limits as special cases. We conclude that for small temperature differences, the combined irreversibilities reduce the COP to values below 1/2, which aligns with the observed performance of the single-stage TER, and can provide realistic estimates for the COP.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a unified framework for optimizing thermoelectric refrigerator cooling power, bridging endoreversible and exoreversible models, and derives COP expressions that account for both internal and external irreversibilities.
AI.motivation: To unify endoreversible and exoreversible approximations for thermoelectric refrigerators within a single framework, addressing the limitation that cooling power cannot be optimized in the endoreversible model with Newtonian heat-transfer law, and to provide realistic COP estimates that account for combined irreversibilities.
AI.method: Developed a steady-state formalism for optimizing TER cooling power, analyzed the near-reversible regime with large but finite external thermal conductances, extended analysis to include both internal and external irreversibilities, derived closed-form COP expressions dependent on thermoelectric figure of merit and ratio of internal to external thermal conductances.
AI.result: The model reproduces endoreversible and exoreversible limits as special cases, shows that combined irreversibilities reduce COP to values below 1/2 for small temperature differences, aligns with observed performance of single-stage TERs, and provides realistic COP estimates.
AI.conclusion: The unified framework successfully bridges endoreversible and exoreversible approximations, provides practical COP expressions that account for real-world irreversibilities, and offers realistic performance estimates for thermoelectric refrigerators that match experimental observations.

title: Protein-Ligand Free Energy Perturbation on Quantum Hardware
summary: The use of free energy perturbation (FEP) methods to study protein-ligand complexes is one of the most important tools in structure-based drug design. Because FEP methods typically rely on force fields, they may suffer from force field parameter-related issues. Herein, we present a quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) hybrid method to overcome deficiencies in force-field models by using QM bookending approaches on both classical and quantum hardware. In the MM part of this QM/MM FEP method, AMBER is used to simulate the protein receptor and the unperturbed moiety of the ligand, with the ff19SB and GAFF2 force fields. In the QM part, QUICK was used to conduct Hartree-Fock (HF) calculations, followed by heat-bath configuration interaction (HCI) as a benchmark on classical devices. To enable the HCI function in QUICK, we developed a Python-based interface to execute HCI from IBM's qiskit-addon-dice-solver. Moreover, the same interface also enabled this work to execute QM/MM FEP calculations on quantum hardware using the Local Unitary Cluster Jastrow (LUCJ) ansatz, followed by sample-based diagonalization (SQD) and extended-SQD (extSQD) post-processing. Using a series of thermolysis inhibitors as an example, we find reasonable agreement with experiment between the classical HCI method and the LUCJ-SQD/extSQD method, with the latter yielding a result closer to the experimental value. The execution time between the HCI-based FEP method and the LUCJ-SQD/extSQD-based FEP method is also comparable, indicating a high potential for utility in the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era.

AI.tldr: QM/MM hybrid FEP method using quantum bookending on classical and quantum hardware shows comparable performance to classical HCI benchmark for protein-ligand binding free energy calculations.
AI.motivation: Traditional FEP methods rely on force fields that may have parameter-related deficiencies; this work aims to overcome these limitations by integrating quantum mechanics approaches on both classical and quantum hardware.
AI.method: Developed QM/MM hybrid FEP method with AMBER (ff19SB/GAFF2) for MM part and QUICK for QM part. Implemented Python interface for HCI calculations on classical devices and LUCJ ansatz with SQD/extSQD post-processing on quantum hardware.
AI.result: Reasonable agreement with experimental values for thermolysis inhibitors; LUCJ-SQD/extSQD method yielded results closer to experimental values than classical HCI. Execution times between classical HCI-based and quantum LUCJ-SQD/extSQD-based methods were comparable.
AI.conclusion: The QM/MM FEP method with quantum bookending shows high potential for utility in the NISQ era, providing improved accuracy over classical force field methods while maintaining comparable computational efficiency.

title: Inverse engineering of cooling protocols: from normal behavior to Mpemba effects
summary: When a cup of hot coffee is suddenly put into a cold environment, it cools down as a function of time $t$ until the internal temperature $T_\text{int}$ of the coffee equals the external ambient temperature $T_\text{ext}$. This instantaneous shock-freezing corresponds to an imposed cooling protocol of the external temperature $T_\text{ext}(t)$, ideally described as a step-function in time, causing the time-dependent change of the internal temperature $T_\text{int}(t)$. While the effect of different given protocols $T_\text{ext}(t)$ on the resulting system cooling behaviour, embodied in $T_\text{int}(t)$, has been studied extensively, we consider here the inverse question: for a given system cooling $T_\text{int}(t)$ how can an appropriate protocol $T_\text{ext}(t)$ be engineered to produce the desired prescribed $T_\text{int}(t)$. We use both the phenomenological Newtonian equation for cooling and microscopic models, such as a discrete two-level system and a Brownian harmonic oscillator with time-dependent noise, to compute analytically the protocol $T_\text{ext}(t)$ needed to achieve a prescribed $T_\text{int}(t)$. We then discuss the same question for phenomenological generalizations of the Newtonian law which include anomalous Mpemba effects, overcooling, asymmetries in cooling and heating as well as delay phenomena. It is shown that backward-engineered protocols do not always exist and can be non-unique. The results are important for steering the cooling behavior by time-varying external heat sources in a systematic way.

AI.tldr: The paper addresses the inverse problem of engineering external temperature protocols to achieve prescribed internal cooling behaviors, using Newtonian cooling and microscopic models, and explores limitations in protocol existence and uniqueness.
AI.motivation: While forward cooling dynamics (effect of given external temperature protocols on internal temperature) are well-studied, the inverse problem - designing external protocols to achieve desired internal cooling behaviors - remains underexplored and has practical applications for controlling thermal systems.
AI.method: Analytical computation using Newtonian cooling equation and microscopic models (discrete two-level system and Brownian harmonic oscillator with time-dependent noise) to derive external temperature protocols needed for prescribed internal cooling behaviors, extended to phenomenological generalizations including anomalous Mpemba effects, overcooling, asymmetries, and delay phenomena.
AI.result: Derived analytical expressions for backward-engineered protocols, demonstrated that such protocols do not always exist and can be non-unique, revealing fundamental constraints on achievable cooling behaviors through external control.
AI.conclusion: The inverse approach provides systematic methodology for steering cooling behavior via time-varying external heat sources, but inherent limitations in protocol existence and uniqueness impose constraints on what internal temperature trajectories can be engineered.

title: Q-PIPE A Practical Quantum Phase Encoding Method
summary: A major hurdle in Quantum Image Processing (QIMP) is efficiently transferring classical, high-dimensional image data into quantum states. Current methods face trade-offs: amplitude encoding (FRQI) is computationally expensive in gate complexity and limited arithmetic capabilities, while basis encoding (NEQR) incurs heavy initialization overhead scaling with image resolution and intensity bit-depth. Frequency-domain approaches further demand complex transformations for basic pixel-wise arithmetic and extensive post-processing to reconstruct pixel information.
