To address the lack of practical phase encodings, we introduce Q-PIPE (Quantum-Gray Phase Injection for Pixel Encoding). Exploiting the quantum phase kickback mechanism and optimized spatial traversal via a Gray-code sequence, Q-PIPE efficiently maps continuous intensity values into the computational basis with an elementary gate count of $O(qN)$ a $O(\text{log}N)$ improvement over standard basis encoding. Operating directly in the phase domain enables native computation of finite differences without deep arithmetic circuits. Classical readout vulnerabilities, including phase aliasing and spectral leakage, are mitigated by mapping inputs to $[-π, π]$ and introducing a probability threshold equation that scales inversely with the dimension of the spatial register. A proof-of-concept performing Quantum Edge Detection (QED) via directional derivatives demonstrates strong accuracy, yielding exact reconstructions for quantized inputs and low Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for continuous data across multiple benchmark datasets. Ultimately, Q-PIPE establishes a highly parallelizable, NISQ-compatible subroutine that advances quantum computer vision while reducing input/output (I/O) data-loading overhead in broader Quantum Machine Learning (QML) workflows.

AI.tldr: Q-PIPE introduces a novel quantum phase encoding method for images that reduces gate complexity to O(qN) and enables native computation of finite differences, demonstrated through quantum edge detection with strong accuracy.
AI.motivation: Current quantum image encoding methods face significant limitations: amplitude encoding (FRQI) has high computational cost and limited arithmetic capabilities, basis encoding (NEQR) suffers from heavy initialization overhead scaling with image resolution and bit-depth, and frequency-domain approaches require complex transformations for basic operations and extensive post-processing.
AI.method: Q-PIPE exploits quantum phase kickback mechanism and optimized spatial traversal via Gray-code sequence to map continuous intensity values into computational basis. It operates directly in phase domain, enabling native computation of finite differences without deep arithmetic circuits. Classical readout vulnerabilities are mitigated by mapping inputs to [-π, π] and introducing a probability threshold equation that scales inversely with spatial register dimension.
AI.result: Proof-of-concept quantum edge detection via directional derivatives demonstrates strong accuracy, yielding exact reconstructions for quantized inputs and low Mean Absolute Error (MAE) for continuous data across multiple benchmark datasets. The method achieves O(qN) gate complexity, representing O(logN) improvement over standard basis encoding.
AI.conclusion: Q-PIPE establishes a highly parallelizable, NISQ-compatible subroutine that advances quantum computer vision while reducing input/output data-loading overhead in broader Quantum Machine Learning workflows, addressing the lack of practical phase encodings in quantum image processing.

title: The Widom line in the Ising model on a decorated bilayer lattice
summary: There has been much recent interest devoted to a class of frustrated one-dimensional statistical mechanics lattice models which exhibit sharp thermodynamics. In this work, we study an extension of one of these models to two dimensions; the Ising model on a decorated bilayer lattice. We show that the pseudo-transitions of the one-dimensional models become a real first order phase transition in this two-dimensional analogue. Moreover, the pseudo-transition is found to still exist above a bi-critical point. This can be characterised as a Widom line, which allows a re-interpretation of the physics in the previously studied one-dimensional models.

AI.tldr: The paper extends frustrated 1D lattice models with sharp thermodynamics to 2D via an Ising model on a decorated bilayer lattice, finding that pseudo-transitions become real first-order phase transitions, with pseudo-transitions persisting above a bi-critical point as Widom lines.
AI.motivation: To investigate how pseudo-transitions observed in frustrated one-dimensional statistical mechanics lattice models evolve when extended to two dimensions, specifically examining whether these pseudo-transitions become genuine phase transitions in higher dimensions.
AI.method: Study an extension of frustrated 1D models to 2D using the Ising model on a decorated bilayer lattice, analyzing thermodynamic behavior and transition properties.
AI.result: Pseudo-transitions of the 1D models become real first-order phase transitions in the 2D analogue. Pseudo-transitions still exist above a bi-critical point and can be characterized as Widom lines, providing new interpretation of physics in previously studied 1D models.
AI.conclusion: The extension to 2D reveals that pseudo-transitions in frustrated 1D models correspond to genuine phase transitions in higher dimensions, with the persistence of pseudo-transitions above bi-critical points as Widom lines offering a reinterpretation of the physics in the original 1D systems.

title: Detuning-Controlled Phase Transition from Passive to Active Regimes in Non-Markovian Quantum Batteries
summary: We investigate a two-qubit quantum battery where coherent charger-battery coupling competes with non-Markovian environmental interactions. By tuning the coupling strengths and detuning, we identify regimes in which environmental memory enhances energy storage and charging power, while strong dissipation suppresses ergotropy by driving the battery into passive states. We show that detuning plays a dual role: reducing dissipation and inducing a phase shift in the memory kernel that controls the interference between coherent energy exchange and environment-induced backflow. As a result, although the stored energy varies smoothly, the extractable work exhibits a discontinuous onset at a critical detuning, signaling a first-order phase transition in ergotropy. The corresponding phase diagram in the coupling-detuning plane reveals a sharp boundary between thermodynamically inactive and work-producing regimes. Our results demonstrate that phase-controlled coherence and non-Markovianity provide a powerful mechanism for optimizing work extraction in open quantum batteries, offering practical strategies for noise-resilient quantum energy storage.

AI.tldr: Non-Markovian environmental interactions can enhance quantum battery performance when properly tuned with coherent coupling and detuning, leading to a first-order phase transition in extractable work.
AI.motivation: To investigate how environmental memory effects and coherent coupling interact in quantum batteries, and whether non-Markovianity can be harnessed to improve energy storage and work extraction rather than being detrimental.
AI.method: Study a two-qubit quantum battery system with coherent charger-battery coupling competing with non-Markovian environmental interactions. Analyze the system by tuning coupling strengths and detuning parameters to identify different regimes of behavior.
AI.result: Environmental memory enhances energy storage and charging power in certain regimes, while strong dissipation suppresses ergotropy. Detuning reduces dissipation and induces phase shifts in memory kernels. Most strikingly, extractable work exhibits a discontinuous onset at critical detuning, indicating a first-order phase transition in ergotropy.
AI.conclusion: Phase-controlled coherence and non-Markovianity provide a powerful mechanism for optimizing work extraction in open quantum batteries, offering practical strategies for noise-resilient quantum energy storage through careful tuning of coupling and detuning parameters.

title: Thermodynamic fluctuations in freely jointed chains under force
summary: It is common to study polymer physics through the use of idealized single-chain models, and the most popular of these is the freely jointed chain model. In certain thermodynamic ensembles, statistical mechanical treatment of this model is analytically tractable or sometimes exactly solvable. This enables useful relations to be ascertained, like the expected chain end-to-end length as a function of an applied force. However, most of these relations return ensemble averages, which are values with inherent uncertainty, as opposed to deterministic values with no variance. This is an important distinction to understand and quantify, because the majority of studies to date involving single-chain models effectively treat these values as deterministic rather than fluctuating. To address this issue, thermodynamic fluctuations are examined in the freely jointed chain model. Specifically, the probability densities and standard deviations of the longitudinal, lateral, transverse, and radial portions of the chain extension, as well as the extension and link angles, are examined for different numbers of links and applied forces. Fluctuations in these quantities are shown to be considerable until the applied force becomes large. Increasing the number of links in the chain gradually reduces fluctuations in all quantities except for the link angles, since they are independent for freely jointed chains in the isotensional ensemble. Quantities are obtained analytically whenever possible and numerically otherwise. Overall, these results provide intuitive admonitions to consider when modeling the stretching of single polymer chains or the deformation of entire polymer networks.

AI.tldr: Analysis of thermodynamic fluctuations in freely jointed chain model, showing significant fluctuations in chain extension components until large forces are applied, with implications for polymer modeling.
AI.motivation: Most studies treat ensemble averages from single-chain polymer models as deterministic values rather than acknowledging their inherent statistical fluctuations, creating a gap in understanding the true uncertainty in polymer physics predictions.
AI.method: Examine probability densities and standard deviations of various chain extension components (longitudinal, lateral, transverse, radial) and angles in freely jointed chain model under different numbers of links and applied forces, using analytical solutions where possible and numerical methods otherwise.
AI.result: Fluctuations are considerable until large forces are applied; increasing chain links reduces fluctuations in all quantities except link angles (which remain independent); analytical and numerical results quantify these fluctuations.
AI.conclusion: Results provide important warnings about treating ensemble averages as deterministic in polymer modeling, highlighting the need to consider statistical fluctuations when modeling single polymer chains or polymer networks.

title: Enhanced dissipative criticality at an exceptional point
summary: Exceptional points (EPs) represent non-Hermitian degeneracies where eigenvalues and eigenvectors coalesce, giving rise to enhanced sensitivity and critically damped dynamics. We demonstrate that when an EP coincides with a dissipative phase transition in an extended open Dicke model of two cavities coupled to a collective spin, the critical fluctuations are strongly amplified and governed by modified critical exponents. Numerical results reveal enhanced critical scaling in both the normal and superradiant phases, in agreement with an analytical theory based on EP-induced Jordan-block dynamics. Our results establish EPs as a mechanism to engineer critical scaling in open quantum systems, with potential applications to critical quantum sensing.

AI.tldr: Exceptional points coinciding with dissipative phase transitions in an extended open Dicke model amplify critical fluctuations and modify critical exponents, enabling engineering of critical scaling for quantum sensing applications.
AI.motivation: To investigate how exceptional points (non-Hermitian degeneracies where eigenvalues and eigenvectors coalesce) can enhance critical phenomena when they coincide with dissipative phase transitions in open quantum systems, potentially enabling improved quantum sensing capabilities.
AI.method: Study an extended open Dicke model of two cavities coupled to a collective spin, combining numerical analysis with analytical theory based on EP-induced Jordan-block dynamics to examine the interplay between exceptional points and dissipative phase transitions.
AI.result: When exceptional points coincide with dissipative phase transitions, critical fluctuations are strongly amplified and governed by modified critical exponents. Numerical results show enhanced critical scaling in both normal and superradiant phases, consistent with analytical predictions based on Jordan-block dynamics.
AI.conclusion: Exceptional points serve as a mechanism to engineer critical scaling in open quantum systems, establishing a pathway for enhanced critical quantum sensing applications through the amplification of critical fluctuations at dissipative phase transitions.

title: Diffusing diffusivity model with dichotomous noise
summary: We study Langevin dynamics with stochastic diffusivity arising from fluctuations of the surrounding medium. The diffusivity is modeled as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process driven by symmetric dichotomous noise, which confines it to a finite interval. We derive analytical expressions for the short-time probability density function (PDF) of the particle displacement and analyse its asymptotic behaviour. While the PDF retains the characteristic logarithmic divergence at the origin, its tails differ from the Gaussian white-noise case: exponential tails are replaced by Gaussian ones modulated by a power-law with a switching-rate-dependent exponent. At long times, the dynamics converges to ordinary Gaussian diffusion. We determine the variance and covariance of the time-averaged stochastic diffusivity and show that it is self-averaging. The model provides a minimal analytically tractable framework for stochastic transport in environments with bounded or switching fluctuations.

AI.tldr: Analytical study of Langevin dynamics with stochastic diffusivity modeled as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process driven by dichotomous noise, showing non-Gaussian short-time behavior with logarithmic divergence at origin and Gaussian tails modulated by power-law, converging to ordinary diffusion at long times.
AI.motivation: To understand stochastic transport in environments with bounded or switching fluctuations by developing a minimal analytically tractable framework for Langevin dynamics with stochastic diffusivity.
AI.method: Model diffusivity as Ornstein-Uhlenbeck process driven by symmetric dichotomous noise (confining it to finite interval), derive analytical expressions for short-time PDF of particle displacement, analyze asymptotic behavior, and study variance/covariance of time-averaged stochastic diffusivity.
AI.result: Short-time PDF retains logarithmic divergence at origin but has Gaussian tails modulated by power-law with switching-rate-dependent exponent (different from Gaussian white-noise case). Long-time dynamics converges to ordinary Gaussian diffusion. Time-averaged stochastic diffusivity is self-averaging.
AI.conclusion: The model provides a minimal analytically tractable framework for studying stochastic transport in environments with bounded or switching fluctuations, capturing non-Gaussian short-time behavior while converging to normal diffusion at long times.

title: Inequivalence of Landau-Lifshitz and Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert dynamics for a single quantum spin
summary: We examine the relation between the quantum Landau-Lifshitz equation ($q$-LL) [Phys. Rev. Lett. 110, 147201 (2013)] and quantum Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert equation ($q$-LLG) [Phys. Rev. Lett. 133, 266704 (2024)]; two non-linear purity preserving master equations that extend classical atomistic spin dynamics into the quantum regime. While the classical LL and LLG counterparts for any number of spins are known to be equivalent, i.e., give identical spin trajectories up to a rescaling of the time parameter, the quantum formulations are equivalent only in certain cases, such as for pure states or for arbitrary single spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ states. Here, we demonstrate that this equivalence breaks down even at the level of a single spin, provided $s \geq 1$. Focusing on a spin-1 particle in an anisotropic crystal field, we show that the $q$-LL and $q$-LLG equations generate inequivalent time evolution. We introduce temporal rescaling misfits that quantify the inequivalence of the two types of dynamics. Although our results highlight fundamental differences in dissipation mechanisms encoded in these equations, the resulting trajectories remain qualitatively similar for this system.

AI.tldr: The paper examines the equivalence between quantum Landau-Lifshitz (q-LL) and quantum Landau-Lifshitz-Gilbert (q-LLG) equations, showing they are not equivalent for single spins with s≥1, unlike their classical counterparts.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether the equivalence between classical LL and LLG equations extends to their quantum formulations, particularly examining cases where this equivalence breaks down for quantum systems.
AI.method: Analyzed the q-LL and q-LLG equations for a spin-1 particle in an anisotropic crystal field, introduced temporal rescaling misfits to quantify inequivalence, and compared the resulting trajectories.
AI.result: Demonstrated that q-LL and q-LLG equations generate inequivalent time evolution for single spins with s≥1, with the equivalence breaking down even at the single-spin level, though trajectories remain qualitatively similar.
AI.conclusion: The quantum formulations of LL and LLG equations are fundamentally different in their dissipation mechanisms and are not equivalent for spins with s≥1, highlighting a key distinction from their classical counterparts.

title: Phase-enhanced excitations in pumped collective nuclear systems
summary: The quantum dynamics of an externally driven ensemble of nuclear two-level systems embedded in a leaky broadband cavity is investigated theoretically. In the considered scenario both the nuclear ensemble and the cavity mode are coherently pumped by two externally applied x-ray electromagnetic fields. When the frequencies of the applied coherent fields are identical, cross-correlations among the existing decay channels increase the nuclear excitation probabilities depending on the phase difference of the applied fields. Our results show that the excited state of the nuclear ensemble may exhibit sub- to super-Poissonian nuclear statistics, demonstrating induced correlations during photon absorption or emission processes. The role of cross-correlations for the superradiant decay and the collective Lamb shift of the ensemble is also investigated.

AI.tldr: Quantum dynamics of driven nuclear two-level systems in a leaky cavity shows phase-dependent excitation enhancement and tunable sub- to super-Poissonian statistics via cross-correlations.
AI.motivation: To investigate quantum dynamics of externally driven nuclear two-level systems in a leaky broadband cavity, exploring how cross-correlations between decay channels affect nuclear excitation and collective quantum phenomena.
AI.method: Theoretical investigation of nuclear ensemble and cavity mode coherently pumped by two external x-ray fields, analyzing phase-dependent effects when field frequencies are identical, and studying cross-correlations among decay channels.
AI.result: Cross-correlations increase nuclear excitation probabilities depending on phase difference; excited state exhibits tunable sub- to super-Poissonian statistics; cross-correlations affect superradiant decay and collective Lamb shift.
AI.conclusion: Cross-correlations between decay channels enable phase control of nuclear excitation and induce correlations in photon absorption/emission, with implications for superradiance and collective Lamb shift in nuclear quantum optics.

title: A Framework for Predicting Entanglement Spectra of Gapless Symmetry-Protected Topological States in One Dimension
comment: Main text contains 12 pages, and 10 figures
summary: The concept of gapped symmetry-protected topological (SPT) states has been generalized to gapless SPT (gSPT) states. Similar to gapped SPT states, gSPT states in one dimension exhibit universal degeneracies in their entanglement spectra. The entanglement spectra of gSPT states are further described by boundary conformal field theories, whose systematic prediction is a key open question. To address this problem, we focus on the class of gSPT states that are obtained by applying unitary SPT entanglers to trivial, critical states in one dimension. We find that the reduced density matrix of a non-trivial gSPT state can be obtained, either exactly or approximately, by applying a quantum channel to the reduced density matrix of the trivial gSPT state. This quantum channel acts only near the entanglement cut and modifies its corresponding conformal boundary condition, allowing us in turn to predict the boundary conformal field theory describing the entanglement spectra. We apply this framework to gSPT states protected by various symmetries and having different central charges, and further analyze the stability of boundary conditions of the entanglement cut. Our work thereby provides a framework for systematically analyzing and understanding the entanglement spectra of gSPT states.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a framework to predict boundary conformal field theories describing entanglement spectra of gapless symmetry-protected topological states by relating them to trivial states via quantum channels.
AI.motivation: While gapless SPT states exhibit universal entanglement degeneracies described by boundary CFTs, there's no systematic method to predict these boundary theories, which is a key open question in understanding gSPT entanglement properties.
AI.method: Focus on gSPT states obtained by applying unitary SPT entanglers to trivial critical states; show that reduced density matrices of non-trivial gSPTs can be obtained by applying quantum channels (acting near entanglement cuts) to reduced density matrices of trivial gSPTs, thereby modifying conformal boundary conditions.
AI.result: Developed framework successfully predicts boundary CFTs describing entanglement spectra for various gSPT states with different symmetries and central charges, and analyzes stability of entanglement cut boundary conditions.
AI.conclusion: The quantum channel approach provides a systematic framework for analyzing and understanding entanglement spectra of gSPT states by relating them to trivial states and predicting their boundary conformal field theories.

title: Logical Compilation for Multi-Qubit Iceberg Patches
summary: Recent advancements in quantum computing have enabled practical use of quantum error detecting and correcting codes. However, current architectures and future proposals of quantum computer design suffer from limited qubit counts, necessitating the use of high-rate codes. Such codes, with their code parameters denoted as $[[n, k, d]]$, have more than $1$ logical qubit per code (i.e., $k > 1$). This leads to reduced error tolerance of the code, since $\lceil (d-1)/2\rceil$ errors on any of the $n$ physical qubits can affect the logical state of all $k$ logical qubits. Therefore, it becomes critical to optimally map the input qubits of a quantum circuit to these codes, in such a way that the circuit fidelity is maximized. \par However, the problem of mapping program qubits to logical qubits for high-rate codes has not been studied in prior work. A brute force search to find the optimal mapping is super exponential (scaling as $O(n!)$, where $n$ is the number of input qubits), making exhaustive search infeasible past a small number of qubits. We propose a framework that addresses this problem on two fronts: (1) for any given mapping, it performs logical-to-physical compilation that translates input gates into efficiently encoded implementations utilizing Hadamard commutation and gate merging; and (2) it quickly searches the space of possible mappings through a merge-optimizing, noise-biased packing heuristic that identifies high-performing qubit assignments without exhaustive enumeration. To the best of our knowledge, our compiler is the first work to explore mapping and compilation for high-rate codes. Across 71 benchmark circuits, we reduce circuit depth by $34\%$, gate counts by up to $31\%$ and $17\%$ for one-qubit and two-qubit gates, and improve total variation distance by $1.75\times$, with logical selection rate improvements averaging $86\%$ relative to naive compilation.

AI.tldr: First framework for mapping program qubits to logical qubits in high-rate quantum error correction codes, reducing circuit depth by 34% and improving fidelity through optimized compilation and heuristic search.
AI.motivation: High-rate quantum error correction codes (with k > 1 logical qubits per code) reduce error tolerance since errors on physical qubits can affect all logical qubits. Optimal mapping of circuit qubits to logical qubits is critical for maximizing circuit fidelity, but brute-force search is super-exponential (O(n!)) and infeasible for practical circuits.
AI.method: Two-part framework: (1) Logical-to-physical compilation that translates input gates into efficiently encoded implementations using Hadamard commutation and gate merging for any given mapping; (2) Merge-optimizing, noise-biased packing heuristic that quickly searches the mapping space without exhaustive enumeration.
AI.result: Across 71 benchmark circuits: 34% reduction in circuit depth, up to 31% reduction in one-qubit gate counts, 17% reduction in two-qubit gate counts, 1.75× improvement in total variation distance, and average 86% improvement in logical selection rate relative to naive compilation.
AI.conclusion: First work to explore mapping and compilation for high-rate quantum error correction codes, demonstrating significant improvements in circuit efficiency and fidelity through optimized qubit assignment and compilation techniques.

title: An Information-Theoretic Bound on Thermodynamic Efficiency and the Generalized Carnot's Theorem
summary: We derive a bound on the efficiency of thermal engines that can be sharper than Carnot's limit. It is a function of statistical correlations between the engine internal state and Hamiltonian, can be saturated even in finite-time cycles, and applies to both classical and quantum engines. Specifically, the bound establishes the exact maximal efficiency of engines operating with multiple baths, tightening the upper limit set by Carnot's theorem. Then, we show that an engine made of a quantum dot coupled with fermionic baths can achieve the bound, even when operating beyond the quasistatic regime. The result provides a design principle for realistic energy harvesting machines.

AI.tldr: A new efficiency bound for thermal engines that can be sharper than Carnot's limit, applicable to both classical and quantum engines, with quantum dot implementation achieving the bound even beyond quasistatic operation.
AI.motivation: To establish a tighter efficiency bound for thermal engines than Carnot's theorem, which applies to both classical and quantum systems and can be saturated even in finite-time cycles, providing practical design principles for realistic energy harvesting machines.
AI.method: Derivation of a new efficiency bound based on statistical correlations between engine internal state and Hamiltonian, applicable to engines operating with multiple baths. Demonstration using a quantum dot coupled with fermionic baths as a specific implementation.
AI.result: The derived bound establishes the exact maximal efficiency for engines operating with multiple baths, tightening Carnot's upper limit. The quantum dot engine achieves this bound even when operating beyond the quasistatic regime.
AI.conclusion: The new efficiency bound provides a sharper theoretical limit than Carnot's theorem, is practically achievable in realistic quantum systems, and offers design principles for efficient energy harvesting machines.

title: Mitigating Barren Plateaus in Variational Quantum Circuits through PDE-Constrained Loss Functions
comment: 21 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables. Code and data available at https://github.com/nimanpra/Barren-Plateau-PDE-Mitigation
summary: The barren plateau phenomenon; where cost function gradients vanish exponentially with system size; remains a fundamental obstacle to training variational quantum circuits (VQCs) at scale. We demonstrate, both theoretically and numerically, that embedding partial differential equation (PDE) constraints into the VQC loss function provides a natural and effective mitigation mechanism against barren plateaus. We derive analytical gradient variance lower bounds showing that physics-constrained loss functions composed of local PDE residuals evaluated at spatial collocation points inherit the favorable polynomial scaling of local cost functions, while additionally benefiting from constraint-induced landscape narrowing that concentrates gradient information. Systematic numerical experiments on the one-dimensional heat equation, Burgers' equation, and the Saint-Venant shallow water equations quantify the gradient variance across 4-8 qubits and 1-5 layer depths, comparing global cost, local cost, PDE-constrained, and PDE-constrained with structured ansatz configurations. We find that PDE-constrained circuits exhibit favorable gradient variance scaling with system size, with the physics constraints creating a stabilizing effect that resists exponential gradient vanishing. Entanglement entropy analysis reveals that structured ansatze operate in a sub-maximal entanglement regime consistent with trainability. Convergence experiments confirm that physics-constrained VQCs achieve lower loss values in fewer epochs. These results establish PDE constraints as a principled, physically motivated strategy for designing trainable variational quantum circuits, with direct implications for quantum physics-informed neural networks and variational quantum simulation.

AI.tldr: PDE constraints in VQC loss functions mitigate barren plateaus by providing polynomial gradient scaling and landscape narrowing, enabling trainable quantum circuits for physics simulation.
AI.motivation: The barren plateau phenomenon causes exponential vanishing of gradients with system size, fundamentally limiting training of variational quantum circuits at scale. This work aims to develop mitigation strategies using physics-informed constraints.
AI.method: Embed partial differential equation constraints into VQC loss functions, creating physics-constrained loss functions composed of local PDE residuals evaluated at spatial collocation points. Derive analytical gradient variance lower bounds and conduct systematic numerical experiments on heat equation, Burgers' equation, and Saint-Venant shallow water equations across 4-8 qubits and 1-5 layer depths, comparing global cost, local cost, PDE-constrained, and structured ansatz configurations.
AI.result: PDE-constrained circuits exhibit favorable gradient variance scaling with system size, resisting exponential gradient vanishing. Physics constraints create stabilizing effects, and structured ansatze operate in sub-maximal entanglement regimes consistent with trainability. Convergence experiments show physics-constrained VQCs achieve lower loss values in fewer epochs.
AI.conclusion: PDE constraints provide a principled, physically motivated strategy for designing trainable variational quantum circuits, with direct implications for quantum physics-informed neural networks and variational quantum simulation by mitigating barren plateaus through constraint-induced landscape narrowing.

title: Protecting Quantum Simulations of Lattice Gauge Theories through Engineered Emergent Hierarchical Symmetries
summary: We present a strategy for the quantum simulation of many-body lattice models with constrained Hilbert spaces. We focus on lattice gauge theories (LGTs), which underlie a wide range of phenomena in particle physics, condensed matter, and quantum information. In present-day quantum computing platforms, perfect restrictions of the Hilbert space to the desired gauge sectors are beyond reach: for LGTs, violations of the local constraint are unavoidable, posing a formidable challenge for the emulation of the underlying physics. Here, we develop a Floquet-engineering framework that restructures departures from a target sector such that a series of emergent local symmetries occurs hierarchically in time and in a controllable way. This leads to a set of approximate dynamical selection rules that strongly restrict inter-sector couplings, resulting in a pronounced, symmetry-controlled hierarchy of lifetimes for the state population to spread among sectors. Concretely, this protects $U(1)$ LGTs against violations of the {defining} local symmetry. While some sectors remain very long-lived, others are destabilized on shorter timescales. We numerically verify our theory for the one-dimensional $U(1)$ quantum link model. In addition, we reveal that `defects', whose movement accounts for violations of the gauge constraint, are kinetically constrained, becoming mobile only through the assistance of intra-sector dynamics, which we describe using an effective quantum marble model. Our results can thus be used to extend the lifetime, in the spirit of passive error correction, of quantum simulations of complex many-body problems when emergent or desired local symmetries are only implemented approximately.

AI.tldr: Floquet-engineering framework for quantum simulation of lattice gauge theories that creates emergent local symmetries to protect against gauge violations through dynamical selection rules and hierarchical lifetimes.
AI.motivation: Quantum simulation of lattice gauge theories faces fundamental challenge: perfect Hilbert space constraints are impossible in current quantum platforms, leading to unavoidable gauge violations that undermine accurate physics emulation.
AI.method: Develop Floquet-engineering framework that restructures departures from target gauge sectors, creating emergent local symmetries hierarchically in time. This establishes approximate dynamical selection rules that strongly restrict inter-sector couplings, resulting in symmetry-controlled hierarchy of lifetimes for state population spread.
AI.result: Numerical verification for 1D U(1) quantum link model shows pronounced protection against gauge violations. Some sectors remain very long-lived while others destabilize faster. Defects causing gauge violations become kinetically constrained, requiring intra-sector dynamics assistance (described via effective quantum marble model).
AI.conclusion: Framework extends quantum simulation lifetimes through passive error correction when emergent or desired local symmetries are only approximately implemented, enabling more robust simulation of complex many-body problems with constrained Hilbert spaces.

title: Quantum metrological advantage of high-order squeezed states
summary: Quantum correlations can be harnessed to improve the precision in parameter estimation beyond classical capabilities. Under a standard interferometric or rotation protocol, it is well established that the optimal single-mode Gaussian state is a standard squeezed vacuum, which enables Heisenberg limited precision. In this work, we investigate the potential metrological advantage of two distinct families involving high-order squeezing, namely, mth-phase and multisqueezed states. Our results show that these non-Gaussian states can grant a significant metrological advantage with respect to the optimal squeezed vacuum under equivalent conditions, i.e. at equal occupations. Their advantage holds both at low and large occupations, but its behavior critically depends on the chosen family of high-order squeezing. While higher squeezing orders enhance the advantage, this comes at the cost of higher-order observables in the measurement for full metrological performance. Finally, we study their robustness to standard decoherence channels, i.e. pure dephasing and zero-temperature damping. Employing standard squeezing as reference state, our results indicate a reasonable robustness against damping up to a certain noise strength, while their metrological advantage becomes fragile under pure dephasing. Our work shows the potential enhancement in quantum metrology beyond Gaussian states, carefully detailing the main challenges and limitations.

AI.tldr: Non-Gaussian states with high-order squeezing (mth-phase and multisqueezed states) offer significant metrological advantages over optimal squeezed vacuum states in quantum parameter estimation, though requiring higher-order measurements and showing varying robustness to decoherence.
AI.motivation: While squeezed vacuum states achieve Heisenberg-limited precision in quantum metrology, this work investigates whether non-Gaussian states with high-order squeezing can provide further metrological advantages beyond what's achievable with optimal Gaussian states.
AI.method: The study analyzes two families of non-Gaussian states: mth-phase squeezed states and multisqueezed states. It compares their metrological performance against the optimal squeezed vacuum state under equivalent conditions (equal occupations). The analysis includes evaluation of quantum Fisher information, consideration of measurement requirements (higher-order observables), and investigation of robustness to standard decoherence channels (pure dephasing and zero-temperature damping).
AI.result: Both families of high-order squeezed states provide significant metrological advantages over optimal squeezed vacuum states at both low and large occupations. Higher squeezing orders enhance the advantage but require higher-order observables for full metrological performance. The states show reasonable robustness against zero-temperature damping up to certain noise levels, but their metrological advantage becomes fragile under pure dephasing.
AI.conclusion: Non-Gaussian states with high-order squeezing offer potential enhancements in quantum metrology beyond Gaussian states, but practical implementation faces challenges including the need for higher-order measurements and varying robustness to different decoherence channels, with particular vulnerability to pure dephasing.

title: Noise-Induced Resurrection of Dynamical Skin Effects in Quasiperiodic Non-Hermitian Systems
summary: The non-Hermitian skin effect (NHSE) refers to the accumulation of an extensive number of eigenstates at system boundaries under open boundary conditions (OBCs). As a dynamical consequence, wave packets in such systems drift and ultimately accumulate at a boundary, giving rise to the dynamical skin effect (DSE). While strong quasiperiodic potentials are known to suppress the DSE by inducing localization, we show that the introduction of Ornstein-Uhlenbeck (OU) noise unexpectedly restores it. Using perturbative analysis, we demonstrate that noise effectively maps the non-Hermitian Schrödinger dynamics onto a non-reciprocal master equation, whose complex spectrum develops a noise-induced point gap. This mechanism enables delocalization, reinstates directional transport, and revives the DSE even in regimes where the static NHSE is absent. Moreover, the relaxation dynamics exhibit a non-monotonic dependence on noise strength, reflecting a competition between noise-assisted delocalization and noise-induced decoherence. Our results uncover a noise-enabled mechanism for resurrecting the DSE and suggest a new route for controlling transport in quasiperiodic, open quantum systems.

AI.tldr: Noise can restore the dynamical skin effect in non-Hermitian quasiperiodic systems where it was previously suppressed by localization.
AI.motivation: To understand how noise affects the dynamical skin effect in non-Hermitian systems with quasiperiodic potentials, particularly when strong potentials normally suppress the effect through localization.
AI.method: Perturbative analysis showing that Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise maps non-Hermitian Schrödinger dynamics onto a non-reciprocal master equation, creating a noise-induced point gap in the complex spectrum.
AI.result: Noise unexpectedly restores the dynamical skin effect by enabling delocalization and reinstating directional transport, even in regimes where the static non-Hermitian skin effect is absent. Relaxation dynamics show non-monotonic dependence on noise strength due to competition between noise-assisted delocalization and noise-induced decoherence.
AI.conclusion: Noise provides a mechanism for resurrecting the dynamical skin effect and offers a new route for controlling transport in quasiperiodic, open quantum systems.

title: A parallel and distributed fixed-point quantum search algorithm for solving SAT problems
summary: Boolean satisfiability (SAT) problem is of fundamental importance in computer science and many application domains. For Grover's algorithm, solving the SAT problem requires $\mathcal{O}(\sqrt{2^n})$ queries--where n denotes the number of logic variables in the problem. However, Grover's algorithm suffers from the Souffle problem: specifically, when the number of solutions is unknown, terminating the algorithm too early or too late leads to a significant reduction in the probability of obtaining a solution. In this paper, we propose a parallel fixed-point (PFP) search algorithm to solve the SAT problem. By exploiting entanglement, each clause in the conjunctive normal form (CNF) formula can be processed independently, leading to a significant reduction in circuit depth. We also discuss how to perform the algorithm in distributed manner. These make the PFPS algorithm particularly suitable for the noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) era.

AI.tldr: Parallel fixed-point quantum search algorithm for SAT problems with reduced circuit depth via entanglement-based clause processing, suitable for NISQ devices.
AI.motivation: Grover's algorithm for SAT problems requires O(√2^n) queries but suffers from the "Souffle problem" where unknown solution counts cause termination timing issues, reducing success probability.
AI.method: Parallel fixed-point (PFP) search algorithm that exploits entanglement to process each clause in CNF formulas independently, reducing circuit depth, with discussion of distributed implementation.
AI.result: Algorithm achieves significant reduction in circuit depth compared to standard Grover, making it particularly suitable for NISQ-era quantum devices.
AI.conclusion: PFP search algorithm provides a practical quantum approach to SAT problems that addresses Grover's Souffle problem and is well-suited for current NISQ quantum hardware limitations.

title: Quantum Lattice Boltzmann with Denoising Collision Operators
summary: The Lattice Boltzmann method (LBM) is a well-established mesoscopic approach for simulating fluid dynamics by evolving particle distribution functions on discrete lattices. While the LBM is highly parallelizable on classical hardware, its translation to quantum algorithms is impeded by the collision process, which is intrinsically nonlinear and irreversible. Several existing quantum formulations implement this process through repeated quantum tomography and state preparation at every timestep, leading to significant overheads. We introduce a quantum LBM based on a denoising-type collision operator that avoids tomography-based updates. The collision dynamics are reformulated as an orthogonal projection onto the linearized manifold of equilibrium distributions around a reference state. This geometric approach filters non-equilibrium components while preserving lattice symmetries and approximating nonlinear terms needed to recover hydrodynamic behavior. A complete pipeline is presented with efficient gate-level realizations, incorporating encoding of distributions, collision, streaming, boundary conditions, and measurement of physical quantities such as hydrodynamic forces. In addition, we outline an approach for implementing projector-based operators deterministically without postselection, paving the way to fully coherent multi-timestep LBM simulations. Numerical experiments for advection-diffusion and flow problems demonstrate that the method reproduces macroscopic behaviors with high accuracy, with performance depending on the choice of reference state.

AI.tldr: Quantum LBM with denoising collision operator avoids tomography overhead by using orthogonal projection onto linearized equilibrium manifold, enabling efficient quantum simulation of fluid dynamics.
AI.motivation: The Lattice Boltzmann Method (LBM) is highly parallelizable but its quantum implementation is hindered by the nonlinear, irreversible collision process. Existing quantum formulations require repeated tomography and state preparation at each timestep, creating significant overhead.
AI.method: Introduces a quantum LBM based on a denoising-type collision operator that reformulates collision dynamics as an orthogonal projection onto the linearized manifold of equilibrium distributions around a reference state. This geometric approach filters non-equilibrium components while preserving lattice symmetries and approximating nonlinear terms. Provides complete pipeline with efficient gate-level realizations including encoding, collision, streaming, boundary conditions, and measurement.
AI.result: Numerical experiments for advection-diffusion and flow problems demonstrate the method reproduces macroscopic behaviors with high accuracy. Performance depends on the choice of reference state. The approach enables deterministic implementation of projector-based operators without postselection.
AI.conclusion: The proposed quantum LBM with denoising collision operator avoids tomography-based updates, reduces overhead, and paves the way for fully coherent multi-timestep LBM simulations on quantum hardware.

title: Quantum Error Mitigation Strategies for Variational PDE-Constrained Circuits on Noisy Hardware
summary: Variational quantum circuits (VQCs) solving partial differential equations (PDEs) on near-term quantum hardware face a critical challenge: hardware noise degrades solution fidelity and disrupts convergence. We present a systematic study of three noise channels; depolarizing, amplitude damping, and bit-flip on VQCs constrained by PDE residual loss functions for the heat equation, Burgers' equation, and the Saint-Venant shallow water equations. We benchmark three error mitigation strategies: zero-noise extrapolation (ZNE) via Richardson polynomial fitting, probabilistic error cancellation (PEC), and measurement error mitigation through inverse confusion matrices. Our numerical experiments on 6-qubit, 4-layer circuits demonstrate that ZNE reduces absolute error by 82-96% at low noise (p = 0.001), with effectiveness degrading gracefully at higher noise strengths. We prove analytically and confirm numerically that physics-constrained circuits exhibit inherent noise resilience: at p = 0.01, constrained circuits maintain 25-47% higher fidelity than unconstrained counterparts, with the advantage scaling with PDE complexity. PEC provides near-exact correction at low gate counts but incurs exponential sampling overhead, rendering it impractical beyond ~60 gates at p >= 0.02. Error budget decomposition reveals that systematic errors dominate at all noise levels (43-58%), while the PDE residual component grows from ~10% to ~31% as noise increases, indicating that physics constraints absorb noise through structured gradient information. These results establish practical guidelines for deploying variational PDE solvers on NISQ hardware.

AI.tldr: VQCs for PDEs suffer from hardware noise; study shows ZNE reduces error by 82-96% at low noise, physics-constrained circuits have 25-47% higher fidelity than unconstrained ones, and PEC has exponential overhead limiting practical use.
AI.motivation: Near-term quantum hardware noise degrades VQC solutions for PDEs, requiring systematic evaluation of noise effects and mitigation strategies to enable practical deployment of variational PDE solvers on NISQ devices.
AI.method: Study of three noise channels (depolarizing, amplitude damping, bit-flip) on VQCs with PDE residual loss functions for heat, Burgers', and Saint-Venant equations. Benchmark three error mitigation strategies: ZNE via Richardson polynomial fitting, PEC, and measurement error mitigation using inverse confusion matrices. Numerical experiments on 6-qubit, 4-layer circuits.
AI.result: ZNE reduces absolute error by 82-96% at low noise (p=0.001), with graceful degradation at higher noise. Physics-constrained circuits show inherent noise resilience: 25-47% higher fidelity than unconstrained at p=0.01, with advantage scaling with PDE complexity. PEC provides near-exact correction at low gate counts but has exponential sampling overhead, impractical beyond ~60 gates at p≥0.02. Error budget: systematic errors dominate (43-58%), PDE residual grows from ~10% to ~31% with noise.
AI.conclusion: Physics constraints provide inherent noise resilience through structured gradient information, ZNE is effective at low noise levels, and PEC has practical limitations due to exponential overhead. Results establish guidelines for deploying variational PDE solvers on NISQ hardware.

title: Correlated decoherence in a common environment activated by relative motion
summary: We study two spatially separated boundary subsystems coupled to a common structured environment under relative motion in a Gaussian open-system framework. By integrating out the environment, we obtain an influence functional governed by a dressed environmental correlator evaluated at the boundary positions, which encodes both coherent mediation and correlated fluctuations. Relative motion opens a correlated decoherence channel through Doppler-shifted spectral overlap of the boundary excitations, leading to a kinematic threshold at $v>2u_φ$. Below threshold, the dominant resonant contribution to the off-diagonal noise kernel is absent and the environment acts predominantly as a coherent mediator at leading resonant order. Above threshold, a resonant shell opens and the same environment supports a finite cross-noise channel, producing irreversible correlated decoherence. In the reduced dynamics, coherent coupling is governed by the retarded component of the dressed correlator, while the decoherence rate is controlled by its Hadamard component. These results establish a direct connection between motion-induced excitation production and correlated decoherence in open quantum systems, and point to experimentally accessible signatures in superconducting--phononic platforms through excess correlated dephasing.

AI.tldr: Relative motion between boundary subsystems in a structured environment creates a kinematic threshold for correlated decoherence, with coherent mediation below threshold and irreversible decoherence above threshold.
AI.motivation: To understand how relative motion between spatially separated quantum subsystems affects their correlated dynamics when coupled to a common structured environment, particularly the interplay between coherent mediation and decoherence channels.
AI.method: Gaussian open-system framework integrating out the environment to obtain an influence functional governed by a dressed environmental correlator evaluated at boundary positions, analyzing Doppler-shifted spectral overlap effects.
AI.result: Kinematic threshold at v>2u_φ separates regimes: below threshold, environment acts as coherent mediator with absent off-diagonal noise; above threshold, resonant shell opens enabling finite cross-noise channel and irreversible correlated decoherence.
AI.conclusion: Establishes direct connection between motion-induced excitation production and correlated decoherence, with experimental signatures accessible in superconducting-phononic platforms through excess correlated dephasing.

title: Unitary Designs from Two Chaotic Hamiltonians and a Random Pauli Operation
summary: The realization of unitary designs is of fundamental interest in quantum science and typically requires the ability to implement structured quantum circuits. Recent developments have explored the possibility of generating unitary designs using only a small number of quantum quenches, in which the evolution during each interval is governed by a static Hamiltonian. In particular, it has been established that at least three chaotic Hamiltonians are required when only Hamiltonian evolutions are employed. In this work, we propose the emergence of unitary designs in the temporal ensemble of qubit systems evolved under two distinct chaotic Hamiltonians for sufficiently long times, supplemented by an intermediate random Pauli operation inserted between them. This result follows from the universal Pauli spectrum of chaotic Hamiltonians, a central concept in the study of non-stabilizerness. Our theoretical predictions are verified numerically using explicit examples, including Gaussian unitary ensemble Hamiltonians and random spin models. We further investigate finite-time and finite-size corrections to the protocol. Our results provide new insights into the dynamical generation of quantum randomness and offer a new route toward realizing unitary designs in chaotic systems.

AI.tldr: Unitary designs can be generated using only two chaotic Hamiltonians plus an intermediate random Pauli operation, reducing the requirement from three Hamiltonians in previous approaches.
AI.motivation: To explore more efficient methods for generating unitary designs using fewer resources, specifically reducing the number of required chaotic Hamiltonians from three to two while maintaining the ability to create quantum randomness.
AI.method: Propose a protocol using two distinct chaotic Hamiltonians with sufficiently long evolution times, supplemented by an intermediate random Pauli operation inserted between them. The approach leverages the universal Pauli spectrum of chaotic Hamiltonians and concepts from non-stabilizerness theory.
AI.result: Theoretical predictions show that unitary designs emerge in the temporal ensemble of qubit systems under this protocol. Numerical verification using Gaussian unitary ensemble Hamiltonians and random spin models confirms the approach. Finite-time and finite-size corrections are investigated.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates a new route to realizing unitary designs in chaotic systems using only two Hamiltonians plus a Pauli operation, providing insights into dynamical generation of quantum randomness and offering a more resource-efficient approach.

title: Characterizing entanglement dynamics in QED scattering processes
comment: 7 pages, 4 figures. Partially overlaps with arXiv:2505.06878
summary: We study entanglement dynamics among helicity degrees of freedom in quantum electrodynamics (QED) scattering processes. For generic initial states, we consider scattering at fixed momentum, corresponding to a generalized measurement described by a positive operator-valued measure, resulting in a post-measurement state. Such processes are modeled in terms of quantum maps, whose spectral structure fully determines the associated entanglement dynamics. For scattering involving fermions only, maximal entanglement present in the initial state is always preserved. Moreover, iterating the corresponding quantum maps on arbitrary initial states, we obtain the fixed points of the maps, which, in the largest number of cases, are asymptotic (pure) maximally entangled states. The structure of the maps also accounts for the entanglement dynamics in processes involving both fermions and photons. The defining properties of these maps originate from discrete symmetries of the QED interaction.

AI.tldr: Entanglement dynamics in QED scattering processes are analyzed using quantum maps derived from helicity degrees of freedom, showing maximal entanglement preservation for fermion-only scattering and asymptotic convergence to maximally entangled states.
AI.motivation: To understand how entanglement evolves among helicity degrees of freedom during QED scattering processes, particularly how quantum maps derived from these processes affect entanglement dynamics and whether maximal entanglement can be preserved or asymptotically achieved.
AI.method: Model QED scattering processes as quantum maps using positive operator-valued measures for generalized measurements at fixed momentum, analyze spectral structure of these maps, study iteration on arbitrary initial states to find fixed points, and examine discrete symmetries of QED interaction.
AI.result: For fermion-only scattering, maximal entanglement in initial states is always preserved; iteration of quantum maps on arbitrary initial states yields fixed points that are asymptotic pure maximally entangled states in most cases; map structure explains entanglement dynamics for fermion-photon processes; properties originate from QED discrete symmetries.
AI.conclusion: QED scattering processes can be effectively modeled as quantum maps whose spectral properties determine entanglement dynamics, with fermion-only processes preserving maximal entanglement and generally converging to maximally entangled asymptotic states, revealing fundamental connections between discrete symmetries and entanglement preservation.

title: Encrypted clones can leak: Classification of informative subsets in Quantum Encrypted Cloning
summary: Encrypted cloning enables the redundant storage of an unknown qubit while remaining compatible with the no-cloning theorem, since only one clone can later be recovered through key-consuming decryption. Because encryption in this protocol is introduced to enable cloning-compatible redundancy rather than to guarantee confidentiality by design, its secrecy properties must be assessed explicitly. Here we classify the subsets of the encrypted-clone storage register into authorized, completely non-informative, and partially informative sets. We show that intermediate non-authorized subsets may retain only a restricted residual dependence on the input state, and we characterize exactly when this dependence occurs. The resulting leakage pattern is parity-dependent, revealing a structural confidentiality limitation of encrypted cloning.

AI.tldr: Encrypted cloning allows redundant storage of unknown qubits while respecting no-cloning theorem, but its confidentiality properties need explicit assessment due to structural leakage patterns.
AI.motivation: Encrypted cloning enables redundant storage of unknown qubits while remaining compatible with the no-cloning theorem, but since encryption is introduced for cloning-compatibility rather than confidentiality by design, its secrecy properties must be explicitly assessed.
AI.method: Classify subsets of the encrypted-clone storage register into authorized, completely non-informative, and partially informative sets. Analyze intermediate non-authorized subsets to determine residual dependence on input state and characterize when this dependence occurs.
AI.result: Intermediate non-authorized subsets may retain only restricted residual dependence on the input state. The resulting leakage pattern is parity-dependent, revealing a structural confidentiality limitation of encrypted cloning.
AI.conclusion: Encrypted cloning has inherent structural confidentiality limitations with parity-dependent leakage patterns, requiring careful assessment of secrecy properties despite its compatibility with the no-cloning theorem.

title: Interference-Protected Subradiance and Bound States in Nested Atomic Arrays
summary: Collective subradiant states in waveguide QED are highly sensitive to disorder, limiting their scalability and robustness. We propose a deterministic approach to engineering atom arrays based on a Minkowski sum construction, generating quasi-disordered structures with built-in correlations. This leads to mode-selective radiative coupling: interactions between dark modes are parametrically suppressed, while bright modes can hybridize. We study the stability of these subradiant and bound-state-like modes against moderate positional disorder. Our work provides a route to robust, analytically controllable subradiance through engineered quasi-disorder, with direct relevance to atom-waveguide and circuit QED experiments.

AI.tldr: Deterministic engineering of atom arrays using Minkowski sum construction creates quasi-disordered structures with built-in correlations, enabling robust subradiant states with mode-selective radiative coupling that suppresses dark mode interactions while allowing bright mode hybridization.
AI.motivation: Collective subradiant states in waveguide QED are highly sensitive to disorder, which limits their scalability and robustness for practical applications in quantum technologies.
AI.method: Proposed deterministic approach using Minkowski sum construction to engineer atom arrays with quasi-disordered structures having built-in correlations, enabling mode-selective radiative coupling where dark mode interactions are suppressed while bright modes can hybridize.
AI.result: The engineered structures demonstrate stable subradiant and bound-state-like modes against moderate positional disorder, providing analytically controllable subradiance through engineered quasi-disorder.
AI.conclusion: This work provides a route to robust, analytically controllable subradiance through engineered quasi-disorder, with direct relevance to atom-waveguide and circuit QED experiments, overcoming the traditional sensitivity limitations of collective subradiant states.

title: Quantum Protocols for Time Synchronisation and Distribution: A Critical Assessment
summary: Precise time synchronisation underpins critical infrastructure from telecommunications and financial markets to power grids and scientific metrology. Several families of quantum protocols have been proposed and demonstrated for clock synchronisation and time distribution, exploiting entangled photon pairs, quantum key distribution (QKD) correlations, Hong-Ou-Mandel interference, and entangled clock networks. We critically assess these approaches, reviewing the main quantum time synchronisation (QTS) protocol families, quantifying the gap between theory and experiment, and identifying practical bottlenecks in sources, detectors, and channels. We survey the classical timing landscape from Network Time Protocol (NTP) and GPS to laboratory-grade optical frequency transfer, and compare quantum and classical methods at equivalent maturity. We examine use cases including financial trading, power grids, telecommunications, scientific metrology, and military applications, evaluating whether quantum timing offers a realistic advantage. We show that time transfer, not clock performance, is now the bottleneck for distributed optical timekeeping: the best demonstrated synchronisation uncertainty (2.46~ps) falls two to three orders of magnitude short of what optical clocks with fractional frequency uncertainties of $10^{-18}$--$10^{-19}$ require. Our assessment is that quantum time synchronisation will not replace classical methods for most applications in the near-to-medium future. Its near-term value lies in physical-layer security against timing manipulation and integration with quantum communication networks, while closing the synchronisation gap for scientific metrology remains the most critical open challenge.

AI.tldr: Quantum time synchronization protocols show promise for security and integration with quantum networks, but currently fall 2-3 orders of magnitude short of optical clock requirements and won't replace classical methods for most applications in the near-to-medium term.
AI.motivation: Time synchronization is critical for infrastructure (telecom, finance, power grids, metrology). While quantum protocols have been proposed for clock synchronization, there's a need to critically assess their practical viability compared to classical methods and identify whether they offer realistic advantages for real-world applications.
AI.method: Critical assessment of quantum time synchronization (QTS) protocol families (entangled photon pairs, QKD correlations, Hong-Ou-Mandel interference, entangled clock networks). Quantification of theory-experiment gaps, identification of practical bottlenecks (sources, detectors, channels). Comparison with classical timing methods (NTP, GPS, optical frequency transfer) at equivalent maturity levels. Examination of use cases across multiple domains.
AI.result: Time transfer is the bottleneck for distributed optical timekeeping: best demonstrated synchronization uncertainty (2.46 ps) falls 2-3 orders of magnitude short of what optical clocks (10^-18–10^-19 fractional frequency uncertainty) require. Quantum timing won't replace classical methods for most applications in near-to-medium future. Near-term value lies in physical-layer security against timing manipulation and integration with quantum communication networks.
AI.conclusion: Quantum time synchronization shows limited near-term practical advantage over classical methods for most applications. Its primary value is in security applications and quantum network integration. Closing the synchronization gap for scientific metrology remains the most critical open challenge.

title: Model-Free Quantum Stabilization via Finite-Difference Lyapunov Control
comment: Accepted manuscript. Published in International Journal of Control (09 April 2026). The Version of Record is freely available at https://doi.org/10.1080/00207179.2026.2656156
summary: We develop a model-free framework for stabilizing quantum states using only empirical finite-difference evaluations of a measurement-derived Lyapunov observable. The controller requires no knowledge of the Hamiltonian, dissipative structure, or generator of the dynamics, and relies solely on discrete measurement data. The approach combines three key elements: sign-based Lyapunov descent, adaptive gain amplification, and a finite-difference analogue of LaSalle's invariance principle. We provide rigorous conditions under which these mechanisms guarantee asymptotic stabilization along the sampling instants in the drift-free case and practical input-to-state stability (ISS) in the presence of unknown drift and noise. The resulting feedback law is simple, derivative-free, and experimentally feasible. A qubit example illustrates the complete closed-loop scheme and the predicted ISS-type behavior. Although demonstrated on a single qubit, the theory applies to arbitrary finite-dimensional quantum systems and offers a foundation for further developments in stochastic, subspace, and multi-qudit model-free quantum control.

AI.tldr: Model-free quantum state stabilization using measurement-derived Lyapunov observables without system knowledge, relying only on discrete measurement data.
AI.motivation: To develop quantum control methods that don't require knowledge of system Hamiltonians, dissipative structures, or dynamics generators, enabling control based solely on empirical measurement data.
AI.method: Combines sign-based Lyapunov descent, adaptive gain amplification, and finite-difference analogue of LaSalle's invariance principle using only discrete measurement data of a measurement-derived Lyapunov observable.
AI.result: Provides rigorous conditions for asymptotic stabilization in drift-free case and practical input-to-state stability (ISS) with unknown drift/noise. The feedback law is simple, derivative-free, and experimentally feasible.
AI.conclusion: The model-free framework successfully stabilizes quantum states using only empirical measurements, applicable to arbitrary finite-dimensional quantum systems and providing foundation for stochastic, subspace, and multi-qudit model-free quantum control.

title: Comparing quantum and classical finite state generators
summary: Bell-CHSH-like inequalities have been very successful in benchmarking {\it spatial} quantum correlations. However, as this paper illustrates, they are in general not sufficient for benchmarking {\it temporal} quantum correlations. To show this, we parametrise classical and quantum stochastic finite state generators based on a single bit and a single qubit, respectively, and compare the temporal correlations of their output sequences using a Bell-CHSH-like inequality. We find that for sequential measurements by two observers, Alice and Bob, classical machines can exceed the Tsirelson bound of $2\sqrt{2}$, due to their fundamental structure. However, when we consider a time delay between consecutive measurements, we find examples where the quantum machines outperform their classical counterparts by maintaining correlations longer under generally scrambling operations. Our result can be used to distinguish quantum from classical processes and to identify novel resources for quantum technology applications.

AI.tldr: Bell-CHSH inequalities are insufficient for benchmarking temporal quantum correlations; classical single-bit machines can exceed Tsirelson bound, but quantum qubit machines maintain correlations longer under time delays and scrambling operations.
AI.motivation: To demonstrate that Bell-CHSH-like inequalities, while effective for spatial quantum correlations, are inadequate for benchmarking temporal quantum correlations, and to develop methods for distinguishing quantum from classical temporal processes.
AI.method: Parameterized classical stochastic finite state generators (single bit) and quantum stochastic finite state generators (single qubit), compared temporal correlations of output sequences using Bell-CHSH-like inequality. Analyzed sequential measurements by two observers (Alice and Bob) with and without time delays between consecutive measurements.
AI.result: Classical single-bit machines can exceed the Tsirelson bound of 2√2 due to their fundamental structure. However, with time delays between measurements, quantum single-qubit machines outperform classical counterparts by maintaining correlations longer under generally scrambling operations.
AI.conclusion: Bell-CHSH inequalities are insufficient for temporal correlation benchmarking. The findings provide methods to distinguish quantum from classical temporal processes and identify novel resources for quantum technology applications, particularly in maintaining correlations under time delays and scrambling operations.

title: Complementary Quantum Time Distributions from a Single Operational Protocol
summary: A single operational protocol based on free evolution and projective measurements yields inequivalent quantum time distributions through distinct post-processing procedures. We construct an activity-based time-of-flow (TF) distribution and a presence-based quantum stroboscopic (QS) distribution, providing complementary operational notions of time. Applied to tunneling, the regional QS mean saturates, whereas the TF mean first decreases in the Hartman regime and then grows for larger barrier widths. Within this framework, we provide an operational interpretation of the Hartman effect in terms of quantum time distributions associated with flow through the exit region and occupation within the barrier, capturing the mechanism of early penetration, dominant reflection, and spectrally filtered transmission.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that different post-processing of the same quantum measurement protocol yields distinct time distributions (activity-based time-of-flow vs presence-based quantum stroboscopic), which provide complementary operational notions of time in quantum tunneling.
AI.motivation: To establish operational quantum time distributions from a single measurement protocol, addressing the ambiguity in defining quantum time observables and providing complementary perspectives on quantum tunneling dynamics.
AI.method: A single operational protocol based on free evolution and projective measurements, with distinct post-processing procedures to construct two distributions: activity-based time-of-flow (TF) and presence-based quantum stroboscopic (QS).
AI.result: The QS mean saturates in tunneling, while the TF mean first decreases (Hartman regime) then grows for larger barrier widths. The framework provides operational interpretation of the Hartman effect in terms of flow through exit region and occupation within barrier.
AI.conclusion: Different post-processing of identical quantum measurements yields inequivalent but complementary time distributions, enabling operational interpretation of quantum tunneling phenomena including the Hartman effect through flow and occupation perspectives.

title: A Detector-Based Inference Framework for Quantum Theory and Spacetime Geometry
summary:
We develop a detector-based framework in which quantum theory and spacetime geometry arise within a common inferential structure. Detector states and a detector kernel assign amplitudes to measurement events, allowing quantum theory to be interpreted as weighting hypothetical configurations consistent with observed detector clicks.
  Using a Gaussian detector model with phase structure, we show that distinguishability induces an information geometry on detector-state space, described by the quantum geometric tensor. A Lorentzian spacetime metric is reconstructed from coupled position and time detector sectors, with both amplitude and phase deformations contributing to geometry. Scalar curvature acquires an operational interpretation as a local deficit of distinguishable outcomes.
  We construct an effective consistency functional combining detector-deformation cost with a geometric term selected by locality and diffeomorphism invariance. Its stationary configurations yield the Einstein equation, with a stress-energy tensor arising from detector deformations. Vacuum configurations need not be flat, while local deformations provide an operational notion of matter and recover standard field-theoretic behavior in the scalar sector.

AI.tldr: Quantum theory and spacetime geometry emerge from a common inferential framework using detectors, where distinguishability induces information geometry and Lorentzian spacetime metrics are reconstructed from detector sectors.
AI.motivation: To develop a unified framework where both quantum theory and spacetime geometry arise from a common inferential structure, bridging the gap between quantum mechanics and general relativity through operational detector-based measurements.
AI.method: A detector-based framework using detector states and kernel to assign amplitudes to measurement events; Gaussian detector model with phase structure; reconstruction of Lorentzian spacetime metric from coupled position and time detector sectors; construction of effective consistency functional combining detector-deformation cost with geometric term.
AI.result: Distinguishability induces information geometry described by quantum geometric tensor; spacetime metric reconstructed with amplitude and phase deformations contributing to geometry; scalar curvature gets operational interpretation as local deficit of distinguishable outcomes; stationary configurations yield Einstein equation with stress-energy tensor from detector deformations.
AI.conclusion: Quantum theory and spacetime geometry can emerge from a common inferential detector framework, providing operational interpretations of geometric concepts and recovering Einstein equations from detector deformations, with vacuum configurations not necessarily flat and local deformations providing matter.

title: An Undergraduate Course in Quantum Computing
summary: This is the text for a one quarter or one semester undergraduate course on quantum computing that has been given at the University of California Santa Cruz. It is intended for students in the physical sciences who have already studied linear algebra (though a review of this topic is given in the course). No prior knowledge of quantum mechanics is required. The most important topics covered are Shor's algorithm and an introduction to quantum error correction. Most of the text is a build-up to these topics.

AI.tldr: An undergraduate quantum computing course text for physical science students with linear algebra background, focusing on Shor's algorithm and quantum error correction.
AI.motivation: To provide accessible quantum computing education for physical science undergraduates without requiring prior quantum mechanics knowledge, building foundational understanding for key quantum algorithms and error correction.
AI.method: Structured course text with linear algebra review, progressive build-up to core topics, designed for quarter/semester undergraduate curriculum at UC Santa Cruz.
AI.result: A comprehensive educational resource covering quantum computing fundamentals, culminating in detailed treatment of Shor's algorithm and introduction to quantum error correction.
AI.conclusion: The text successfully provides a pedagogical pathway for physical science students to understand quantum computing essentials, with Shor's algorithm and error correction as central learning objectives.

title: Quantum Vacuum Radiation Near a Critical Point
summary: Equilibrium quantum phase transitions profoundly reshape the ground state of light-matter systems, yet, the resulting quantum correlations, such as squeezing and entanglement, remain experimentally inaccessible since they involve virtual ground state excitations. We investigate how nonadiabatic modulation of a Hamiltonian parameter can convert these virtual excitations into real photons, enabling quantum vacuum radiation. We show that proximity to the critical point strongly enhances the emitted photon flux and the non-classical nature of the emitted radiation, even when thermal fluctuations are expected to dominate. In addition, higher-order processes become relevant even for small modulation amplitudes, and we develop a framework that systematically incorporates them. Our results reveal that criticality can act as an efficient amplifier of vacuum fluctuations, offering new routes to probe and exploit quantum critical ground states.

AI.tldr: Nonadiabatic modulation near quantum critical points converts virtual ground state excitations into real photons, enabling quantum vacuum radiation with enhanced photon flux and non-classical properties.
AI.motivation: Equilibrium quantum phase transitions create quantum correlations (squeezing, entanglement) in ground states, but these remain experimentally inaccessible as they involve virtual excitations. The paper aims to make these quantum correlations observable by converting virtual excitations into real photons.
AI.method: Investigates nonadiabatic modulation of Hamiltonian parameters near critical points to convert virtual excitations into real photons. Develops a systematic framework to incorporate higher-order processes that become relevant even for small modulation amplitudes.
AI.result: Proximity to critical point strongly enhances emitted photon flux and non-classical nature of radiation, even when thermal fluctuations dominate. Criticality acts as an efficient amplifier of vacuum fluctuations, enabling quantum vacuum radiation.
AI.conclusion: Criticality can amplify vacuum fluctuations, offering new routes to probe and exploit quantum critical ground states by making their quantum correlations experimentally accessible through quantum vacuum radiation.

title: Worst-case Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd algorithm with average-case correct quantum Fourier transform
