summary: In [\href{https://quantum-journal.org/papers/q-2022-12-07-872/}{Quantum 6, 872, 2022}], Linden and de Wolf proposed a lightweight protocol for verifying the average-case correct behavior of the quantum Fourier transform (QFT). They proved that good average-case QFT performance suffices for good worst-case performance in several quantum tasks. Here we provide another application of this worst-case-to-average-case reduction, using a strengthened Linden-de Wolf protocol. We show that, across three distinct scenarios, the Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd algorithm can be executed with provably good worst-case performance, assuming only that the QFT is correct on average.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that the Harrow-Hassidim-Lloyd (HHL) algorithm can achieve provably good worst-case performance assuming only average-case correctness of the Quantum Fourier Transform (QFT), building on Linden and de Wolf's worst-case-to-average-case reduction.
AI.motivation: To extend the applicability of Linden and de Wolf's worst-case-to-average-case reduction for QFT verification by showing it enables provable worst-case performance guarantees for the important HHL algorithm for solving linear systems.
AI.method: Uses a strengthened version of Linden and de Wolf's lightweight QFT verification protocol to establish worst-case-to-average-case reduction, then applies this to analyze the HHL algorithm across three distinct scenarios.
AI.result: Demonstrates that the HHL algorithm can be executed with provably good worst-case performance in three different scenarios, requiring only the assumption that QFT performs correctly on average.
AI.conclusion: The worst-case-to-average-case reduction for QFT verification has broader applicability than previously shown, enabling rigorous performance guarantees for important quantum algorithms like HHL with relaxed assumptions about QFT correctness.

title: Finite-temperature quantum Krylov method from real-time overlaps
summary: Accurately evaluating finite-temperature properties of quantum many-body systems remains a central challenge. Many existing quantum approaches typically require thermal-state preparation at each target temperature, making low-temperature calculations especially demanding in terms of circuit depth and accuracy. Here we introduce a distinct framework based only on the real-time overlap sequence $g_n=\langle φ|e^{-inτH}|φ\rangle$, which enables thermodynamic quantities to be obtained over a broad temperature range, without specifying a target temperature on the quantum device. For the one-dimensional spin-$\frac{1}{2}$ Heisenberg model with periodic boundary conditions, we obtain accurate specific heat, magnetic susceptibility, and entropy in the noiseless case. Magnetic susceptibility is also evaluated accurately without explicit symmetry-sector decomposition by employing pseudorandom vectors compatible with $S_{\mathrm{tot}}^{z}$ conservation. With suitable stabilization, the method further retains the main thermodynamic features under finite-shot statistical errors up to $σ\sim10^{-3}$. Our results establish real-time-overlap-based finite-temperature evaluation as a promising framework for finite-temperature computation on near-future quantum hardware.

AI.tldr: A quantum computing framework using real-time overlap sequences to compute finite-temperature thermodynamic properties without target-temperature-specific thermal state preparation, demonstrated on the 1D Heisenberg model.
AI.motivation: Existing quantum approaches for finite-temperature evaluation require thermal-state preparation at each target temperature, making low-temperature calculations computationally demanding in terms of circuit depth and accuracy.
AI.method: A framework based on real-time overlap sequences $g_n=\langle φ|e^{-inτH}|φ\rangle$ that enables thermodynamic quantity computation over a broad temperature range without specifying target temperatures on quantum devices, using pseudorandom vectors compatible with symmetry conservation.
AI.result: Accurate specific heat, magnetic susceptibility, and entropy obtained for the 1D spin-1/2 Heisenberg model in noiseless case; magnetic susceptibility evaluated accurately without explicit symmetry-sector decomposition; method retains main thermodynamic features under finite-shot statistical errors up to $σ\sim10^{-3}$.
AI.conclusion: Real-time-overlap-based finite-temperature evaluation establishes a promising framework for finite-temperature computation on near-future quantum hardware, overcoming limitations of temperature-specific thermal state preparation.

title: The spontaneous disentanglement hypothesis and causality
summary: The hypothesis that disentanglement spontaneously occurs in quantum systems is motivated by some outstanding issues in the foundations of quantum mechanics. However, for some cases, spontaneous disentanglement enables the violation of the causality principle. To mitigate the conflict with causality, a formulation for the hypothesis, which is based on the maximum entropy principle, is proposed. The method of Lagrange multipliers is implemented to ensure consistency with causality. The proposed formulation is applicable for any quantum system having a Hilbert space of finite dimensionality.

AI.tldr: A maximum entropy formulation of spontaneous disentanglement that preserves causality through Lagrange multipliers for finite-dimensional quantum systems.
AI.motivation: Addressing foundational issues in quantum mechanics where spontaneous disentanglement could violate causality, requiring a formulation that reconciles disentanglement with causal principles.
AI.method: Proposes a maximum entropy principle formulation with Lagrange multipliers to enforce consistency with causality, applicable to any finite-dimensional quantum system.
AI.result: Develops a causality-preserving formulation of spontaneous disentanglement that avoids violations of the causality principle while maintaining the core hypothesis.
AI.conclusion: The maximum entropy approach with Lagrange multipliers provides a viable framework for spontaneous disentanglement that respects causality in finite-dimensional quantum systems.

title: Post-Cut Metadata Inference Attacks on Quantum Circuit Cutting Pipelines
summary: Quantum circuit cutting enables near-term quantum devices to execute workloads exceeding their qubit capacity by decomposing circuits into independently runnable fragments. While this extends computational reach, it creates a previously unexplored confidentiality surface: the fragment-level execution transcript observable by a semi-honest cloud provider. We formalise this surface and demonstrate that post-cut transcripts constitute a practical metadata side channel. Operating solely on provider-visible compiled circuit metadata (fragment width, depth, and two-qubit gate count), we evaluate a structured inference attack across six classification objectives spanning algorithm identity, cut mechanism, and coarse Hamiltonian structure. Our corpus comprises 1,200 circuit fragments across eight algorithm families transpiled against three hardware topologies, validated on a 156-qubit production quantum computer confirming that QPU execution time remains invariant across a 25x variation in compiled depth. Under strict instance-disjoint generalisation, our attack recovers algorithm family with 0.960 accuracy (AUC 0.999), cut mechanism with 0.847 accuracy (AUC 0.924), and Hamiltonian k-locality with 0.960 accuracy (AUC 0.998). Connectivity and geometry inference achieve AUC of 0.986 and 0.942 with strong stability under size-holdout. Topology inference remains above chance (AUC 0.666). A matched-footprint control and ablation study confirm leakage is structure-dominated and not explained by scale artefacts. These results demonstrate that circuit cutting is not confidentiality-neutral and that metadata leakage should be treated as a first-class security concern in quantum cloud systems.

AI.tldr: Circuit cutting creates a metadata side channel in quantum cloud computing, allowing semi-honest providers to infer algorithm details from execution transcripts.
AI.motivation: To investigate confidentiality risks in quantum circuit cutting, where execution fragments create metadata leakage that could compromise algorithm privacy in cloud quantum computing.
AI.method: Formalized the confidentiality surface and conducted structured inference attacks using only provider-visible compiled circuit metadata (fragment width, depth, two-qubit gate count) across six classification objectives on 1,200 circuit fragments from eight algorithm families.
AI.result: High accuracy recovery: algorithm family (0.960 AUC 0.999), cut mechanism (0.847 AUC 0.924), Hamiltonian k-locality (0.960 AUC 0.998). Connectivity/geometry inference achieved AUC 0.986/0.942. QPU execution time remained invariant across 25x depth variation.
AI.conclusion: Circuit cutting is not confidentiality-neutral; metadata leakage constitutes a practical side channel that should be treated as a first-class security concern in quantum cloud systems.

title: Adaptive H-EFT-VA: A Provably Safe Trajectory Through the Trainability-Expressibility Landscape of Variational Quantum Algorithms
summary: H-EFT-VA established a physics-informed solution to the Barren Plateau (BP) problem via a hierarchical EFT UV-cutoff, guaranteeing gradient variance in Omega(1/poly(N)). However, localization restricts the ansatz to a polynomial subspace, creating a reference-state gap for states distant from |0>^N. We introduce Adaptive H-EFT-VA (A-H-EFT) to navigate the trainability-expressibility tradeoff by expanding the reachable Hilbert space along a safe trajectory. Gradient variance is maintained in Omega(1/poly(N)) if sigma(t) <= 0.5/sqrt(LN) (Theorem 1). A Safe Expansion Corollary and Monotone Growth Lemma confirm expansion without discontinuous jumps. Benchmarking across 16 experiments (up to N=14) shows A-H-EFT achieves fidelity F=0.54, doubling static H-EFT-VA (F=0.27) and outperforming HEA (F~0.01), with gradient variance >= 0.5 throughout. For Heisenberg XXZ (Delta_ref=1), A-H-EFT identifies the negative ground state while static methods fail. Results are statistically significant (p < 10^-37). Robustness over three decades of hyperparameters enables deployment without search. This is the first rigorously bounded trajectory through the VQA landscape.

AI.tldr: A-H-EFT extends H-EFT-VA with adaptive expansion to overcome reference-state limitations while maintaining polynomial gradient scaling, achieving significantly higher fidelity than static methods across quantum system benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Static H-EFT-VA solves the Barren Plateau problem but is limited to polynomial subspaces near |0>^N, creating a reference-state gap for distant quantum states. The tradeoff between trainability (avoiding barren plateaus) and expressibility (reaching target states) needs adaptive navigation.
AI.method: Adaptive H-EFT-VA (A-H-EFT) expands the reachable Hilbert space along a safe trajectory while maintaining gradient variance. The method uses a hierarchical EFT UV-cutoff with adaptive expansion controlled by sigma(t) ≤ 0.5/√(LN) to guarantee polynomial gradient scaling. Includes Safe Expansion Corollary and Monotone Growth Lemma to ensure continuous expansion without jumps.
AI.result: Across 16 experiments (up to N=14 qubits), A-H-EFT achieves fidelity F=0.54, doubling static H-EFT-VA (F=0.27) and vastly outperforming HEA (F~0.01). Maintains gradient variance ≥0.5 throughout. For Heisenberg XXZ (Δ_ref=1), identifies negative ground state where static methods fail. Results statistically significant (p < 10^-37). Robust over three decades of hyperparameters.
AI.conclusion: A-H-EFT provides the first rigorously bounded trajectory through the VQA landscape, navigating the trainability-expressibility tradeoff while maintaining polynomial gradient scaling. Enables deployment without hyperparameter search and overcomes reference-state limitations of static methods.

title: Symplectic Constraints in Quantum Reaction Dynamics: Squeezed-State Suppression and Candidate Width Scales
summary:
Classical reaction dynamics suggests transport through an index-1 saddle is organized not just by flux, but by local symplectic width scales of bounded proxy neighborhoods near the bottleneck. We investigate if a related geometric effect appears in the quantum regime for highly squeezed Gaussian wavepackets. Building on de Gosson's symplectic approach, we analyze how transverse bath-mode squeezing modifies transmission across a quantum normal-form (QNF) bottleneck.
  To avoid the instability of propagating states with extreme phase-space eccentricity, we use the Weyl-symbol formulation of the QNF. For the quadratic saddle-center model, we derive an exact baseline transmission formula by convolving the bath's squeezed-state number distribution with the 1D Kemble transmission factor. For anharmonic truncated QNF models, we enforce strict algebraic energy conservation and evaluate exact Gaussian expectation-value diagnostics of the Weyl symbol via Wick-Isserlis moment formulas.
  Results reveal a pronounced squeeze-induced suppression of transmission. As the squeezed state's bath-plane geometric scale grows relative to the classical candidate width, the expected bath action grows rapidly. Consequently, effective reactive energy is strongly depleted, driving transmission into a severely suppressed regime. We interpret this as evidence of a quantum geometric suppression mechanism consistent with the classical candidate symplectic-width picture. While not yet a rigorous quantum non-squeezing theorem, this work provides a concrete framework linking squeezed-state covariance geometry, normal-form action scales, and mode-specific quantum reactivity near an index-1 saddle.

AI.tldr: Quantum transmission through an index-1 saddle is suppressed by transverse bath-mode squeezing due to geometric effects on effective reactive energy.
AI.motivation: Investigate whether classical geometric effects related to symplectic width scales near saddle bottlenecks manifest in quantum regime for highly squeezed Gaussian wavepackets, building on de Gosson's symplectic approach.
AI.method: Use Weyl-symbol formulation of quantum normal-form (QNF) to avoid instability from extreme phase-space eccentricity. For quadratic saddle-center model, derive exact transmission formula by convolving bath's squeezed-state number distribution with 1D Kemble transmission factor. For anharmonic truncated QNF models, enforce strict algebraic energy conservation and evaluate exact Gaussian expectation-value diagnostics via Wick-Isserlis moment formulas.
AI.result: Pronounced squeeze-induced suppression of transmission observed. As squeezed state's bath-plane geometric scale grows relative to classical candidate width, expected bath action increases rapidly, depleting effective reactive energy and driving transmission into severely suppressed regime.
AI.conclusion: Evidence of quantum geometric suppression mechanism consistent with classical candidate symplectic-width picture. Provides concrete framework linking squeezed-state covariance geometry, normal-form action scales, and mode-specific quantum reactivity near index-1 saddle, though not yet a rigorous quantum non-squeezing theorem.

title: Role of Asymmetry in the Performance Optimization of a Relativistic Quantum Otto Engine
summary: We present an analytical study of the relativistic quantum Otto cycle driven by a time-dependent harmonic oscillator. By imposing an asymmetry on the two adiabatic processes of this cycle, we obtain distinct scenarios of sudden compression and sudden expansion, and analyze how asymmetry affects the performance of the relativistic quantum Otto engine. By leveraging the Omega function as a unified performance metric, we analytically characterize the efficiency in both scenarios. Our findings demonstrate that the efficiency approaches unity in the sudden compression case, while it is restricted to one-half for the sudden expansion case. Furthermore, we investigate the impact of increasing oscillator velocity on the extracted work and identify parameter regimes where either sudden compression or sudden expansion dominates. Additionally, we examine the optimal operating point using parametric efficiency-work plots, whose loop-shaped structure shows that increasing oscillator velocity enhances both work output and efficiency. Finally, through a detailed phase diagram analysis of the Otto cycle, we observe that the operational region corresponding to the engine mode expands with increasing oscillator velocity, while the refrigeration regime shrinks correspondingly.

AI.tldr: Analytical study of relativistic quantum Otto cycle with time-dependent harmonic oscillator shows asymmetry in adiabatic processes leads to different efficiency limits: unity for sudden compression, one-half for sudden expansion, with oscillator velocity enhancing work output and expanding engine operational region.
AI.motivation: To understand how asymmetry in adiabatic processes affects the performance of relativistic quantum Otto engines, particularly examining the distinct scenarios of sudden compression and sudden expansion, and how increasing oscillator velocity influences work extraction and operational regimes.
AI.method: Analytical study using the Omega function as unified performance metric to characterize efficiency in both sudden compression and sudden expansion scenarios; parametric efficiency-work plots for optimal operating point analysis; phase diagram analysis of Otto cycle operational regions.
AI.result: Efficiency approaches unity for sudden compression but is limited to one-half for sudden expansion; increasing oscillator velocity enhances both work output and efficiency; engine operational region expands while refrigeration regime shrinks with higher oscillator velocity; parameter regimes identified where either sudden compression or expansion dominates.
AI.conclusion: Asymmetry in adiabatic processes significantly impacts relativistic quantum Otto engine performance, with sudden compression achieving near-perfect efficiency while sudden expansion is fundamentally limited; oscillator velocity serves as a key parameter for enhancing work extraction and expanding engine operational capabilities.

title: Symplectic perspective to quantum computing for Hamiltonian systems
summary: This work develops a symplectic framework for quantum computing to be applied to classical Hamiltonian systems, exploiting the intrinsic geometric compatibility between unitary quantum evolution and symplectic phase-space dynamics in a two-fold way. The first part is devoted in establishing an exact correspondence between quantum evolution and classical Hamiltonian flow on a Kahler manifold. This correspondence enables a geometric quantization scheme that identifies a family of classical Hamiltonian systems admitting exponentially compressed quantum representations-appropriate for quantum simulation. In the second part we demonstrate that Liouville-integrable Hamiltonian dynamics induce finite-dimensional unitary evolution through action-angle variables and Koopman-von Neumann encoding. This allows efficient quantum representation and parallel evolution of large phase-space ensembles, where entangled encodings provide exponential compression in ensemble size and enable quantum speed-ups in observable estimation via amplitude estimation techniques. For non-integrable systems, Lie canonical perturbation theory is incorporated to construct near-symplectic transformations that map dynamics to approximately integrable forms, preserving unitary evolution up to a controlled error. We derive the resulting quantum computational complexity of the proposed quantum-symplectic scheme, revealing both an exponential compression in memory requirements and a potential polynomial speed-up with respect to the system size. Finally, the transport evolution equation governing the quantum phase-space observables is obtained.

AI.tldr: A symplectic framework for quantum computing applied to classical Hamiltonian systems, establishing geometric correspondence between quantum evolution and classical dynamics, enabling exponential compression and potential quantum speed-ups.
AI.motivation: To exploit the intrinsic geometric compatibility between unitary quantum evolution and symplectic phase-space dynamics for quantum simulation of classical Hamiltonian systems, aiming to achieve exponential compression in representation and potential quantum speed-ups.
AI.method: 1) Establishes exact correspondence between quantum evolution and classical Hamiltonian flow on Kähler manifold for geometric quantization. 2) Uses Liouville-integrable Hamiltonian dynamics with action-angle variables and Koopman-von Neumann encoding for finite-dimensional unitary evolution. 3) For non-integrable systems, applies Lie canonical perturbation theory to construct near-symplectic transformations to approximately integrable forms. 4) Derives quantum computational complexity and transport evolution equation for quantum phase-space observables.
AI.result: The framework enables exponential compression in memory requirements for representing classical Hamiltonian systems and potential polynomial speed-up with respect to system size. Entangled encodings provide exponential compression in ensemble size and enable quantum speed-ups in observable estimation via amplitude estimation techniques.
AI.conclusion: The symplectic framework successfully bridges quantum computing and classical Hamiltonian dynamics, providing both theoretical foundations and practical methods for quantum simulation with significant advantages in compression and computational efficiency.

title: Training single-electron and single-photon stochastic physical neural networks
summary: The computational demands of deep learning motivate the investigation of alternative approaches to computation. One alternative is physical neural networks~(PNNs), in which learning and inference are performed directly via physical processes. Stochastic PNNs arise when the underlying neurons are realized by the dynamics of a stochastic activation switch. Here we propose novel electronic and photonic stochastic neurons. The electronic realization is implemented by single-electron tunneling through a quantum dot. The photonic realization is implemented via a single-photon source driving one of two modes coupled via a controllable beam-splitter-like interaction. In the electronic case, the charge state of the quantum dot forms the basis for the stochastic neuron, whereas in the photonic case the occupation of the undriven mode serves as the basis for the stochastic neuron. Training of stochastic PNNs is performed with models of stochastic neurons, as well as with coherently-driven, single-photon detector stochastic neurons previously introduced. Several training strategies for MNIST handwritten digit classification have been investigated using single-hidden-layer stochastic PNNs, including varying the number of trials in each layer to control forward pass stochasticity and employing either true probability or empirical outputs in the backward pass to evaluate their influence on gradient estimation. We show that when empirical outputs are used in the backward pass, the network achieves more than 97\% test accuracy with few trials per layer. Despite the simplicity of the model architecture, high test accuracy is maintained in the presence of a high degree of noise and model uncertainty. The results demonstrate the potential of embracing stochastic PNNs for deep learning.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes novel electronic and photonic stochastic neurons for physical neural networks, demonstrating high MNIST classification accuracy despite noise and stochasticity.
AI.motivation: To address the computational demands of deep learning by investigating alternative physical neural networks that perform learning and inference directly via physical processes, specifically focusing on stochastic PNNs where neurons are realized through stochastic activation switches.
AI.method: Proposes two novel stochastic neuron implementations: electronic (single-electron tunneling through quantum dots) and photonic (single-photon source with controllable beam-splitter interaction). Trains single-hidden-layer stochastic PNNs on MNIST using various strategies including controlling forward pass stochasticity via trial numbers and using either true probability or empirical outputs in backward passes for gradient estimation.
AI.result: Achieves over 97% test accuracy on MNIST with few trials per layer when using empirical outputs in backward pass. Maintains high accuracy despite significant noise and model uncertainty, demonstrating robustness of stochastic PNNs.
AI.conclusion: Stochastic physical neural networks show significant potential for deep learning applications, offering computational advantages while maintaining high performance even under noisy conditions and model uncertainties.

title: Effective schemes for fusion of hyperentangled W states
summary: Hyperentangled states are fascinating resources in quantum information processing as they can significantly increase the channel capacity and enhance noise resistance. We explore a hyperfusion mechanism to fuse one n photon hyper-W state and one m-photon hyper-W state into a large-scale (n+m-2)-photon hyper-W state. Another mechanism to fuse one n-photon hyper-W state, one m-photon hyper-$W$ state, and one $t$-photon hyper-W state into an (n+m+t-3)-photon hyper-W state is also proposed. These two hyperfusion mechanisms are constructed employing only polarizing beam splitters, balanced beam splitters, half-wave plates, single-photon detectors, and cross-Kerr nonlinearities. Conditional quantum gates, path couplers, and ancillary photons are not required in our constructions. Moreover, our fused $W$ states are hyperentangled in the polarization and spatial degrees of freedom of single-photon systems. The presence of only one garbage output state demonstrates that high efficiency can be achieved in our schemes.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes two hyperfusion mechanisms for combining hyper-W states using only basic optical elements, achieving high efficiency without ancillary photons or complex quantum gates.
AI.motivation: Hyperentangled states offer advantages in quantum information processing by increasing channel capacity and enhancing noise resistance, but efficient methods for creating large-scale hyper-W states are needed.
AI.method: Two hyperfusion mechanisms: 1) fusing n-photon and m-photon hyper-W states into (n+m-2)-photon states, and 2) fusing n, m, and t-photon hyper-W states into (n+m+t-3)-photon states using only polarizing beam splitters, balanced beam splitters, half-wave plates, single-photon detectors, and cross-Kerr nonlinearities.
AI.result: The schemes produce hyperentangled W states in polarization and spatial degrees of freedom with only one garbage output state, demonstrating high efficiency without requiring conditional quantum gates, path couplers, or ancillary photons.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hyperfusion mechanisms provide efficient methods for creating large-scale hyper-W states using minimal optical components, offering practical advantages for quantum information processing applications.

title: Answering Counting Queries with Differential Privacy on a Quantum Computer
summary: Differential privacy is a mathematical notion of data privacy that has fast become the de facto standard in privacy-preserving data analysis. Recently a lot of work has focused on differential privacy in the quantum setting. Continuing on this line of study, we investigate how to answer counting queries on a quantum encoded dataset with differential privacy. An example of a counting query is ``How many people in the dataset are over the age of 25 and with a university education?'' Counting queries form the most basic but nonetheless rich set of statistics extractable from a dataset. We show that answering these queries on a quantum encoded dataset reduces to measuring the amplitude of one of two orthogonal states. We then analyze the differential privacy properties of two algorithms from literature to measure amplitude: one which performs repeated measurements in the computational basis, and the other which utilizes the classic amplitude estimation algorithm. For the first technique, we prove privacy results for the case of counting queries that improve on previously known results on general queries, and show that the mechanism in fact \emph{amplifies} privacy due to inherent randomness. For the second method, we derive a tight bound on maximum possible change in the amplitude if we add or remove a single item in the dataset, a quantity called global sensitivity which is central in making an algorithm differentially private. We then show a differentially private version of the amplitude estimation algorithm for counting queries. We also discuss how these methods can be outsourced to a quantum server to blindly compute counting queries with differential privacy.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates differential privacy for counting queries on quantum-encoded datasets, analyzing two amplitude measurement techniques and showing privacy improvements and outsourcing possibilities.
AI.motivation: With differential privacy becoming the standard for privacy-preserving data analysis and growing interest in quantum privacy, there's a need to understand how to answer basic counting queries on quantum datasets while maintaining differential privacy guarantees.
AI.method: The authors show that answering counting queries on quantum datasets reduces to measuring amplitudes of orthogonal states. They analyze two amplitude measurement techniques: (1) repeated measurements in computational basis, and (2) amplitude estimation algorithm. For each, they derive privacy properties, including privacy amplification for the first method and tight global sensitivity bounds for the second.
AI.result: For repeated measurements, they prove improved privacy results for counting queries compared to general queries, showing privacy amplification due to inherent randomness. For amplitude estimation, they derive tight global sensitivity bounds and present a differentially private version of the algorithm. They also discuss outsourcing these methods to quantum servers.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes foundational results for differentially private counting queries on quantum datasets, demonstrating privacy improvements for basic measurement techniques and providing a framework for private amplitude estimation, with implications for quantum server outsourcing of privacy-preserving computations.

title: The non-local Hong-Ou-Mandel effect
summary: Two-photon interference effects arise because photons are indistinguishable particles. In the wellknown Hong-Ou-Mandel (HOM) effect, the transmission of two photons at a beam splitter interferes destructively with the reflection of both photons, requiring both photons to "bunch up" by leaving the beam splitter on the same side. Here, we show that the interference between locally propagating photons and photons exchanged by a mode swap can be implemented by post-selecting spatially separated photon outputs of a four-path interferometer. Even though the photons detected at spatially separated locations must have travelled along paths that never met up at the same beam splitter, the Hong-Ou-Mandel effect can be observed in correlations between the output ports that originate from the association of detection events with non-local output modes defined by the two single photon inputs. Local phase shifts can be used to map out non-classical correlations between the photons detected at different output locations, clarifying the role of linear optics in generating entanglement between spatially separated photons. Our work thus establishes a fundamental relation between multiphoton interference and entanglement, opening the door to new possibilities in optical quantum technologies.

AI.tldr: The paper demonstrates that Hong-Ou-Mandel interference can be observed between spatially separated photons using a four-path interferometer with post-selection, revealing a fundamental relation between multiphoton interference and entanglement.
AI.motivation: To explore the relationship between multiphoton interference and entanglement, specifically investigating whether Hong-Ou-Mandel effects can be observed between photons that never physically meet at a beam splitter but are instead correlated through non-local output modes.
AI.method: A four-path interferometer setup with post-selection of spatially separated photon outputs, using local phase shifts to map non-classical correlations between photons detected at different output locations.
AI.result: Demonstrated that Hong-Ou-Mandel interference can be observed between photons detected at spatially separated locations, even though their paths never intersected at a beam splitter, revealing non-classical correlations and entanglement generation through linear optics.
AI.conclusion: Established a fundamental relation between multiphoton interference and entanglement, opening new possibilities for optical quantum technologies by showing that entanglement can be generated between spatially separated photons through interference effects in linear optical systems.

title: Quantum Measurement Statistics as Bayesian Uncertainty Estimators for Physics-Constrained Learning
comment: 14 pages, 6 figures, 5 tables. Code available at https://github.com/nimanpra/quantum_state_uq
summary: Uncertainty quantification (UQ) is essential for deploying machine learning models in safety-critical physical systems, yet classical Bayesian approaches incur substantial computational overhead. We establish a formal connection between Born-rule measurement statistics from variational quantum circuits (VQCs) and Bayesian posterior uncertainty, proving that repeated quantum measurements naturally produce calibrated prediction intervals without requiring explicit Bayesian neural network (BNN) machinery. We demonstrate this framework on physics-constrained VQCs trained on PDE residuals. Systematic experiments comparing quantum shot-based UQ against MC Dropout and Deep Ensemble baselines show that quantum UQ achieves coverage probabilities within 1-3% of target confidence levels at N >= 5000 shots, while MC Dropout systematically over-covers by 4-5%. Physics-constrained circuits reduce the expected calibration error (ECE) by 34-40% compared to unconstrained counterparts, with interval widths 14-30% narrower at equivalent coverage. Information-theoretic analysis reveals that quantum circuits extract ~15% more bits of UQ information per evaluation than MC Dropout and ~42% more than Deep Ensembles (M = 10), owing to the exponential Hilbert space accessible through Born-rule sampling. These results establish quantum measurement statistics as a principled, computationally efficient framework for uncertainty quantification in physics-informed learning.

AI.tldr: Quantum measurement statistics from variational quantum circuits provide calibrated prediction intervals for uncertainty quantification, outperforming classical Bayesian methods in computational efficiency and information content.
AI.motivation: Classical Bayesian approaches for uncertainty quantification in safety-critical physical systems incur substantial computational overhead, creating a need for more efficient alternatives that maintain calibration accuracy.
AI.method: Established formal connection between Born-rule measurement statistics from variational quantum circuits and Bayesian posterior uncertainty. Used physics-constrained VQCs trained on PDE residuals, with quantum shot-based UQ compared against MC Dropout and Deep Ensemble baselines.
AI.result: Quantum UQ achieves coverage probabilities within 1-3% of target confidence levels at N≥5000 shots, while MC Dropout systematically over-covers by 4-5%. Physics-constrained circuits reduce ECE by 34-40% and produce 14-30% narrower intervals at equivalent coverage. Quantum circuits extract ~15% more bits of UQ information than MC Dropout and ~42% more than Deep Ensembles.
AI.conclusion: Quantum measurement statistics provide a principled, computationally efficient framework for uncertainty quantification in physics-informed learning, leveraging the exponential Hilbert space accessible through Born-rule sampling.

title: Compiler Framework for Directional Transport in Zoned Neutral Atom Systems with AOD Assistance: A Hybrid Remote CZ Approach
summary: We present a directional-transport (DT)-based remote CZ gate and compiler for zoned neutral-atom arrays that overcomes movement-bound entanglement limitations. Current AOD-based shuttling faces row/column non-crossing constraints, device-speed limits, and hardware-restricted range - bottlenecks for long-distance connectivity. Our approach reserves AODs for channel setup and micro-tuning while making DT the default for remote entanglement. Under antiblockade, a detuning-modulated pi-pulse sequence drives directional transport of a Rydberg excitation along a dynamic and resettable ancilla corridor, realizing a CZ gate between stationary, non-adjacent qubits. This cuts entangling-stage duration by approximately 50 to 90 percent versus AOD-only baselines and enables long-distance connectivity beyond objective-limited shuttling.

AI.tldr: A directional-transport-based remote CZ gate and compiler for neutral-atom arrays that uses Rydberg excitation transport along ancilla corridors to overcome AOD shuttling limitations, achieving 50-90% faster entanglement and long-distance connectivity.
AI.motivation: Current AOD-based shuttling for neutral-atom arrays faces fundamental limitations: row/column non-crossing constraints, device-speed limits, and hardware-restricted range. These bottlenecks prevent efficient long-distance connectivity and remote entanglement between stationary, non-adjacent qubits.
AI.method: The approach reserves AODs for channel setup and micro-tuning while making directional transport (DT) the default for remote entanglement. Under antiblockade conditions, a detuning-modulated pi-pulse sequence drives directional transport of a Rydberg excitation along a dynamic and resettable ancilla corridor, realizing a CZ gate between stationary qubits.
AI.result: The method cuts entangling-stage duration by approximately 50-90% compared to AOD-only baselines and enables long-distance connectivity beyond objective-limited shuttling constraints.
AI.conclusion: The directional-transport-based approach overcomes movement-bound entanglement limitations in neutral-atom arrays, providing faster remote CZ gates and enabling scalable long-distance connectivity that surpasses conventional AOD shuttling constraints.

title: QuMod: Parallel Quantum Job Scheduling on Modular QPUs using Circuit Cutting
summary: The quantum computing community is increasingly positioning quantum processors as accelerators within classical HPC workflows, analogous to GPUs and TPUs. However, many real-world applications require scaling to hundreds or thousands of physical qubits to realize logical qubits via error correction. To reach these scales, hardware vendors employing diverse technologies -- such as trapped ions, photonics, neutral atoms, and superconducting circuits -- are moving beyond single, monolithic QPUs toward modular architectures connected via interconnects. For example, IonQ has proposed photonic links for scaling, while IBM has demonstrated a modular QPU architecture by classically linking two 127-qubit devices. Using dynamic circuits, Bell-pair-based teleportation, and circuit cutting, they have shown how to execute a large quantum circuit that cannot fit on a single QPU. As interest in quantum computing grows, cloud providers must ensure fair and efficient resource allocation for multiple users sharing such modular systems. Classical interconnection of QPUs introduces new scheduling challenges, particularly when multiple jobs execute in parallel. In this work, we develop a multi-programmable scheduler for modular quantum systems that jointly considers qubit mapping, parallel circuit execution, measurement synchronization across subcircuits, and teleportation operations between QPUs using dynamic circuits.

AI.tldr: A multi-programmable scheduler for modular quantum systems that addresses qubit mapping, parallel circuit execution, measurement synchronization, and teleportation operations between interconnected QPUs.
AI.motivation: As quantum computing scales toward hundreds/thousands of qubits via modular architectures with interconnects, cloud providers need fair and efficient resource allocation for multiple users sharing such systems. Classical interconnection of QPUs introduces new scheduling challenges when multiple jobs execute in parallel.
AI.method: Developed a multi-programmable scheduler for modular quantum systems that jointly considers: qubit mapping, parallel circuit execution, measurement synchronization across subcircuits, and teleportation operations between QPUs using dynamic circuits.
AI.result: Not specified in the abstract, but the work presents a scheduler designed to handle the complexities of modular quantum systems with interconnected QPUs.
AI.conclusion: A scheduling solution is needed for modular quantum architectures to enable fair and efficient resource allocation as quantum computing scales beyond single monolithic QPUs toward interconnected modular systems.

title: SPATE: Spiking-Phase Adaptive Temporal Encoding for Quantum Machine Learning
summary: Most quantum machine learning (QML) pipelines still rely on static encodings such as angle and amplitude maps, and this limits their ability to handle temporal information. To address this limitation, this paper uses spike-based data representation as an effective encoding mechanism that incorporates temporal structure into quantum feature preparation. Specifically, we propose Spiking-Phase Adaptive Temporal Encoding (SPATE), a novel spike-driven temporal encoding method that converts real-valued tabular features into leaky integrate-and-fire spike trains and maps spike statistics to quantum rotations, augmented with a small set of temporal qubits through controlled phase operations. An encoding-centric evaluation protocol is also introduced to assess representation quality independently of the classifier, covering centered kernel-target alignment (CKTA), Fisher-style separability, inter/intra-class distance ratios, silhouette score, normalized entropy, and pairwise total-variation (TVpair) collapse indicators. Under stratified cross-validation, SPATE yields stronger representations across multiple datasets. For example, SPATE reaches a CKTA of 0.966 and a Fisher score of 7.37 on Blobs, compared with a CKTA of 0.632 and a Fisher score of 0.70 using angle encoding, and achieves a CKTA of 0.506 on Moons, compared with 0.015 using angle or amplitude encoding. These gains translate into stronger hybrid quantum neural network performance within a fixed qubit budget across several tasks, including an accuracy of 0.826 and an AUC of 0.978 for Wine, as well as an accuracy of 0.840 and an AUC of 0.923 for Moons. These results demonstrate that SPATE provides a practical spike-to-phase interface for building more informative quantum feature representations under constrained resources.

AI.tldr: SPATE is a spike-driven temporal encoding method that converts tabular features into spike trains and maps them to quantum rotations, enabling better temporal information processing in quantum machine learning pipelines.
AI.motivation: Current quantum machine learning pipelines rely on static encodings (angle/amplitude maps) which limit their ability to handle temporal information. There's a need for encoding mechanisms that incorporate temporal structure into quantum feature preparation.
AI.method: Proposes Spiking-Phase Adaptive Temporal Encoding (SPATE): converts real-valued tabular features into leaky integrate-and-fire spike trains, maps spike statistics to quantum rotations, and augments with temporal qubits through controlled phase operations. Also introduces an encoding-centric evaluation protocol to assess representation quality independently of classifiers.
AI.result: SPATE yields stronger representations across multiple datasets: achieves CKTA of 0.966 and Fisher score of 7.37 on Blobs (vs. 0.632 and 0.70 with angle encoding), and CKTA of 0.506 on Moons (vs. 0.015 with angle/amplitude encoding). Leads to improved hybrid quantum neural network performance: accuracy of 0.826 and AUC of 0.978 for Wine, accuracy of 0.840 and AUC of 0.923 for Moons.
AI.conclusion: SPATE provides a practical spike-to-phase interface for building more informative quantum feature representations under constrained resources, demonstrating that spike-based data representation effectively incorporates temporal structure into quantum machine learning pipelines.

title: Automorphism-Induced Entanglement Bounds in Many-Body Systems
summary: We derive an upper bound on the maximum balanced bipartite entanglement entropy of ground states of many-body Hamiltonians defined on a graph, agnostic to any particular model, that possesses a nontrivial automorphism group. We show that the entropy is bounded by the logarithm of a weighted sum of multiplicities of irreducible representations of the bipartition-preserving automorphism subgroup. This bound complements the known degeneracy-based bound, with neither universally dominating the other. For the complete graph $K_n$, the new bound yields an exponential improvement from linear to logarithmic scaling in the system size, consistent with the exact value of the entropy.

AI.tldr: Upper bound on bipartite entanglement entropy for graph Hamiltonians with automorphism groups, based on representation theory rather than degeneracy.
AI.motivation: Existing bounds on entanglement entropy often rely on degeneracy arguments. The authors seek a complementary bound using symmetry properties (automorphism groups) that can provide better scaling in certain cases.
AI.method: Derive an upper bound on bipartite entanglement entropy using representation theory of the bipartition-preserving automorphism subgroup. The bound involves logarithm of weighted sum of irreducible representation multiplicities.
AI.result: For complete graphs K_n, the new bound yields exponential improvement from linear to logarithmic scaling in system size, matching exact entropy values. The bound complements degeneracy-based bounds, with neither universally dominating.
AI.conclusion: Symmetry-based bounds using automorphism groups provide powerful alternative to degeneracy-based bounds for entanglement entropy, particularly effective for highly symmetric graphs like complete graphs.

title: Schrödinger-Navier-Stokes Equation for the Quantum Simulation of Navier-Stokes Flows
summary:
The search for quantum-like wave formulations of the Navier-Stokes (Schrödinger-Navier-Stokes, SNS for short) equations describing classical dissipative fluids has met with increasing attention in the recent years, due to the large portfolio of potential applications in science and engineering. A SNS formulation of classical fluids was first presented in a largely un-noticed paper by Dietrich and Vautherin back in 1985(Journal de Physique). In this paper, we revisit this specific SNS approach and assess its viability for quantum implementations based on Carleman embedding/linearization techniques. Specifically, we i) Clarify in full mathematical detail why the SNS dissipator presents a steep challenge for quantum computers and propose a way out strategy based on the Hamilton-Jacobi (HJ) formulation of fluid dynamics; ii) Develop a corresponding quantum algorithm using a new technique based on a tensor-network representation of Carleman embedding of the HJ equations (CHJ) which permits substantial memory savings; iii) Emulate the CHJ quantum algorithm on a classical computer and analyse its convergence and accuracy for the specific case of Kolmogorov-like flows at moderate Reynolds numbers.
  To the best of our knowledge, this is the first quantum algorithm based on a quantum-like wave formulation of the genuine Navier-Stokes equations, including pressure, dissipation and vorticity.

AI.tldr: Quantum algorithm development for Navier-Stokes equations using Schrödinger-Navier-Stokes formulation and Carleman embedding with Hamilton-Jacobi approach for quantum implementation.
AI.motivation: The search for quantum-like wave formulations of Navier-Stokes equations has gained attention due to potential applications in science and engineering. The paper revisits a 1985 SNS approach to assess its viability for quantum implementations using Carleman embedding techniques.
AI.method: 1) Clarify mathematical challenges of SNS dissipator for quantum computers and propose Hamilton-Jacobi formulation as solution; 2) Develop quantum algorithm using tensor-network representation of Carleman embedding of HJ equations (CHJ) for memory savings; 3) Emulate CHJ quantum algorithm classically and analyze convergence/accuracy for Kolmogorov-like flows at moderate Reynolds numbers.
AI.result: First quantum algorithm based on quantum-like wave formulation of genuine Navier-Stokes equations including pressure, dissipation and vorticity. The CHJ approach enables quantum implementation through Carleman linearization with tensor-network representation for computational efficiency.
AI.conclusion: The paper presents a viable pathway for quantum computing of classical fluid dynamics by combining SNS formulation with Hamilton-Jacobi approach and Carleman embedding, demonstrating potential for quantum advantage in fluid simulations through novel tensor-network techniques.

title: Autonomous Quantum Error Correction of Spin-Oscillator Hybrid Qubits
summary: We propose a novel measurement-free scheme for stabilizing a spin-oscillator hybrid qubit via autonomous quantum error correction. The engineered Lindbladian renders the code space into an attractive steady-state subspace, realized by coupling the storage mode to a rapidly cooled bath through a controlled beam-splitter and spin-dependent displacement interactions. The continuous variable-discrete variable hybrid approach to autonomous quantum error correction preserves the hardware efficiency of conventional dissipation engineering while simplifying the required system-bath coupling. The construction is compatible with simple logical gates and leverages primitives already demonstrated in experimental platforms, such as trapped-ion systems, suggesting a practical route to hardware-efficient, noise-biased logical qubits without repeated syndrome measurements and feedforward.

AI.tldr: A measurement-free scheme for stabilizing spin-oscillator hybrid qubits via autonomous quantum error correction using engineered dissipation to create noise-biased logical qubits without syndrome measurements.
AI.motivation: To develop hardware-efficient quantum error correction that avoids the overhead of repeated syndrome measurements and feedforward operations, which are resource-intensive in conventional quantum error correction schemes.
AI.method: Engineered Lindbladian dynamics that renders the code space into an attractive steady-state subspace, achieved by coupling the storage mode to a rapidly cooled bath through controlled beam-splitter and spin-dependent displacement interactions in a continuous variable-discrete variable hybrid approach.
AI.result: A practical autonomous quantum error correction scheme that preserves hardware efficiency while simplifying system-bath coupling requirements, compatible with simple logical gates and leveraging experimental primitives demonstrated in platforms like trapped-ion systems.
AI.conclusion: The proposed scheme offers a practical route to hardware-efficient, noise-biased logical qubits without the need for repeated syndrome measurements and feedforward, potentially advancing fault-tolerant quantum computing with reduced experimental complexity.

title: Cross-Sensor RGB Spectrograms: A Visual Method for Anomaly Detection in Classical and Quantum Magnetometer Triads
summary: Stationary multi-magnetometer arrays are routinely deployed in geomagnetic observatories, laboratory shielded rooms, and ground-based monitoring stations. The standard analysis pipeline reduces each sensor to an independent power spectrum, discarding any inter-sensor structure that is itself diagnostic of measurement health and of localised magnetic activity. This paper develops a purely theoretical framework for a deliberately simple visualisation that maps the short-time Fourier (STFT) power spectra of three concurrent magnetometers into the red, green, and blue channels of a single image: the \emph{cross-sensor RGB spectrogram}. Inter-sensor coherence appears as neutral grey or white, while spectral energy that is unique to one or two sensors stands out as saturated colour. We formalise the construction of the image, derive its time-frequency resolution properties, give an explicit account of the per-channel normalisation choice, and present a colour-anomaly taxonomy that distinguishes coherent broadband activity, single-sensor faults, asymmetric pairwise sources, and slow temporal drift. A companion long-window variant is described for resolving features in the ultra-low frequency (ULF) band. The construction is presented without reference to any particular dataset or implementation; it is intended as a self-contained methodological building block that can be inserted into any monitoring pipeline whose front end is a synchronously sampled magnetometer triad. Because the construction operates on scalar magnitude time series alone, it applies equally to classical fluxgate sensors and to quantum magnetometers -- optically pumped magnetometers (OPMs), nitrogen-vacancy (NV) centre arrays, and superconducting quantum interference devices (SQUIDs) -- where distinguishing quantum-limited noise from technical artefacts is a central diagnostic challenge.

AI.tldr: A theoretical framework for visualizing three concurrent magnetometer spectra as RGB images to reveal inter-sensor coherence and anomalies.
AI.motivation: Standard magnetometer analysis discards inter-sensor structure that is diagnostic of measurement health and localized magnetic activity. Current methods reduce each sensor to independent power spectra, losing valuable diagnostic information about sensor coherence and anomalies.
AI.method: Develops a cross-sensor RGB spectrogram that maps STFT power spectra of three concurrent magnetometers into RGB channels. Formalizes image construction, derives time-frequency resolution properties, specifies per-channel normalization, and creates a color-anomaly taxonomy. Includes a long-window variant for ULF band features.
AI.result: Creates a visualization where inter-sensor coherence appears as neutral grey/white, while unique spectral energy appears as saturated color. Enables distinction between coherent broadband activity, single-sensor faults, asymmetric pairwise sources, and slow temporal drift.
AI.conclusion: Provides a self-contained methodological building block for any monitoring pipeline with synchronously sampled magnetometer triads. Works with both classical fluxgate and quantum magnetometers (OPMs, NV centers, SQUIDs), addressing the challenge of distinguishing quantum-limited noise from technical artifacts.

title: Low-dose Image Recognition with Quantum Computational Electron Microscopy
comment: 4 pages, 1 figure. Supersedes the unpublished preliminary version in arXiv:2209.04819
summary: We show that quantum computational imaging is advantageous in the setting of low-dose electron microscopy of beam-sensitive specimens. Two qudits placed near the electron beam enable full transfer of quantum information between the electron microscope and a quantum computer in the proposed scheme, providing the specimen is a phase object. We present a quantum algorithm that identifies the correct image among n candidate images, where n is larger than the effective dimension of the Hilbert space of the imaging electron.

AI.tldr: Quantum computational imaging offers advantages for low-dose electron microscopy of beam-sensitive specimens using quantum information transfer and phase object imaging.
AI.motivation: To address the challenge of imaging beam-sensitive specimens in electron microscopy while minimizing electron dose, which can damage delicate samples.
AI.method: Uses two qudits placed near the electron beam to enable full quantum information transfer between electron microscope and quantum computer for phase objects, with a quantum algorithm that identifies correct images among n candidates.
AI.result: Demonstrates quantum advantage in low-dose electron microscopy, enabling identification of correct images even when n exceeds the effective dimension of the imaging electron's Hilbert space.
AI.conclusion: Quantum computational imaging provides significant advantages for beam-sensitive specimen imaging in electron microscopy through quantum information transfer and quantum algorithms.

title: Engineered non-Gaussian Coherence as a Thermodynamic Resource for Quantum Batteries
comment: The main text consists of 7 pages and includes 3 figures. The supplemental material comprises 4 pages and contains 4 figures
summary: Accessing quantum advantage (QA) is a legitimate task in energy harvesting devices, and it is potentially reshaping thermodynamic concepts. In this respect, the resourceful quantum non-Gaussian (QNG) states are promising candidates that precisely enable universal quantum operations to enhance thermodynamic performance with capabilities beyond what Gaussian states can achieve. We recently proposed [K. Adhikary, D. W. Moore, and R. Filip, {\em Quantum Sci. Technol.} \textbf{10}, 035048 (2025)] the QNG state generation scheme, which serves as the framework for this study and is directly integrated into the battery setting to figure out QA. By leveraging coherence in the engineered QNG states, we aim to optimize the performance of quantum batteries for various Gaussian charger profiles under unitary dynamics. We further exploit the degree of thermal broadening and environmental coupling to the charger, which is capable of fostering stable performance under precise thermal management. This study provides a proof-of-concept for exploiting thermodynamic resources in quantum energy storage units.

AI.tldr: QNG states enable quantum advantage in quantum batteries by enhancing thermodynamic performance beyond Gaussian states under various charger profiles and thermal conditions.
AI.motivation: To achieve quantum advantage in energy harvesting devices by leveraging quantum non-Gaussian states to enhance thermodynamic performance beyond what Gaussian states can achieve, particularly in quantum battery applications.
AI.method: Integrates previously proposed QNG state generation scheme into battery setting, leverages coherence in engineered QNG states to optimize quantum battery performance under various Gaussian charger profiles with unitary dynamics, and exploits thermal broadening and environmental coupling for stable performance under thermal management.
AI.result: Demonstrates quantum advantage in quantum batteries through QNG states, shows optimization of battery performance across different Gaussian charger profiles, and achieves stable performance under controlled thermal conditions.
AI.conclusion: Provides proof-of-concept for exploiting thermodynamic resources in quantum energy storage units using QNG states, establishing framework for quantum advantage in quantum batteries with practical thermal management considerations.

title: Fidelity-informed neural pulse compilation of a continuous family of quantum gates with uncertainty-margin analysis
summary: We develop a fidelity-informed neural pulse-compilation framework for a continuous family of single-qubit gates on a three-qubit liquid-state nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) processor. Instead of decomposing each target unitary into a sequence of calibrated basis gates, the method learns a direct map from the axis-angle parameters of an arbitrary U_2 in SU(2) operation to a piecewise-constant radio-frequency control sequence that implements the desired transformation. Training is performed end-to-end through the time-ordered propagator of the driven Hamiltonian using global-phase-insensitive unitary fidelity as the learning signal. We show numerically that a single model generalizes across a continuous range of gate parameters and experimentally validate representative compiled pulses on a benchtop three-qubit NMR device. In addition, we analyze sensitivity to structured perturbations in Hamiltonian and control parameters by introducing a prescribed uncertainty set and performing a comparative risk-aware redesign based on right-tail Conditional Value-at-Risk (RU-CVaR). This stage produces pulse solutions with broader tolerance margins within the chosen uncertainty model. The results demonstrate continuous pulse-level gate synthesis in an experimentally accessible setting and illustrate a hardware-aware compilation strategy that can be extended to other quantum platforms. While the uncertainty model considered here is tailored to NMR, the neural compilation and risk-aware optimization framework are general and may be useful in architectures where calibration overhead, parameter drift, or control constraints make repeated per-gate optimization costly.

AI.tldr: Neural pulse compilation framework for continuous single-qubit gates on NMR quantum processor using direct mapping from axis-angle parameters to control sequences, with risk-aware optimization for robustness.
AI.motivation: To overcome limitations of traditional gate decomposition methods and enable continuous gate synthesis while addressing hardware-specific uncertainties and calibration overhead in quantum control.
AI.method: End-to-end neural network learns direct map from SU(2) axis-angle parameters to piecewise-constant RF control sequences, trained via time-ordered propagator with phase-insensitive unitary fidelity. Risk-aware redesign uses RU-CVaR optimization for robustness against Hamiltonian and control parameter uncertainties.
AI.result: Single model generalizes across continuous gate parameter range, validated experimentally on 3-qubit NMR device. Risk-aware optimization produces pulses with broader tolerance margins against structured perturbations.
AI.conclusion: Demonstrates continuous pulse-level gate synthesis in experimental setting with hardware-aware compilation strategy. Framework is generalizable to other quantum platforms facing calibration overhead, parameter drift, or control constraints.

title: Optimal Two-Qubit Gates for Group-IV Color-Centers in Diamond
summary: Color centers associated with group-IV dopants in diamond with long-lived nuclear spins have emerged as major candidates for distributed quantum computing nodes and quantum repeaters. Several proof-of-principle experiments have already been demonstrated. A key operation for long-distance entanglement-distribution protocols are fast and robust gates between the electron spin and a nuclear spin. Here, we investigate numerically for an existing experimental platform of a Germanium-vacancy (GeV) center with a strongly-coupled ${}^{13}$C spin, how such gates can be implemented via quantum optimal control. In the presence of realistic noise we investigate different parameter regimes and gate operations and obtain robust two-qubit gates with fidelities exceeding $99.9 \%$. The framework provides a scalable strategy for group-IV quantum nodes and can be adapted to related architectures.

AI.tldr: Quantum optimal control enables high-fidelity (>99.9%) electron-nuclear spin gates for GeV diamond centers with strongly-coupled 13C spins, advancing distributed quantum computing nodes.
AI.motivation: Group-IV dopants in diamond with long-lived nuclear spins are promising for distributed quantum computing and quantum repeaters. A key requirement is fast, robust gates between electron and nuclear spins for long-distance entanglement distribution.
AI.method: Numerical investigation using quantum optimal control for an existing GeV center platform with strongly-coupled 13C spin. Analysis of different parameter regimes and gate operations under realistic noise conditions.
AI.result: Achieved robust two-qubit gates with fidelities exceeding 99.9%. The framework provides scalable strategy for group-IV quantum nodes and can be adapted to related architectures.
AI.conclusion: Quantum optimal control enables high-performance electron-nuclear spin gates for diamond-based quantum nodes, advancing practical implementation of distributed quantum computing and quantum repeater networks.

title: Quantum Sensing with Joint Emitter-Fluorescence Measurements
summary: We present an analytically tractable model of a driven quantum harmonic emitter, such as an oscillating charged dipole, emitting radiation via resonance fluorescence. With this model we are able to characterize the quantum mechanical correlations that are built up at early times between the drive, the resonant emitter, and its fluorescence. We describe detection strategies that can reveal these quantum signatures in experiments by performing joint measurements on the quantum emitter and its fluorescence field. In particular, we show that simultaneous quantum measurements of a driven quantum emitter and its fluorescence can be used to probe the quantum noise of the driving field, relative to the maximally classical coherent state of the driving field, in short-time experiments. We conclude by discussing the applications to quantum sensing in quantum optical, quantum acoustic, and quantum gravitational scenarios of interest.

AI.tldr: Analytical model for driven quantum harmonic emitter reveals early-time quantum correlations between drive, emitter, and fluorescence, enabling quantum noise sensing via joint measurements.
AI.motivation: To understand and characterize quantum mechanical correlations that develop at early times between a driven quantum harmonic emitter (like an oscillating charged dipole), the driving field, and the emitted fluorescence radiation, which has applications in quantum sensing across various domains.
AI.method: Developed an analytically tractable model of a driven quantum harmonic emitter undergoing resonance fluorescence. Proposed detection strategies involving joint quantum measurements on both the emitter and its fluorescence field to reveal quantum signatures.
AI.result: The model shows that simultaneous quantum measurements of a driven quantum emitter and its fluorescence can probe the quantum noise of the driving field relative to coherent states. This enables characterization of quantum correlations built up at early times between drive, emitter, and fluorescence.
AI.conclusion: The approach provides a framework for quantum sensing applications in quantum optical, quantum acoustic, and quantum gravitational scenarios by exploiting quantum correlations in driven quantum harmonic emitters.

title: From GDSII to Wafer: EDA Design Flow and Data Conversion for Wafer-Scale Manufacturing of Superconducting Quantum Chips
summary: Superconducting quantum computing is advancing toward the thousand- and even million-qubit regime, making wafer-scale fabrication an essential pathway for achieving large-scale, cost-effective quantum processors. This manufacturing paradigm imposes new requirements on quantum-chip electronic design automation (Q-EDA): design tools must not only generate layouts (GDSII files) that satisfy quantum-circuit physical constraints but also ensure that the design data can be seamlessly converted into a complete set of manufacturing files executable by a wafer foundry, thereby enabling reliable translation from design intent to physical chip. This paper focuses on this critical data-conversion pipeline and presents a systematic treatment of the Q-EDA technology stack for wafer-scale fabrication. Starting from GDSII as the single authoritative data source, we analyze the key stages including process-design-kit (PDK)-based design rule checking (DRC), layout-versus-schematic (LVS) verification, design for manufacturability (DFM) optimization, wafer layout planning, and mask data preparation (MDP). We describe the concrete architecture of a Q-EDA system, present nine quantum-specific DRC rules together with their physical underpinnings and a multi-layer process stack model, and benchmark the manufacturing data-flow coverage of mainstream Q-EDA tools. Finally, we discuss the core challenges and future directions in this field.

AI.tldr: This paper presents a systematic Q-EDA technology stack for wafer-scale superconducting quantum processor fabrication, focusing on the critical data-conversion pipeline from GDSII to manufacturing files.
AI.motivation: As superconducting quantum computing advances to thousand- and million-qubit scales, wafer-scale fabrication becomes essential. This requires Q-EDA tools that not only generate layouts but also ensure seamless conversion to complete manufacturing files for reliable design-to-chip translation.
AI.method: The paper analyzes the Q-EDA technology stack starting from GDSII as the single authoritative data source. It covers key stages: PDK-based design rule checking (DRC), layout-versus-schematic (LVS) verification, design for manufacturability (DFM) optimization, wafer layout planning, and mask data preparation (MDP). The authors present a concrete Q-EDA system architecture, nine quantum-specific DRC rules with physical underpinnings, a multi-layer process stack model, and benchmark manufacturing data-flow coverage of mainstream Q-EDA tools.
AI.result: The paper provides a systematic framework for Q-EDA in wafer-scale fabrication, including specific quantum DRC rules and process models. It benchmarks existing tools' manufacturing data-flow coverage and identifies gaps in current Q-EDA capabilities.
AI.conclusion: The paper establishes a comprehensive Q-EDA technology stack for wafer-scale quantum processor fabrication and discusses core challenges and future directions in the field, emphasizing the need for robust design-to-manufacturing data conversion pipelines.

title: When T-Depth Misleads: Predicting Fault-Tolerant Quantum Execution Slowdown under Magic-State Delivery Constraints
comment: 10 pages, 10 figures. Code available at https://github.com/C2-Q/BARC/
summary: The efficient execution of fault-tolerant quantum algorithms is fundamentally limited by the production rate of magic states required for non-Clifford operations. While circuit optimization typically targets T-depth, static T-depth does not reliably predict executable performance under bounded T-state delivery. We introduce a model that captures demand-supply imbalance using two key quantities: slack ratio, a structural indicator of scheduling flexibility, and Delta_max, a measure of cumulative demand surplus. We show that Delta_max is a strong schedule-level indicator of execution slowdown and yields a provable lower bound on executable makespan for a fixed schedule. Empirical evaluation on constructed directed acyclic graph (DAG) families, with arithmetic circuits and exact quantum Fourier transform (QFT) traces providing additional grounding, shows that slack ratio is a stronger structural predictor than T-depth for stall and inversion risk, while Delta_max is the strongest predictor of slowdown. Across 4,904 instances, the lower bound shows zero violations, with 88.9% of cases within one cycle. These results highlight the importance of explicitly modeling delivery constraints in fault-tolerant quantum compilation.

AI.tldr: Paper introduces a model using slack ratio and Δ_max to predict execution slowdown in fault-tolerant quantum algorithms under bounded T-state delivery constraints, showing these metrics outperform T-depth for predicting stall and inversion risks.
AI.motivation: Fault-tolerant quantum algorithms are limited by magic state production rates for non-Clifford operations, and traditional circuit optimization focusing on T-depth fails to predict executable performance under bounded T-state delivery constraints.
AI.method: Introduces a demand-supply imbalance model with two key quantities: slack ratio (structural indicator of scheduling flexibility) and Δ_max (measure of cumulative demand surplus). Shows Δ_max provides a provable lower bound on executable makespan for fixed schedules.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation on constructed DAG families, arithmetic circuits, and exact QFT traces shows slack ratio is stronger than T-depth for predicting stall and inversion risk, while Δ_max is strongest predictor of slowdown. Across 4,904 instances, lower bound shows zero violations with 88.9% within one cycle.
AI.conclusion: Results highlight the importance of explicitly modeling delivery constraints in fault-tolerant quantum compilation, as traditional T-depth optimization fails to capture performance under bounded T-state delivery.

title: Topological Device-Independent Quantum Key Distribution Using Majorana-Based Qubits
summary: Device-independent quantum key distribution (DI-QKD) provides the highest level of cryptographic security by certifying secrecy through observed Bell inequality violations, independent of the internal device physics. However, the transition from theory to practice is obstructed by the dual challenge of closing the detection loophole and achieving viable key rates over fiber distances. In this paper, we present a comprehensive theoretical framework for DI-QKD implemented on topological Majorana Zero Mode (MZM) processors. While MZMs offer a native parity-readout basis that simplifies Bell-state measurement, their viability as QKD nodes is fundamentally constrained by the interplay between storage latency and quasiparticle poisoning. We bridge the gap between microscopic hardware noise and macroscopic security by: (i) developing a hardware-native error model that maps MZM-specific processes, including poisoning rates, braid infidelities, and readout anisotropy, directly to the CHSH Bell parameter $S$; (ii) introducing a loss-disciplined protocol that monitors setting-conditional efficiencies to strictly enforce detection-loophole closure in a heralded architecture; and (iii) providing a composable finite-size security proof based on the Entropy Accumulation Theorem (EAT). Our analysis reveals that while topological protection stabilizes the system against calibration drift, the achievable secure distance is strictly bounded by the poisoning-induced visibility collapse during the photonic round-trip time. We identify specific hardware thresholds, particularly the suppression of poisoning rates to $Γ_p τ_{\text{max}} \ll 1$ and high-fidelity sensor integration, as the critical path for viable topological quantum networks.

AI.tldr: Topological Majorana Zero Mode processors enable device-independent quantum key distribution with hardware-native error modeling, loss-disciplined protocols, and composable security proofs, but require suppression of quasiparticle poisoning rates for viable quantum networks.
AI.motivation: Device-independent QKD offers highest cryptographic security but faces practical challenges: detection loophole closure and viable key rates over fiber distances. Topological MZM processors provide native parity-readout basis for Bell-state measurements but face constraints from storage latency and quasiparticle poisoning.
AI.method: Three-part framework: (1) hardware-native error model mapping MZM-specific processes (poisoning rates, braid infidelities, readout anisotropy) to CHSH Bell parameter S; (2) loss-disciplined protocol monitoring setting-conditional efficiencies for detection-loophole closure in heralded architecture; (3) composable finite-size security proof using Entropy Accumulation Theorem.
AI.result: Topological protection stabilizes against calibration drift, but secure distance is bounded by poisoning-induced visibility collapse during photonic round-trip time. Critical hardware thresholds identified: suppression of poisoning rates to Γ_p τ_max ≪ 1 and high-fidelity sensor integration.
AI.conclusion: MZM processors enable DI-QKD with theoretical framework bridging microscopic hardware noise to macroscopic security, but practical implementation requires specific hardware improvements, particularly poisoning rate suppression and sensor integration, for viable topological quantum networks.

title: Unfair Sampling of Quantum Annealing in Weighted Graph Bipartitioning Problems
summary: Quantum annealing (QA) is a promising approach for solving combinatorial optimization problems; however, it is known to exhibit unfair sampling, in which degenerate ground states are not sampled with equal probability even for sufficiently long annealing times. Fair sampling is important in applications such as solution diversity assessment and combinatorial counting, yet the mechanism of unfair sampling remains poorly understood, particularly in constrained combinatorial optimization problems. In this work, we investigate unfair sampling of QA in weighted graph bipartitioning problems (GBP), a representative constrained optimization problem. We study how the penalty coefficient in the penalty method affects sampling fairness. Through numerical simulations and experiments on the D-Wave Advantage2 system, we show that increasing the penalty coefficient reduces unfair sampling in a representative single instance, and that this qualitative behavior is also observed on actual hardware. A scaling analysis over randomly generated instances with up to 12 spins reveals that, while this trend does not hold universally, more than 70% of instances exhibit monotonically increasing sampling fairness as the penalty coefficient increases, even at the largest system size studied. These results show that increasing the penalty coefficient improves sampling fairness, though at the cost of ground-state probability under practical annealing conditions, and call for a deeper theoretical understanding of unfair sampling in constrained optimization problems.

AI.tldr: Increasing penalty coefficient in quantum annealing improves sampling fairness for graph bipartitioning problems, though with trade-off in ground-state probability.
AI.motivation: Quantum annealing exhibits unfair sampling where degenerate ground states are not sampled equally, which is problematic for applications requiring solution diversity assessment and combinatorial counting. The mechanism behind unfair sampling in constrained optimization problems remains poorly understood.
AI.method: Investigated unfair sampling in weighted graph bipartitioning problems using penalty method. Conducted numerical simulations and experiments on D-Wave Advantage2 system, studying how penalty coefficient affects sampling fairness. Performed scaling analysis over randomly generated instances with up to 12 spins.
AI.result: Increasing penalty coefficient reduces unfair sampling in representative instances, with hardware experiments confirming qualitative behavior. While not universal, >70% of instances show monotonically increasing sampling fairness with higher penalty coefficients, even at largest system size studied. However, this improvement comes at cost of reduced ground-state probability under practical annealing conditions.
