AI.conclusion: Higher penalty coefficients improve sampling fairness in constrained optimization problems like graph bipartitioning, but trade-off exists with ground-state probability. Results highlight need for deeper theoretical understanding of unfair sampling mechanisms in constrained optimization problems.

title: Accuracy-Cost Trade-offs for Reference VQE Calculations of H$_2$ on IBM Quantum Hardware
summary:
We present a hardware-validated reference dataset for variational ground-state energy calculations of the hydrogen molecule H\(_2\) on several IBM Quantum processors available in 2026. Using a standardized workflow, we benchmark the impact of shot count, backend choice, optimization strategy, and runtime variability on the achievable energy accuracy relative to exact diagonalization. The resulting dataset and analysis provide a transparent baseline for assessing the current capabilities and limitations of IBM Quantum hardware for quantum-chemistry applications, and are meant to ease the entry for new users by providing a comprehensive overview of choices and their effects as well as runtime efforts and costs that can be expected.
  Across the configurations studied here, circuit simplification through tapered mappings provides the most consistent accuracy gains, resilience level 1 improves accuracy at a substantial cost premium, and session-based execution yields no systematic accuracy advantage over single-job execution despite markedly higher billed time.

AI.tldr: Hardware-validated reference dataset for variational ground-state energy calculations of H₂ on IBM Quantum processors, benchmarking shot count, backend choice, optimization strategies, and runtime variability against exact diagonalization.
AI.motivation: To provide a transparent baseline for assessing current capabilities and limitations of IBM Quantum hardware for quantum-chemistry applications, and to ease entry for new users by offering comprehensive overview of choices, effects, runtime efforts, and costs.
AI.method: Standardized workflow for variational ground-state energy calculations of H₂ on several IBM Quantum processors, benchmarking impact of shot count, backend choice, optimization strategy, and runtime variability relative to exact diagonalization.
AI.result: Circuit simplification through tapered mappings provides most consistent accuracy gains; resilience level 1 improves accuracy at substantial cost premium; session-based execution yields no systematic accuracy advantage over single-job execution despite higher billed time.
AI.conclusion: The dataset and analysis establish a transparent baseline for assessing IBM Quantum hardware capabilities for quantum chemistry, revealing key trade-offs between accuracy improvements, costs, and execution strategies.

title: Arbitrary-Velocity Volkov Wavepackets
summary: The evolution of a charged lepton in the field of an electromagnetic plane wave can be described as a superposition of Volkov states. Here we demonstrate that imposing specific momentum correlations among Volkov states produces a spatiotemporally structured wavepacket whose probability-density peak travels at an arbitrary, tailored velocity. This velocity can be chosen independently of both the field amplitude and the velocity expectation value. The imposed momentum correlations modify the expectation-value trajectory, providing a measurable signature of the arbitrary velocity within a physical observable.

AI.tldr: Researchers create structured electron wavepackets with arbitrary, tailored velocities using correlated Volkov states in electromagnetic fields, independent of field amplitude and expectation values.
AI.motivation: To develop a method for controlling the propagation velocity of charged lepton wavepackets in electromagnetic fields beyond conventional constraints, enabling tailored spatiotemporal structuring independent of field parameters.
AI.method: Imposing specific momentum correlations among Volkov states (solutions for charged particles in plane waves) to construct spatiotemporally structured wavepackets with probability-density peaks traveling at arbitrary, tailored velocities.
AI.result: Demonstrated creation of wavepackets with arbitrary velocities that can be chosen independently of both field amplitude and velocity expectation value, with modified expectation-value trajectories providing measurable signatures of the arbitrary velocity.
AI.conclusion: Momentum correlations among Volkov states enable precise control over wavepacket propagation velocities in electromagnetic fields, offering new possibilities for tailored particle dynamics and measurable velocity signatures.

title: Tackling instabilities of quantum Krylov subspace methods: an analysis of the numerical and statistical errors
comment: 17 pages, 4 figures, followed by supplementary information
summary: Krylov subspace methods are among the most extensively studied early fault-tolerant quantum algorithms for estimating ground-state energies of quantum systems. However, the rapid onset of ill-conditioning might make accurate energies difficult or even impossible to retrieve. In this communication, we analyse the numerical stability and statistical problems of these methods using numerical simulations both in the presence and absence of sampling noise. While in ideal numerical simulations the generalized eigenvalue problem indeed becomes unstable with increased Krylov subspace size, we find that, in realistic noisy settings, these methods do not primarily suffer from ill-conditioning. Instead, statistical fluctuations dominate and can prevent reliable solution extraction unless appropriate regularization or filtering techniques are employed. We consequently introduce two new metrics, the imaginary and unitary filters, that successfully assess the reliability of the obtained solutions without any knowledge of the true eigenspectrum.

AI.tldr: Krylov subspace methods for quantum ground-state energy estimation face numerical instability in ideal settings but statistical noise dominates in realistic noisy environments, requiring regularization/filtering; new reliability metrics introduced.
AI.motivation: To analyze the numerical stability and statistical problems of Krylov subspace methods for quantum ground-state energy estimation, particularly understanding whether ill-conditioning or statistical noise is the primary limitation in realistic noisy quantum computing settings.
AI.method: Numerical simulations analyzing Krylov subspace methods both with and without sampling noise, examining the generalized eigenvalue problem stability, and introducing two new reliability assessment metrics: imaginary and unitary filters.
AI.result: In ideal noise-free simulations, the generalized eigenvalue problem becomes unstable with increased Krylov subspace size due to ill-conditioning. However, in realistic noisy settings, statistical fluctuations dominate over ill-conditioning and can prevent reliable solution extraction unless appropriate regularization/filtering techniques are used.
AI.conclusion: Krylov subspace methods for quantum ground-state energy estimation primarily suffer from statistical noise rather than ill-conditioning in practical noisy quantum computing environments, and the proposed imaginary and unitary filters provide effective reliability assessment without requiring knowledge of the true eigenspectrum.

title: Quantum circuit optimization for arbitrary high-dimensional bipartite quantum computation
summary: Implementation of high-dimensional (HD) quantum gates shows very promising perspectives for HD quantum computation. A bipartite quantum system with arbitrary dimensions $n$ and $m$ is termed a quNit-quMit. Here we propose a synthesis scheme to construct the quantum circuit for general quNit-quMit gates with controlled increment (CINC) gates and local gates. This shows that CINC gates combined with local gates form a universal gate set for HD quantum computation. An upper bound of $O(n^2)$ CINC gates is achieved for arbitrary quNit-quMit gate implementation in the proposed scheme, which is the best known result. Especially for the controlled quNit-quMit gates, our scheme requires only 2 CINC gates, whereas the previous scheme required $2n$.

AI.tldr: Proposes a synthesis scheme using controlled increment (CINC) gates and local gates to implement arbitrary quNit-quMit gates with O(n²) CINC gates, achieving the best known bound and requiring only 2 CINC gates for controlled gates.
AI.motivation: High-dimensional quantum gates are promising for HD quantum computation, but efficient implementation of general quNit-quMit gates (bipartite systems with dimensions n and m) needs improved synthesis methods with better resource bounds.
AI.method: Proposes a synthesis scheme using controlled increment (CINC) gates and local gates to construct quantum circuits for general quNit-quMit gates, proving CINC gates combined with local gates form a universal gate set for HD quantum computation.
AI.result: Achieves an upper bound of O(n²) CINC gates for arbitrary quNit-quMit gate implementation, which is the best known result. For controlled quNit-quMit gates, the scheme requires only 2 CINC gates compared to the previous 2n gates.
AI.conclusion: The proposed synthesis scheme demonstrates efficient implementation of high-dimensional quantum gates using CINC and local gates, establishing their universality and providing significant improvements in gate count for both general and controlled quNit-quMit gates.

title: A Systematic Study of Noise Effects in Hybrid Quantum-Classical Machine Learning
summary:
Near-term quantum machine learning (QML) models operate in environments wherein noise is unavoidable, arising from both imperfect classical data acquisition and the limitations of noisy intermediate-scale quantum (NISQ) hardware. Although most existing studies have focused primarily on quantum circuit noise in isolation, the combined influence of corrupted classical inputs and quantum hardware noise has received comparatively little attention.
  In this work, we present a systematic experimental study of the robustness of a variational quantum classifier under realistic multi-level noise conditions. Using the Titanic dataset as a benchmark, a range of dataset-level noise models-including speckle noise, impulse noise, quantization noise, and feature dropout are applied to classical features prior to quantum encoding using a ZZ feature map. In parallel, hardware-inspired quantum noise channels such as depolarizing noise, amplitude damping, phase damping, Pauli errors, and readout errors are incorporated at the circuit level using the Qiskit Aer simulator.
  The experimental results indicate that noise in classical input data can significantly intensify the effects of quantum decoherence, resulting in less stable training and noticeably lower classification accuracy. Together, these observations emphasize the importance of designing and evaluating quantum machine learning pipelines with noise in mind, and highlight the need to consider classical and quantum noise simultaneously when assessing QML performance in the NISQ era

AI.tldr: Study examines combined effects of classical dataset noise and quantum hardware noise on variational quantum classifiers, finding classical noise amplifies quantum decoherence effects and reduces classification accuracy.
AI.motivation: Most existing QML studies focus on quantum circuit noise in isolation, but real-world NISQ environments involve both corrupted classical inputs from imperfect data acquisition and quantum hardware noise. The combined influence of these multi-level noise sources has received little attention.
AI.method: Systematic experimental study using Titanic dataset benchmark with: 1) Dataset-level noise models (speckle noise, impulse noise, quantization noise, feature dropout) applied to classical features before quantum encoding via ZZ feature map; 2) Hardware-inspired quantum noise channels (depolarizing noise, amplitude damping, phase damping, Pauli errors, readout errors) incorporated at circuit level using Qiskit Aer simulator.
AI.result: Noise in classical input data significantly intensifies effects of quantum decoherence, resulting in less stable training and noticeably lower classification accuracy. Combined noise sources have synergistic negative effects beyond individual contributions.
AI.conclusion: Emphasizes importance of designing and evaluating QML pipelines with noise in mind, highlighting need to consider classical and quantum noise simultaneously when assessing QML performance in NISQ era.

title: Minimizing classical resources in variational measurement-based quantum computation for generative modeling
summary: Measurement-based quantum computation (MBQC) is a framework for quantum information processing in which a computational task is carried out through one-qubit measurements on a highly entangled resource state. Due to the indeterminacy of the outcomes of a quantum measurement, the random outcomes of these operations, if not corrected, yield a variational quantum channel family. Traditionally, this randomness is corrected through classical processing in order to ensure deterministic unitary computations. Recently, variational measurement-based quantum computation (VMBQC) has been introduced to exploit this measurement-induced randomness to gain an advantage in generative modeling. A limitation of this approach is that the corresponding channel model has twice as many parameters compared to the unitary model, scaling as $N \times D$, where $N$ is the number of logical qubits (width) and $D$ is the depth of the VMBQC model. This can often make optimization more difficult and may lead to poorly trainable models. In this paper, we present a restricted VMBQC model that extends the unitary setting to a channel-based one using only a single additional trainable parameter. We show, both numerically and algebraically, that this minimal extension is sufficient to generate probability distributions that cannot be learned by the corresponding unitary model.

AI.tldr: A restricted variational measurement-based quantum computation model is introduced that extends unitary models to channel-based ones using only one additional parameter, enabling generation of distributions unreachable by unitary models.
AI.motivation: Traditional MBQC corrects measurement randomness for deterministic unitary computations, but VMBQC exploits this randomness for generative modeling advantages. However, standard VMBQC has twice as many parameters as unitary models (N×D scaling), making optimization difficult and models poorly trainable.
AI.method: A restricted VMBQC model is presented that extends the unitary setting to a channel-based framework using only a single additional trainable parameter, maintaining computational advantages while dramatically reducing parameter complexity.
AI.result: The minimal single-parameter extension is shown to be sufficient to generate probability distributions that cannot be learned by the corresponding unitary model, demonstrated both numerically and algebraically.
AI.conclusion: The restricted VMBQC model provides a practical compromise between the computational power of channel-based models and the trainability of unitary models, enabling generation of more complex distributions with minimal parameter overhead.

title: Spectrum analysis with quantum dynamical systems. II. Finite-time analysis
summary: The prequel to this work [Ng et al., Phys. Rev. A 93, 042121 (2016)] proposes the method of spectral photon counting to enhance noise spectroscopy with an optical interferometer. While the predicted enhancement over homodyne detection is promising, the results there are derived by taking an asymptotic limit of infinite observation time; their validity for a finite time remains unclear. To validate the theory, here we perform a numerical study of a finite-time model. Assuming that the signal is an Ornstein--Uhlenbeck process with an unknown variance parameter, we evaluate the Fisher information for homodyne detection, a lower bound on the Fisher information for spectral photon counting, and a quantum upper bound, all without taking the infinite-time limit. To confirm that the Fisher-information quantities are satisfactory precision measures, we also compute the errors of the maximum-likelihood estimator by Monte-Carlo simulations. The results demonstrate that the Fisher-information quantities and the estimation errors all smoothly approach their asymptotic limits, and the advantage of spectral photon counting over homodyne detection can remain substantial for finite times.

AI.tldr: Numerical validation of spectral photon counting for noise spectroscopy shows finite-time advantage over homodyne detection using Fisher information analysis and Monte-Carlo simulations.
AI.motivation: Previous theoretical work proposed spectral photon counting to enhance noise spectroscopy but used asymptotic infinite-time limits, leaving finite-time validity unclear. This work aims to validate the theory for practical finite observation times.
AI.method: Numerical study of finite-time model assuming Ornstein-Uhlenbeck signal process with unknown variance parameter. Evaluated Fisher information for homodyne detection, lower bound for spectral photon counting, and quantum upper bound without infinite-time limit. Confirmed precision measures using Monte-Carlo simulations of maximum-likelihood estimator errors.
AI.result: Fisher information quantities and estimation errors smoothly approach asymptotic limits. Spectral photon counting maintains substantial advantage over homodyne detection for finite observation times, validating the practical utility of the method.
AI.conclusion: The theoretical advantage of spectral photon counting for noise spectroscopy is validated for finite-time scenarios, demonstrating its practical superiority over homodyne detection without requiring infinite observation times.

title: Quantum state transfer on a scalable network under unital and non-unital noise
summary: We investigate quantum state transfer on a class of bipartite graphs, namely the butterfly graphs, within the framework of discrete-time quantum walks. These graphs facilitate the construction of scalable quantum networks that enable communication between a sender and a receiver via perfect state transfer. Our analysis demonstrates that state transfer occurs across different butterfly graphs, thereby extending the known families of networks that support high-fidelity quantum state transfer. In addition to the ideal noiseless dynamics, we further investigate the robustness of quantum state transfer in the presence of non-Markovian environmental noise, specifically, random telegraph noise, modified Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise, which are examples of unital noise and non-Markovian amplitude damping noise, non-unital noise. These noise models capture different types of system-environment interactions and memory effects that influence the coherence of the quantum walk. These findings contribute to the theoretical understanding of how butterfly graph constructions influence quantum transport phenomena.

AI.tldr: Quantum state transfer via discrete-time quantum walks on butterfly graphs enables perfect communication between sender and receiver, with analysis showing robustness against various non-Markovian noise types.
AI.motivation: To investigate quantum state transfer on bipartite butterfly graphs as scalable quantum networks for communication, and to extend known families of networks supporting high-fidelity quantum state transfer while examining robustness against environmental noise.
AI.method: Analysis of discrete-time quantum walks on butterfly graphs, examining perfect state transfer between sender and receiver nodes, with additional investigation of robustness against non-Markovian environmental noise including random telegraph noise (unital), modified Ornstein-Uhlenbeck noise (unital), and non-Markovian amplitude damping noise (non-unital).
AI.result: State transfer occurs across different butterfly graphs, extending known families of networks supporting high-fidelity quantum state transfer. The analysis demonstrates robustness of quantum state transfer in the presence of various non-Markovian noise models that capture different system-environment interactions and memory effects.
AI.conclusion: Butterfly graphs provide scalable quantum network constructions that facilitate perfect state transfer and show resilience to different types of environmental noise, contributing to theoretical understanding of quantum transport phenomena in graph-based quantum networks.

title: Robust quantum metrology using disordered probes
summary: Disorder is ubiquitous in quantum devices including quantum probes designed and fabricated for quantum parameter estimation and sensing. We investigate the robustness of a quantum probe against the presence of glassy disorder. We define a disorder marker quantifying the effect of the disorder by expanding the quantum Fisher information in terms of different orders of the standardized central moments of the disorder-distributions. We classify the quantum probes in terms of the possible values of the disorder marker, and analytically show, for a disorder-sensitive probe with identical and weak disorder on all or a subset of the parameters of the probe-Hamiltonian, that the absolute value of the disorder marker exhibits a quadratic dependence on the disorder strength. We derive a robustness scale intrinsic to the probe that competes with the disorder, and provide a prescription for estimating the maximum disorder strength that the probe can withstand from the disorder-free probe-Hamiltonian for a given initial state of the probe, which can be computed without the disorder averaging. We demonstrate our results in the case of a single-qubit probe under disordered magnetic field, and a multi-qubit probe described by a disordered one-dimensional Kitaev model with nearest-neighbor interactions.

AI.tldr: The paper investigates how quantum probes for parameter estimation are affected by glassy disorder, developing a disorder marker to quantify disorder effects on quantum Fisher information and deriving robustness scales for quantum probes.
AI.motivation: Disorder is ubiquitous in quantum devices used for sensing and parameter estimation, but its effects on quantum probes need systematic characterization to understand probe robustness and performance limitations.
AI.method: Develop a disorder marker by expanding quantum Fisher information in terms of standardized central moments of disorder distributions. Classify probes based on disorder marker values, derive analytical relationships between disorder marker and disorder strength, and establish intrinsic robustness scales.
AI.result: For disorder-sensitive probes with weak identical disorder, the absolute disorder marker shows quadratic dependence on disorder strength. Derived intrinsic robustness scale that competes with disorder, and provided method to estimate maximum tolerable disorder strength from disorder-free Hamiltonian and initial state.
AI.conclusion: The framework enables characterization of quantum probe robustness against disorder, with applications demonstrated for single-qubit probes under disordered magnetic fields and multi-qubit probes described by disordered Kitaev models.

title: NV-ensemble enabled microwave/NV parametric amplifier with optimal driving
summary: We present a fast, memory-efficient, unitarity-preserving numerical method beyond the rotating-wave approximation for the closed Tavis-Cummings model in which a multilevel spin system interacts with a cavity mode. This model can describe the interaction of an ensemble of spins with a cavity mode in which the spin frequency and other parameters are time-dependent. The method exploits the fact that, while the Tavis-Cummings model is not tri-diagonal, it can be brought into tri-diagonal form by a change of basis that can be implemented purely by re-indexing (permuting basis elements), which is a fast operation. By truncating the Fock basis of the cavity mode, the computational complexity of the method is linear in the total dimension of the coupled system in both time and memory. The method can be employed to simulate any closed quantum system whose Hamiltonian terms can be brought into tri-diagonal form.

AI.tldr: A fast, memory-efficient numerical method for simulating the Tavis-Cummings model beyond rotating-wave approximation using basis permutation to achieve tridiagonal Hamiltonian structure.
AI.motivation: To develop an efficient numerical method for simulating the closed Tavis-Cummings model (multilevel spin system interacting with cavity mode) that preserves unitarity while going beyond the rotating-wave approximation, particularly for systems with time-dependent parameters.
AI.method: Exploits that the Tavis-Cummings Hamiltonian can be brought into tridiagonal form through basis permutation (re-indexing). Uses Fock basis truncation for the cavity mode, resulting in linear computational complexity in both time and memory relative to total system dimension.
AI.result: The method achieves fast, memory-efficient, unitarity-preserving simulations for the Tavis-Cummings model and can be extended to any closed quantum system with Hamiltonian terms that can be transformed into tridiagonal form.
AI.conclusion: The developed numerical method provides an efficient approach for simulating complex quantum systems like the Tavis-Cummings model beyond rotating-wave approximation, with broad applicability to systems that can be transformed into tridiagonal form.

title: A Comparative Study of Hybrid Quantum and Classical Genetic Algorithms in Portfolio Optimization
summary: This work investigates the performance of a Hybrid Quantum Genetic Algorithm (HQGA) compared to a classical Genetic Algorithm (GA) for solving the portfolio optimization problem. Our results indicate that the HQGA converges faster to the optimal solution than its classical counterpart, while also maintaining a higher level of population diversity throughout the optimization process. In addition, the HQGA requires significantly fewer evaluations-to-solution than a brute-force approach to reach the global optimum.

AI.tldr: Hybrid Quantum Genetic Algorithm outperforms classical GA in portfolio optimization with faster convergence, better diversity, and fewer evaluations needed.
AI.motivation: To investigate whether a Hybrid Quantum Genetic Algorithm (HQGA) can provide performance advantages over classical Genetic Algorithms for solving the computationally challenging portfolio optimization problem.
AI.method: Comparative analysis between a Hybrid Quantum Genetic Algorithm and a classical Genetic Algorithm applied to portfolio optimization, with evaluation of convergence speed, population diversity, and computational efficiency.
AI.result: HQGA converges faster to optimal solutions than classical GA, maintains higher population diversity throughout optimization, and requires significantly fewer evaluations-to-solution compared to brute-force approaches.
AI.conclusion: The Hybrid Quantum Genetic Algorithm demonstrates superior performance characteristics for portfolio optimization, offering faster convergence, better diversity preservation, and improved computational efficiency over classical approaches.

title: Frustration-Induced Expressibility Limitations in Variational Quantum Algorithms
summary: Geometric frustration, arising from competing interactions that prevent simultaneous energy minimization, presents a fundamental challenge for variational quantum algorithms applied to quantum many-body systems. We investigate the transverse-field Ising model on a square lattice with frustrated diagonal coupling and show that geometric frustration leads to strongly inhomogeneous correlations that are difficult to capture using standard Hamiltonian-inspired ansätze with global parameters. As a result, the required circuit depth increases significantly in the intermediate-field regime. We demonstrate that this limitation is not caused by optimization difficulties such as barren plateaus, but instead arises from insufficient expressibility of the ansatz. By introducing bond-resolved variational parameters, we recover accurate results at reduced circuit depth. We also study low-energy excitations and find that near-degenerate spectra in the frustrated regime further challenge variational methods. Our results provide a clear physical explanation for the limitations of variational quantum algorithms in frustrated systems and suggest improved ansatz design strategies for quantum simulation.

AI.tldr: Geometric frustration in quantum systems creates inhomogeneous correlations that standard variational quantum algorithms with global parameters cannot capture, requiring deeper circuits; bond-resolved parameters solve this limitation.
AI.motivation: Geometric frustration in quantum many-body systems presents fundamental challenges for variational quantum algorithms, as competing interactions prevent simultaneous energy minimization and create complex correlation patterns that are difficult to represent with standard approaches.
AI.method: Study the transverse-field Ising model on a square lattice with frustrated diagonal coupling; analyze limitations of Hamiltonian-inspired ansätze with global parameters; introduce bond-resolved variational parameters; examine low-energy excitations and near-degenerate spectra in frustrated regimes.
AI.result: Geometric frustration leads to strongly inhomogeneous correlations that standard ansätze with global parameters cannot capture, requiring significantly increased circuit depth; this limitation stems from insufficient expressibility rather than optimization difficulties; bond-resolved parameters recover accurate results at reduced depth; near-degenerate spectra in frustrated regimes further challenge variational methods.
AI.conclusion: The limitations of variational quantum algorithms in frustrated systems arise from insufficient ansatz expressibility for capturing inhomogeneous correlations, not optimization problems; improved ansatz design with bond-resolved parameters provides a solution and offers guidance for quantum simulation of geometrically frustrated systems.

title: First-principles study of dispersive readout in circuit QED
summary: The speed and fidelity of dispersive readout of superconducting qubits should improve by increasing the amplitude of the measurement drive. Experiments show, however, that beyond some drive amplitude there is always a saturation or drop in fidelity, often associated with a decrease in qubit energy relaxation time $T_1$. A simple Lindblad master equation does not capture the latter effect. More involved approaches based on effective master equations rely on strong assumptions about the spectra of the system and the bath and only partially agree with observations. Here, we perform a first-principles simulation of the full unitary dynamics of dispersive readout by considering the circuit QED Hamiltonian coupled to a microscopic model for the measurement transmission line, allowing for its arbitrary spectrum, including filters. Our access to the dynamics of the bath degrees of freedom allows us to investigate the emission spectrum of the system as a function of drive power. We show how the dependence of qubit $T_1$ on readout drive amplitude is sensitive to the details of the bath spectrum. In particular, we find that $T_1$ drops with increasing drive amplitude when a Purcell notch filter is placed at the qubit frequency, and that the Lindblad master equation shows general qualitative defects compared to the first-principles model.

AI.tldr: First-principles simulation of dispersive readout in superconducting qubits reveals that qubit T₁ degradation with increasing drive amplitude depends on bath spectrum details, particularly Purcell filters, showing limitations of Lindblad master equation approaches.
AI.motivation: Experimental observations show that increasing measurement drive amplitude in dispersive readout of superconducting qubits leads to saturation or fidelity drop, often associated with decreased T₁, which simple Lindblad master equations fail to capture and more complex approaches only partially explain.
AI.method: First-principles simulation of full unitary dynamics using circuit QED Hamiltonian coupled to microscopic model of measurement transmission line, allowing arbitrary bath spectra including filters, with access to bath dynamics and emission spectrum analysis.
AI.result: Qubit T₁ dependence on readout drive amplitude is sensitive to bath spectrum details; T₁ drops with increasing drive when Purcell notch filter is at qubit frequency; Lindblad master equation shows qualitative defects compared to first-principles model.
AI.conclusion: First-principles modeling reveals critical dependence of qubit coherence on bath spectrum details during dispersive readout, highlighting limitations of simplified master equation approaches and importance of microscopic bath modeling for accurate performance prediction.

title: Sub-nanosecond control for spin-defect quantum memories with a low-cost, compact FPGA platform
summary: Dynamical decoupling techniques are widely used to characterize and control the environments of solid-state quantum defects, enabling solid-state quantum memories and nanoscale quantum sensors. However, resolution is often limited by the timing granularity of control hardware, which can undersample narrow spectral features and distort extracted parameters. Here, we demonstrate sub-nanosecond timing control on an inexpensive FPGA-based platform by extending the open-source QICK (Quantum Instrumentation Control Kit) framework using a waveform-offset method. This approach achieves an effective timing resolution of 200~ps on an RF system-on-chip device without modification to the underlying hardware. We apply this capability to dynamical decoupling spectroscopy of nitrogen-vacancy centers in diamond, enabling precise extraction of hyperfine couplings of individual $^{13}\mathrm{C}$ nuclear spins and resolving spectral features that are otherwise undersampled. These results demonstrate that high-resolution, device-level characterization of spin-based quantum memories can be achieved using flexible, inexpensive control hardware, providing a scalable alternative to commercial arbitrary waveform generators.

AI.tldr: FPGA-based quantum control platform achieves 200ps timing resolution via waveform-offset method, enabling precise dynamical decoupling spectroscopy of NV centers for hyperfine coupling extraction.
AI.motivation: Existing dynamical decoupling techniques for solid-state quantum defects are limited by control hardware timing granularity, which undersamples narrow spectral features and distorts extracted parameters.
AI.method: Extended the open-source QICK framework using a waveform-offset method on an FPGA-based platform to achieve 200ps effective timing resolution without hardware modifications.
AI.result: Demonstrated sub-nanosecond timing control enabling precise extraction of hyperfine couplings of individual 13C nuclear spins in NV centers and resolution of otherwise undersampled spectral features.
AI.conclusion: High-resolution device-level characterization of spin-based quantum memories can be achieved using flexible, inexpensive FPGA control hardware as a scalable alternative to commercial arbitrary waveform generators.

title: Hybrid Quantum-Classical Optimization Workflows for the Shipment Selection Problem
summary: We present a quantum optimization framework for the Shipment Selection Problem (SSP) in electric freight logistics, developed jointly by IonQ and Einride. Idle gaps arising from stochastic shipment cancellations reduce fleet utilization and revenue; filling them optimally requires solving a combinatorial assignment problem with quadratic inter-gap dependencies. We formulate the SSP as a Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program, map it to an Ising cost Hamiltonian, and solve it using Iterative-QAOA, a non-variational warm-start extension of the Quantum Approximate Optimization Algorithm (QAOA) with a fixed linear-ramp parameter schedule. An end-to-end hybrid workflow integrates Einride's vehicle routing problem (VRP) solver with IonQ's quantum simulations, enabling evaluation on real, anonymized logistics data spanning up to 130 qubits. We assess solution quality through application-level performance metrics, including Shipments Delivered (SD), Schedule Compatibility Score (SCS), and Total Drive Distance (TDD). When the quantum assignment is passed to the classical solver as a warm start, the resulting hybrid workflow achieves improvements of up to 12\% in SD and a reduction of up to 6\% in total drive distance per shipment for specific instances, while total operational cost remains effectively unchanged. These results show that Iterative-QAOA can generate compatibility-aware assignments that become operationally valuable when embedded in a hybrid logistics optimization workflow.

AI.tldr: Quantum optimization framework using Iterative-QAOA solves Shipment Selection Problem in electric freight logistics, achieving up to 12% improvement in shipments delivered and 6% reduction in drive distance when used as warm start for classical solver.
AI.motivation: Idle gaps from stochastic shipment cancellations reduce fleet utilization and revenue in electric freight logistics. Filling these gaps optimally requires solving a combinatorial assignment problem with quadratic inter-gap dependencies, which is challenging for classical methods.
AI.method: Formulate SSP as Mixed-Integer Quadratic Program, map to Ising cost Hamiltonian, solve using Iterative-QAOA (non-variational warm-start extension of QAOA with fixed linear-ramp parameter schedule). End-to-end hybrid workflow integrates Einride's VRP solver with IonQ's quantum simulations.
AI.result: Evaluation on real anonymized logistics data up to 130 qubits shows quantum assignment as warm start achieves up to 12% improvement in Shipments Delivered (SD) and up to 6% reduction in Total Drive Distance (TDD) per shipment for specific instances, with total operational cost effectively unchanged.
AI.conclusion: Iterative-QAOA can generate compatibility-aware assignments that become operationally valuable when embedded in a hybrid logistics optimization workflow, demonstrating practical quantum advantage in real-world freight logistics.

title: Measuring what matters: A scalable framework for application-level quantum benchmarking
summary: As quantum computing systems continue to mature, there is an increasing need for benchmarking methodologies that capture performance in terms of meaningful, application-level metrics. In this work, we present a scalable framework for application-level quantum benchmarking that is designed to support internal system evaluation and cross-platform comparison across technology providers. Our framework is guided by a set of core principles, including measurability, simplicity, scalability, and extensibility. We present 13 benchmark families that reflect realistic workloads across multiple domains. This enables the systematic evaluation of the quality of solutions, the total execution time, total used energy, as well as Time-to-Solution. The benchmarks are designed to be reproducible, interpretable across stakeholder groups, and adaptable to evolving system capabilities. The framework aims to bridge the gap between low-level performance metrics and real-world value, providing a unified approach to assessing quantum systems. The resulting benchmarks support development and validation and contribute to the foundation of industry-wide benchmarking standards.

AI.tldr: A scalable framework for application-level quantum benchmarking designed for cross-platform comparison, featuring 13 benchmark families across multiple domains to evaluate solution quality, execution time, energy consumption, and Time-to-Solution.
AI.motivation: As quantum computing systems mature, there is a need for benchmarking methodologies that capture performance using meaningful, application-level metrics rather than just low-level technical measurements. The framework aims to bridge the gap between low-level performance metrics and real-world value, supporting both internal system evaluation and cross-platform comparison across different technology providers.
AI.method: The framework is guided by core principles of measurability, simplicity, scalability, and extensibility. It presents 13 benchmark families that reflect realistic workloads across multiple domains. These benchmarks are designed to be reproducible, interpretable across stakeholder groups, and adaptable to evolving system capabilities. The framework systematically evaluates solution quality, total execution time, total energy consumption, and Time-to-Solution metrics.
AI.result: The framework provides a unified approach to assessing quantum systems that supports development, validation, and contributes to the foundation of industry-wide benchmarking standards. It enables meaningful comparison across different quantum computing platforms and technology providers through standardized application-level benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: The scalable application-level quantum benchmarking framework successfully addresses the need for meaningful performance evaluation in quantum computing, bridging the gap between low-level metrics and real-world value while supporting the development of industry-wide standards for quantum system assessment.

title: Many-Body Super- and Subradiance in Ordered Atomic Arrays
summary: When quantum emitters couple indistinguishably to light, they can synchronize into a collective light matter system with radiative properties profoundly different from those of independent particles. To date, the resulting collective effects have largely been confined to point like or homogeneous ensembles. Here, we open access to a qualitatively new collective regime by realizing geometrically ordered, spatially extended atom arrays with subwavelength spacing. This establishes a fundamentally new platform in which collective emission is no longer confined to a single Dicke mode but instead emerges from an ordered network of photon mediated interactions. We find that 2D atom arrays undergo strong super and subradiant emission. Despite subwavelength spacing, we achieve site resolved imaging and directly observe the buildup of spatial correlations, demonstrating the transformation of cooperative decay into a strongly correlated many-body process. We observe extensive scaling of superradiance, uncover superradiant revivals, and reveal the ferromagnetic nature of superradiance and the antiferromagnetic nature of subradiance. Our results realize a novel programmable platform for exploring and utilizing dissipative many-body quantum physics, opening new possibilities for photon capture, storage, and atom photon entanglement.

AI.tldr: 2D atom arrays with subwavelength spacing enable novel collective quantum effects beyond single Dicke mode, revealing super/subradiant emission, spatial correlations, and magnetic-like ordering in dissipative many-body systems.
AI.motivation: To extend collective quantum effects beyond point-like or homogeneous ensembles by creating geometrically ordered, spatially extended atom arrays with subwavelength spacing, enabling exploration of new collective regimes where emission emerges from ordered networks of photon-mediated interactions rather than single Dicke modes.
AI.method: Realization of 2D atom arrays with subwavelength spacing, enabling site-resolved imaging and direct observation of spatial correlations, transforming cooperative decay into a strongly correlated many-body process.
AI.result: Observation of strong superradiant and subradiant emission, extensive scaling of superradiance, superradiant revivals, and demonstration that superradiance exhibits ferromagnetic character while subradiance shows antiferromagnetic nature.
AI.conclusion: The work establishes a novel programmable platform for exploring dissipative many-body quantum physics, opening new possibilities for photon capture, storage, and atom-photon entanglement through geometrically ordered atom arrays.
